name: Home
title: Home
description: Entry page that gives families an overview of current school information and direct access to key tools such as activities, lunch, transportation, calendar, and reference pages.

core_features: Load and display top-level overview content (header text, announcements, highlights, and quick tool metadata) from getHomePageOverview.
Provide navigation into key areas: Activities, Lunch Menu, Transportation, Calendar, About/Staff Directory, Homework Policies, Supply Lists, and Summer Camps.
Show a list of announcements that can be scanned quickly and optionally deep-linked via URL to a specific announcement.
Show a list of highlights that surfaces notable items or tools from the school overview data.
Expose a quick tools panel driven entirely by quick_tools from getHomePageOverview, mapping each tool to the appropriate navigation behavior.
Offer a transportation quick link that sends the user to the bus routes page pre-filtered by a street they provide.
Offer a calendar link that sends the user to the calendar page with month and year parameters determined by URL state or the current date.
Offer grade-aware entry points from the home page into Homework Policies and Supply Lists pages using a gradeLevel URL parameter.
Provide a dedicated entry point to after-school programs (Activities) and to the staff directory (About teaser).
Provide a July summer camp promo link that routes to the summer camps page with a fixed month parameter as required.
user_workflows: View overview and announcements: 1) User opens Home (index.html). 2) homepage-data-loader calls getHomePageOverview. 3) overview-header-text displays header text. 4) announcements-list renders announcements. 5) If the URL includes focusAnnouncementId, that announcement is auto-expanded in the list.

Open the lunch menu from quick tools: 1) User opens Home. 2) quick-tools-panel renders tools from getHomePageOverview.quick_tools. 3) User clicks the tool with tool_key mapped to lunch_menu. 4) Browser navigates to lunch_menu.html with no additional parameters.

Find a bus route by street: 1) User opens Home. 2) transportation-quick-link-form reads any existing streetQuery from the URL and pre-fills its input. 3) User types a street name (or keeps the pre-filled value). 4) User submits the form. 5) Component navigates to bus_routes.html?query={encodedStreetName}.

Open the calendar to a featured month: 1) User opens Home. 2) calendar-teaser reads month and year from URL if present; otherwise it derives them from the current date. 3) User clicks the 'View full calendar' link. 4) Browser navigates to calendar.html?month={featuredMonthNumber}&year={featuredYearNumber}.

Go to Activities (after-school programs): 1) User opens Home. 2) activities-entry-tile is rendered as part of the main navigation or a prominent tile. 3) User clicks the Activities tile. 4) Browser navigates to after_school_programs.html.

Use grade-specific Homework Policies from Home: 1) User opens Home. 2) grade-context-selector reads any gradeLevel from URL; if none, it shows an unselected state. 3) User selects a grade in grade-context-selector, which updates the page URL to include gradeLevel={selectedGrade}. 4) homework-policies-teaser reacts to the URL change and enables its link. 5) User clicks the Homework Policies callout. 6) Browser navigates to homework_policies.html?gradeLevel={selectedGrade}.

Use grade-specific Supply Lists from Home: 1) User opens Home. 2) grade-context-selector manages the gradeLevel URL parameter as above. 3) supply-list-teaser reads the same gradeLevel and enables its call-to-action. 4) User clicks the Supply List callout. 5) Browser navigates to supply_lists.html?gradeLevel={selectedGrade}.
Access staff directory via About teaser: 1) User opens Home. 2) about-staff-teaser renders an entry for the staff directory. 3) User clicks this teaser. 4) Browser navigates to staff_directory.html.
Open July summer camps from promo: 1) User opens Home. 2) summer-camp-promo renders a simple promo link. 3) User clicks the promo. 4) Browser navigates to summer_camps.html?month=july.

Open a specific announcement from an external link: 1) User follows a link to index.html?focusAnnouncementId={announcementId}. 2) homepage-data-loader loads overview data. 3) announcements-list compares focusAnnouncementId against the loaded announcements and automatically expands or scrolls to the matching item, giving it focus for accessibility.
interactions: On page load (index.html), homepage-data-loader invokes getHomePageOverview and populates shared page state with hero_title, hero_subtitle, announcements, highlights, and quick_tools.
Clicking an item in the main navigation labelled Home reloads or remains on index.html without changing URL parameters (unless other controls modify them).
Clicking the Activities main navigation item or activities-entry-tile triggers navigation to after_school_programs.html.
Clicking a quick tools item whose tool_key maps to the lunch menu triggers navigation to lunch_menu.html.
Submitting transportation-quick-link-form sends the user to bus_routes.html with a query parameter query set to the entered street value.

Clicking the quick tools item (or dedicated control) associated with transportation may pre-fill transportation-quick-link-form with a street name from the current URL’s streetQuery parameter before navigating on submit.
Clicking the calendar teaser control labeled as the full calendar entry navigates to calendar.html with month and year URL parameters determined by calendar-teaser.
Changing the grade selection in grade-context-selector updates the gradeLevel parameter in the current page URL via history APIs and triggers dependent components to re-evaluate their state.
Clicking the Homework Policies callout in homework-policies-teaser navigates to homework_policies.html with gradeLevel taken from the current page URL.
Clicking the Supply List callout in supply-list-teaser navigates to supply_lists.html with gradeLevel taken from the current page URL.
Clicking the Staff Directory link in about-staff-teaser navigates to staff_directory.html.
Clicking the July summer camps promo in summer-camp-promo navigates to summer_camps.html with month=july.
Clicking an announcement item in announcements-list toggles its expanded state for that session and may update the focusAnnouncementId parameter in the URL.
Clicking a highlight item in highlights-list can optionally update the tool parameter in the URL to focus related quick tools or sections on the page.
page_functionality.state_logic: The Home page uses URL parameters to control which items are focused, pre-filled, or targeted when navigating away. gradeLevel: When present in index.html?gradeLevel={value}, this value represents a selected grade and is used by grade-context-selector, homework-policies-teaser, and supply-list-teaser. Changing the grade selection updates this parameter, and dependent components re-render accordingly. streetQuery: When present in index.html?streetQuery={value}, transportation-quick-link-form pre-fills its street input with this value. Submitting the form navigates to bus_routes.html?query={value}. month and year: When present in index.html?month={m}&year={y}, calendar-teaser uses them as the featuredMonthNumber and featuredYearNumber when constructing the link to calendar.html. If they are not present, calendar-teaser derives month and year from the current date at runtime. focusAnnouncementId: When present in index.html?focusAnnouncementId={id}, announcements-list checks loaded announcements for a matching id, auto-expands that announcement, and optionally scrolls it into view. tool: An optional tool parameter (index.html?tool={tool_key}) can be used by quick-tools-panel to auto-highlight a specific tool tile and by highlights-list to adjust focus. All state is maintained via these URL parameters only; no localStorage or other client-side persistence is used.

components.functionality: Fetches high-level home page overview data using getHomePageOverview on initial load and exposes the result (hero_title, hero_subtitle, announcements, highlights, quick_tools) to other components via shared page state.
Provides top-level navigation links including Home, Activities, Transportation, Calendar, About, and other sections as defined globally. Marks Home as the current active page state.

Displays the primary header text and supporting line for the Home page using hero_title and hero_subtitle. If either is missing from the API response, it falls back to non-marketing generic placeholders that indicate the absence of content.

Renders an ordered or grouped list of announcements retrieved from getHomePageOverview. Each announcement can be expanded or collapsed to show or hide its summary. A focusAnnouncementId URL parameter controls which announcement is auto-expanded on load.

Displays a set of highlight items retrieved from getHomePageOverview. Each highlight item can be clicked to optionally adjust page focus (for example, by setting a tool URL parameter to align with a related quick tool) without requiring additional API calls.

Renders buttons or tiles for each item in quick_tools. Each tool is mapped by its tool_key to a specific navigation action: for example, a lunch_menu tool_key navigates to lunch_menu.html, a bus_routes tool_key focuses transportation-quick-link-form or navigates to bus_routes.html?query={streetQuery}, and a calendar tool_key navigates to calendar.html?month={featuredMonthNumber}&year={featuredYearNumber}. Unrecognized tool_keys can be handled with a default behavior (such as linking to a generic page or section).

Provides a simple input where the user can enter a street name for transportation lookup. The field is pre-populated from the streetQuery URL parameter if present. When submitted, it navigates to bus_routes.html?query={encodedStreetName}, satisfying the preFilledStreetName requirement without hardcoded street values.

Constructs a single 'View full calendar' navigation link. It determines featuredMonthNumber and featuredYearNumber using URL parameters month and year when provided; otherwise it derives them from the current date at runtime. The link navigates to calendar.html?month={featuredMonthNumber}&year={featuredYearNumber}.

Provides the Activities tile or call-to-action that directly navigates to after_school_programs.html when clicked, offering a clear path into the After School Programs section independent of API data.

Allows the user to select a grade level that will apply across the Home page for grade-specific links. When the user selects a grade, it updates the gradeLevel parameter in the page URL (index.html?gradeLevel={selectedGrade}). Other components read this value to create targeted links to Homework Policies and Supply Lists.

Displays a homework policies callout that becomes fully active when a gradeLevel is available in the URL. On click, it navigates to homework_policies.html?gradeLevel={currentGradeLevel}, using the gradeLevel parameter managed by grade-context-selector. If no gradeLevel is set, the component can either disable the link or prompt the user to choose a grade.

Displays a supply list callout that similarly depends on the gradeLevel URL parameter. On click, it navigates to supply_lists.html?gradeLevel={currentGradeLevel}. If gradeLevel is missing, the component prompts the user to select a grade via grade-context-selector.

Provides an About section teaser that links directly to the staff directory. When the user clicks the 'Staff Directory' link within this teaser, the browser navigates to staff_directory.html, giving a direct path into staff information.

Displays a simple promo entry that navigates the user to the summer camps page filtered for July sessions. Clicking this component sends the user to summer_camps.html?month=july, using the fixed month parameter required by the navigation specification.

layout.grid_system: Centered fixed-width content column likely based on a 12-column grid (~1080–1200px wide) within full-bleed horizontal bands.
layout.layout_pattern: Use a classic, linear, top-to-bottom page with stacked full-width horizontal bands: a dense, multi-bar header/navigation region at the top; a centered main content column below. Within the main column, content is organized in a single primary column, with occasional two-column rows for related items (e.g., announcements vs. highlights; paired teasers) that still feel part of the same main column rather than a persistent sidebar. Secondary items appear in right-aligned or right-hand columns within those rows, reflecting the mockup’s pattern of occasional inline right-side content.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use a classic, linear, top-to-bottom page with stacked full-width horizontal bands: a dense, multi-bar header/navigation region at the top; a centered main content column below. Within the main column, content is organized in a single primary column, with occasional two-column rows for related items (e.g., announcements vs. highlights; paired teasers) that still feel part of the same main column rather than a persistent sidebar. Secondary items appear in right-aligned or right-hand columns within those rows, reflecting the mockup’s pattern of occasional inline right-side content.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Roughly the top 40% of the initial viewport is given to header-navigation (including utility bar, main nav, banner, and tabs). The remaining 60% above the fold begins the main content: intro, announcements, and key tools. The main content column is a centered fixed-width container (~1080–1200px) inside full-bleed horizontal bands. Sections (overview, announcements/highlights, quick tools, reference teasers) are separated vertically by 24–32px, while internal spacing (between headings, lists, and cards) uses 8–16px increments.
layout_strategies.content_density: Maintain moderate density: the header bars are relatively compact but readable; the main content uses comfortable line heights and margins without feeling airy. Lists and tools are tight enough that several key items are visible above the fold, but each block has enough padding (8–16px) to clearly separate items. Multi-column rows (like announcements/highlights) are compact, avoiding large patches of whitespace, consistent with the practical, information-focused feel.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Preserve an F-pattern: the eye begins at the top-left of the header-navigation (school logo, utility elements), scans right to the board logo/search, then down to the main blue nav bar, then to the centered banner title. From there, attention drops into the main column at the left: breadcrumb + H1, then down through announcements (left) and highlights (right), then into quick tools and key links. Right-hand elements (highlights, some teasers) are visually secondary but aligned so they can be picked up by a secondary rightward scan, mirroring the mockup’s behavior of in-row right-aligned secondary content.

layout.overall_description: The page opens with a strong, institutional header region made of stacked horizontal bars spanning the full browser width, visually anchored by a deep blue navigation band and a muted grey page banner. Within these bars, a centered content column (~1120px wide) holds the school logo and utility links at the top, the main navigation menus, and a banner labeling the site and page. Below this top-heavy frame, the layout transitions into a single centered main column that carries all home-page content. At the top of this column, a breadcrumb and large, confident H1 introduce the school, optionally paired with a small right-aligned school image and a grade selector. Immediately below, the column splits briefly into two: announcements on the left and highlights on the right, forming a compact information band that still sits within the same content column. Further down, a full-width quick tools block provides clear, button-like entry points to daily tasks, followed by a bus/transportation quick-link form and a paired row of calendar and activities tiles. The page closes with a reference-and-resources band where homework policies, supply lists, staff information, and a seasonal summer camp promo appear in a two-column grid, left and right, maintaining the F-pattern scan while keeping everything in a structured, predictable vertical flow. The overall effect is orderly and institutional, with generous but not luxurious spacing and obvious visual hierarchy that supports quick orientation for busy families.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component operates behind the scenes and does not occupy a permanent layout position. When an initial loading state is needed, it appears as a subtle, full-width notice bar directly beneath the lowest header-navigation bar, inside the centered content column. The bar is approximately 40px tall, spans 100% of the main content width (~1080–1200px), and uses light background and small left-aligned text to indicate loading. Once data is loaded, the bar collapses completely and the main content sections shift up to occupy the space, preserving the linear vertical flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Normally invisible and not part of the layout; when active, it is a static horizontal band in normal document flow, pushing content down rather than overlaying it.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Positioned at the very top of the page, this component spans the full browser width as a series of stacked horizontal bands. Within each band, content is centered in a fixed-width container (~1120px) aligned to the page center. The topmost band is a slim utility bar (~32px tall) with the school logo anchored on the far left and secondary items (district logo, search, possibly language or login links) aligned to the far right; spacing between items follows the 8–16px scale. Below this, a deeper primary navigation bar (~56px tall) in the school’s strong blue brand color contains left-aligned main navigation items distributed horizontally across roughly 70% of the width, with each nav item padded 12–16px horizontally and 8–12px vertically. Beneath the blue nav, a muted grey or gradient banner (~80–96px tall) spans the same width; it centers the site or school name and the current section (e.g., “Home”) in large, bold type. Directly under the banner, a secondary tab strip (~40–48px tall) runs full width within the content column, showing key site areas as tabs; the active tab for the home page is visually highlighted with the blue brand color and sits roughly centered. The entire header-navigation block occupies approximately the top 40% of the initial viewport on desktop before scrolling reveals more of the main content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static at the top of the document, full-bleed horizontally with a centered fixed-width inner container; scrolls away with the page rather than sticking.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component marks the beginning of the main content area and sits directly beneath the header-navigation region, separated by about 24–32px of vertical space. It is contained within the same centered main column (~1080–1200px wide) and follows a left-to-right, then top-to-bottom arrangement that echoes the mockup’s content pages. At the very top of this block, a breadcrumb trail is left-aligned and spans roughly 70% of the column width, with modest vertical padding (8px) above and below. Beneath the breadcrumb, a large, prominent H1 heading occupies the left ~70% of the main column, with a comfortable 8–16px gap below it before any descriptive text. The remaining right ~30% of this horizontal row can hold a small, right-aligned school image or crest, aligned vertically with the H1 so that the text and image share a single visual row, similar to the mockup’s right-aligned school logo. A short introductory paragraph (or two very brief lines of copy) sits below the H1 on the left, using about 70–80% of the main column width, with line lengths kept moderate for readability. The vertical spacing from the bottom of this intro text to the next major block (announcements/highlights) is about 24px, maintaining clear separation while keeping the content compact.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block-level section within the main content column, flowing normally with the page scroll; no fixed or floating behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The announcements list begins immediately below the overview-header-text, sharing a horizontal row with the highlights-list to create a compact information band. On desktop, this row is split into two columns inside the centered main content column: announcements occupy the left column, roughly 60% of the content width, while highlights sit to the right at about 40%. The top edge of the announcements-list aligns vertically with the top edge of the highlights-list, establishing a clear two-column structure that still reads as a single section. The announcements-list itself starts with a left-aligned “Announcements” subheading, followed by a list of recent items stacked vertically with 8–12px between items. Each announcement line spans the full width of the left column and may show a short title and date, but is kept concise to preserve moderate density; 3–5 items are typically visible above the fold. The left column has 16px of internal padding on both sides relative to the column boundaries, and there is a 24px horizontal gap between the left and right columns to prevent crowding. The bottom of this announcements column aligns roughly with, or slightly exceeds, the bottom of the highlights column; below both columns, the next section (quick-tools-panel) begins after a 24–32px vertical separation.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block within a two-column row; participates in responsive behavior where, on smaller screens, the announcements-list stacks above the highlights-list.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The highlights-list sits directly to the right of the announcements-list within the same horizontal row, occupying about 40% of the main content column width. It aligns with the top of the announcements section and adheres to the same vertical spacing rhythm. A “Highlights” or “What’s New” subheading is left-aligned within this right column, followed by a short list of key callouts—such as featured events or reminders—each item separated by approximately 8–12px. Item text is kept brief so that the right column feels lighter and more scannable than the announcements side, reinforcing its secondary nature but maintaining visibility within the F-pattern’s rightward sweep. The right column has 16px padding on its left and right edges, and the gap between this column and the left announcements column is about 24px. The overall height of the highlights-list is similar to or slightly shorter than the announcements column so that the two blocks read as a unified band. Below the lowest highlight item, a 24–32px vertical gap is maintained before the next major section (quick-tools-panel).
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block within the same two-column row as announcements; on smaller screens, it stacks beneath announcements in normal document flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The quick-tools-panel forms its own horizontal section directly below the announcements/highlights band, separated from it by approximately 24–32px of vertical whitespace. It spans the full width of the main content column and is visually grouped as a flat, light-toned panel with a subtle border or background to distinguish it from surrounding text. At the top-left of this panel is a brief label such as “Quick Tools” or “Family Tools,” with the interactive tool buttons arranged below in a simple grid. Each tool (e.g., Lunch, Transportation, Attendance, Payments) is represented as a rectangular button or tile with 12–16px horizontal padding and 8–12px vertical padding, arranged in one or two rows of 3–4 items across depending on available width. The grid uses 16px horizontal and vertical gaps between buttons. On desktop, the tools roughly occupy the central 80–90% of the column width, leaving modest side margins to keep the layout from feeling cramped. The panel height is kept compact so that all primary tools are visible at a glance without needing to scroll within the panel; the bottom of the panel sits about 24px above the transportation-quick-link-form.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level section within the main content, expanding vertically as tools are added but not scrolling independently; maintains a simple responsive grid that collapses to fewer columns on smaller screens.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component sits directly beneath the quick-tools-panel, separated by about 16–24px of vertical space to indicate it is related but distinct. It is left-aligned within the main content column and spans roughly 60–70% of the column width, matching the left-hand alignment of the announcements section to reinforce the primary reading path. The form consists of a brief left-aligned label (e.g., “Check your bus route”) positioned at the top, with a single-line input field and a button arranged on a single horizontal line just below the label. The input occupies about 70% of this component’s width, with the action button to the right taking the remaining 30%, separated by an 8–12px gap. The vertical padding inside this block is modest (8–12px above and below the controls), keeping the element compact and consistent with the site’s conservative, functional style. To the right of this form, the remaining 30–40% of the main content width is left mostly open or used only for brief helper text, maintaining the sense that this is a linear tool rather than a full-width hero element. A 24px vertical gap separates the bottom of this form from the next row of tiles (calendar and activities).
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block that flows with the page; no sticking or overlay behavior. On smaller screens, the field and button may stack vertically but maintain their linear order.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The calendar-teaser is part of a paired row of navigational tiles immediately below the transportation-quick-link-form. This row divides the main content column into two equal halves, each about 48% of the width with a 24px gutter between them. The calendar-teaser occupies the left half, aligned directly beneath the left edge of the transportation form, reinforcing its importance in the primary scan path. It appears as a flat card or tile with a subtle border, approximately 120–160px tall, containing a concise heading (e.g., “School Calendar”), a one-line description, and possibly a small calendar icon aligned to the left or right. Internal padding is about 16px on all sides, and vertical spacing between its text elements is 8–12px. The card’s visual treatment is slightly stronger than standard text but more subdued than the quick-tools-panel, ensuring it reads as a secondary navigation target. The bottom edge of the calendar-teaser aligns with or is slightly longer than the activities-entry-tile to the right.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard card within a two-column row; on smaller screens it stacks above the activities-entry-tile, maintaining a full-width layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This tile sits in the same horizontal row as the calendar-teaser, occupying the right half (approximately 48% of the main content width) with a 24px horizontal gap between the two cards. It aligns at the top and bottom with the calendar-teaser for a balanced appearance. The activities-entry-tile uses a similar card treatment—flat, lightly bordered or shaded—but may include slightly more visual emphasis (e.g., a bolder icon or accent color) to suggest interactive enrichment. Within the tile, a short heading such as “Clubs & Activities” is placed near the top-left, followed by a concise description or action line (e.g., “View after-school programs”) with 8–12px spacing. Padding is consistent with the calendar tile at about 16px on all sides, keeping the visual system coherent. Together, these two tiles form a compact navigation band for time-based and extracurricular information. A 24–32px gap below this row separates it from the lower reference and promotional content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard card within a two-column row; on smaller screens it stacks beneath the calendar-teaser, expanding to full width.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The grade-context-selector is positioned near the top of the main content area to allow families to quickly filter the relevance of information. It is visually associated with the overview-header-text and sits either on the same horizontal line as the H1 or just below it, aligned to the right edge of the main content column. On desktop, it occupies roughly 20–25% of the column width and appears as a compact dropdown or segmented control with a clear label (e.g., “Viewing: All Grades”), taking advantage of the right-side space opposite the H1 and introductory text—similar in spirit to the right-aligned school logo seen in the mockup. Vertical alignment is such that the top of the control is approximately aligned with the midpoint of the H1, ensuring both elements are easily visible in the same glance. The control uses 8–12px internal vertical padding and 12–16px horizontal padding, adhering to the base spacing system. A small 8–12px vertical gap separates it from surrounding text blocks, keeping the overall header region moderately dense but not cramped. On narrower screens, it stacks below the H1 and intro, remaining directly beneath the primary heading.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline block within the overview section; flows with the content and repositions responsively (shifting from right-aligned beside the H1 to full-width beneath the H1 on smaller screens).

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This teaser appears in the lower portion of the page as part of a reference-and-resources band beneath the calendar/activities row, separated from that row by about 24–32px of vertical space. Within this band, the main content column is divided into two vertical columns: homework-policies-teaser and supply-list-teaser stack in the left column (about 50% width), while about-staff-teaser and summer-camp-promo stack in the right column (about 50% width with a 24px gutter between columns). The homework-policies-teaser is the topmost card in the left column, aligned with the top of the about-staff-teaser on the right. It is a flat, simple card with a heading like “Homework & Assignment Policies,” a brief one-line summary, and a clear link or button label (e.g., “View policies”) aligned to the left. The card has about 16px internal padding and sits within its column with 8–12px vertical spacing above and below. This placement ensures that key academic expectations are easy to find while remaining distinct from the more operational tools higher on the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard card in a two-column grid; stacks above supply-list-teaser in the left column and, on smaller screens, appears as a full-width card that stacks above the other three teasers.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Located directly beneath the homework-policies-teaser in the left column of the lower reference band, the supply-list-teaser occupies the same approximate width (about 50% of the main content column) and aligns with the left column’s grid. There is a 16px vertical gap between the bottom of the homework-policies-teaser and the top of the supply-list-teaser. The card presents a concise heading such as “School Supply Lists,” with a short description or grade-based note and a link label like “View supply lists.” The visual style matches the homework-policies card: flat, minimally decorated, with 16px internal padding and 8–12px spacing between the heading, description, and link. The overall height is slightly smaller than the homework-policies-teaser, giving the column a subtle top-heavy feel that matches the hierarchy of importance. The bottom margin of this card lines up roughly with, or slightly above, the summer-camp-promo in the right column, maintaining a clean baseline across the two columns.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard card stacked in the left column of a two-column grid; on smaller screens it appears as a full-width card beneath the homework-policies-teaser and above the right-column teasers.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The about-staff-teaser sits at the top of the right column in the same lower reference band that houses homework and supply list content. This right column occupies about 50% of the main content width, with a 24px horizontal gap separating it from the left column. The top edge of this teaser aligns horizontally with the top of the homework-policies-teaser for a structured grid appearance. The card is similar in size and treatment to homework-policies: a heading such as “Our Staff” or “Meet the Teachers,” a short supportive sentence, and a clear link or button. Padding (around 16px) and spacing (8–12px between elements) follow the same system, but the copy can be slightly warmer to reflect the human aspect of the content while still retaining the institutional tone. This placement in the upper right assures staff information is easily discoverable within the secondary scan path without competing with primary tools and announcements.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard card forming the top of the right column in the two-column grid; on smaller screens it stacks below the two left-column cards in the linear reading order.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The summer-camp-promo appears directly beneath the about-staff-teaser in the right column of the lower reference band, with a vertical gap of about 16px between them. It shares the same width as the about-staff card (about 50% of the main content column) and aligns with the global two-column structure. This card can be slightly more visually attention-grabbing than the other reference cards—perhaps using a bolder accent color bar along its top edge or a small image thumbnail on the right—while still remaining flat and consistent with the overall conservative design. The content includes a heading like “Summer Camp & Programs,” a short descriptor (dates, age range, or key message), and an action label such as “Learn more” or “Register.” Internal padding is about 16px, with 8–12px spacing between text elements. The bottom edge of this card roughly aligns with or slightly extends below the supply-list-teaser to its left, forming a coherent lower boundary for the page’s main content. A final 32–40px of whitespace beneath this band gives breathing room before any global footer.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard card stacked in the right column; on smaller screens it appears as the last full-width card in the vertical flow, following the about-staff-teaser.

name: After-School Programs
title: After-School Programs
description: This page lets families search, filter, sort, and save after-school programs using dynamic data from the school’s system.

core_features: Load dynamic filter and sort options for after-school programs using getAfterSchoolProgramFilterOptions.
Search and filter after-school programs based on the selected criteria and URL parameters using searchAfterSchoolPrograms.
Sort the list of programs by a selected sort option (for example, time or cost) using the sort options returned by getAfterSchoolProgramFilterOptions and passed to searchAfterSchoolPrograms.
Display program information in program cards (name, description, category, grades, fees, schedule, instructor, and other fields returned by searchAfterSchoolPrograms).
Allow parents to save/favorite individual programs directly from each program card using saveAfterSchoolProgramFavorite and reflect the is_favorited state in the UI.
Keep filter and sort state in the URL (gradeLevel, category, maxFee, endTimeBefore, sortBy) so that links from other pages and browser navigation restore the same view.

user_workflows.name: Browse programs from site navigation (no preselected filters)
steps: User clicks a navigation link labeled for after-school activities (for example, from the site header or footer), which opens after_school_programs.html with no query parameters.
On page load, the page controller component calls getAfterSchoolProgramFilterOptions to retrieve available grade levels, categories, fee frequencies, schedule days, and sort options.

The page initializes an internal filter state with no user-selected filters and a default sort option derived from the sort options returned by getAfterSchoolProgramFilterOptions (for example, the first option).
The page controller calls searchAfterSchoolPrograms with a filters object containing at least an is_active constraint and any default constraints, plus the current sort option.
When searchAfterSchoolPrograms returns results, the page displays them as a list of program cards, each showing the fields provided (including is_favorited).
The user can scroll through the list, inspect details on each program card, and optionally click the favorite control on any program to save it.

user_workflows.name: Open page with preselected filters from another page
steps: User clicks a link on index.html that includes query parameters such as gradeLevel, category, maxFee, endTimeBefore, and sortBy in the URL for after_school_programs.html.
On page load, the page controller parses the URL parameters and stores them as a preliminary filter and sort state.

The page controller calls getAfterSchoolProgramFilterOptions and uses its response to validate and normalize the URL parameters. Any URL parameter value that does not match one of the allowed filter values (for example, an unknown grade level or category) is ignored.

The validated gradeLevel, category, maxFee, endTimeBefore, and sortBy values are applied to the filter controls (grade level selection, category selection, fee input, end time input, and sort control).

The page controller builds a filters object from the validated state (for example, mapping gradeLevel to a grade_levels filter array, category to a category filter, maxFee to a maximum fee filter, and endTimeBefore to an end time cutoff filter) and passes it, along with sortBy, to searchAfterSchoolPrograms.
When searchAfterSchoolPrograms returns results, the page shows the filtered list of program cards. A summary component indicates which filters are active, reflecting what was passed in via the URL.
The user can adjust or clear filters, which updates both the visible controls and the URL parameters, and then re-runs searchAfterSchoolPrograms.

user_workflows.name: Refine search with filters and sorting
steps: User interacts with the filter bar to select or change filters (for example, selecting a grade level from a dropdown backed by getAfterSchoolProgramFilterOptions.grade_levels or entering a maximum fee).
User clicks an "Apply filters" action in the filter bar. The filter bar emits an event containing the new filter state.

The page controller converts the current filter state into the filters object expected by searchAfterSchoolPrograms (including any schedule day, fee frequency, or other filters that have been selected).

The page controller updates the browser URL query string to reflect the new gradeLevel, category, maxFee, endTimeBefore, and sortBy values (adding, updating, or removing parameters as needed) without a full page reload.
The page controller calls searchAfterSchoolPrograms with the updated filters and current sort option.
When results return, the program list and summary components re-render with the new set of programs and updated active filter chips.

If the user changes the sort option (using the sort control connected to sort_options from getAfterSchoolProgramFilterOptions), the page controller updates the sortBy URL parameter and calls searchAfterSchoolPrograms again with the same filters but the new sort_by value.

user_workflows.name: Save/favorite a program from its program card
steps: User reviews a program card in the program list. The card shows whether is_favorited is true or false (for example, via the icon or button state).
If the program is not yet favorited (is_favorited is false), the user clicks the favorite action on the card.
The program card triggers a favorite event with the program’s id. The page controller (or card-level handler) calls saveAfterSchoolProgramFavorite with the selected programId.
While the request is in progress, the favorite control can be temporarily disabled or show a loading state to prevent duplicate submissions.

When saveAfterSchoolProgramFavorite returns success, the page updates the local representation of that program’s is_favorited field to true and optionally shows a brief confirmation message using the response message.
If saveAfterSchoolProgramFavorite indicates failure, the page leaves the is_favorited state unchanged and can show an error message, allowing the user to retry.

If the user clicks the favorite action on a program that is already favorited (is_favorited is true), the page does not call saveAfterSchoolProgramFavorite again and instead can show a non-blocking message that the program is already saved or simply keep the favorite control in a non-interactive state.

user_workflows.name: Use browser navigation and shareable URLs
steps: User adjusts filters and sorting on the page; each change updates the URL query parameters to reflect the current gradeLevel, category, maxFee, endTimeBefore, and sortBy.
User copies the URL from the browser to share it with another guardian or to save it.
Another user opens the same URL or the original user returns via the browser’s history.

On load, the page controller reads the URL parameters, calls getAfterSchoolProgramFilterOptions, validates the parameter values, and then calls searchAfterSchoolPrograms with filters that match the URL.
The same filtered and sorted program list is displayed, reproducing the state implied by the URL without relying on localStorage or other client-side persistence.

interactions.name: Initial data load
interactions.description: On page load, the page controller reads URL parameters, calls getAfterSchoolProgramFilterOptions, initializes filter and sort state (using the API response and any valid URL parameters), and then calls searchAfterSchoolPrograms to retrieve the initial list of programs.

interactions.name: Filter control changes (grade level, category, fee, time, schedule, fee frequency)
interactions.description: When the user changes any filter control in the filter bar (for example, selecting a grade level or changing the maximum fee), the filter bar updates its internal state but does not immediately trigger a search until the user activates the "Apply filters" action, minimizing unnecessary API calls.

interactions.name: Apply filters
interactions.description: When the user clicks the "Apply filters" action, the filter bar emits the full current filter state. The page controller maps this state to the filters object expected by searchAfterSchoolPrograms, updates the URL parameters (gradeLevel, category, maxFee, endTimeBefore, and leaves sortBy unchanged), and calls searchAfterSchoolPrograms.

interactions.name: Clear all filters
interactions.description: When the user clicks a "Clear filters" control, the filter bar resets all filter controls to an unfiltered state. The page controller clears the corresponding URL parameters (gradeLevel, category, maxFee, endTimeBefore) while preserving or resetting sortBy according to a default rule, and then calls searchAfterSchoolPrograms with only the baseline filters (such as is_active) applied.

interactions.name: Change sort option
interactions.description: When the user selects a different sort option from the sort control, the sort control emits the new sort key. The page controller updates the sortBy URL parameter and calls searchAfterSchoolPrograms with the current filters and the new sort_by value.

interactions.name: Favorite a program
interactions.description: When the user clicks the favorite action on a program card that is not already favorited, the card triggers a handler that calls saveAfterSchoolProgramFavorite with the program id, then updates the card’s is_favorited state based on the response.

interactions.name: View more/less program details on a card
interactions.description: When the user expands a program card (for example, by clicking a "details" toggle), the card reveals additional fields already returned by searchAfterSchoolPrograms (such as full description, instructor_name, location, schedule_days_label, max_enrollment). This interaction uses only data already loaded from the search results and does not require additional API calls.

interactions.name: Remove individual filter from summary
interactions.description: When the user clicks an active filter chip in the results summary (for example, a chip representing a selected category), the summary emits a request to remove that specific filter. The page controller updates the filter state and URL parameters accordingly and calls searchAfterSchoolPrograms with the updated filters.

interactions.name: Client-side pagination or result limiting (optional)
interactions.description: If the result set is large, the page can implement client-side pagination by displaying only a subset of the programs per page. When the user changes pages, the pagination controls adjust which portion of the in-memory searchAfterSchoolPrograms results is shown without making additional API calls, keeping pagination state in URL parameters if desired.

page_functionality.state_logic: The page state is fully controlled by in-memory state and URL parameters, not by localStorage. On every page load or history navigation, the page controller parses the current URL query string and uses the following logic: (1) gradeLevel: If present, this value is treated as a candidate grade level filter and is validated against the grade_levels options returned by getAfterSchoolProgramFilterOptions. If valid, it initializes the grade level filter and the corresponding control; if invalid or missing, no grade level filter is applied and the grade level control is left unselected. (2) category: If present, this value is validated against the categories returned by getAfterSchoolProgramFilterOptions. If valid, it initializes the category filter and control; otherwise it is ignored. (3) maxFee: If present and parseable as a number, it is set as the maximum fee filter in the filter state (using a filter key that the searchAfterSchoolPrograms implementation understands, such as a maximum fee amount applied across the chosen or default fee frequency). The numeric input in the filter bar is initialized to this value. If invalid or missing, no maximum fee constraint is applied. (4) endTimeBefore: If present, it is used as a cutoff value for the program end_time filter (using a key such as end_time_before in the filters object passed to searchAfterSchoolPrograms). The time control in the filter bar is initialized to this value. If absent, no time cutoff is applied. (5) sortBy: If present, its value is validated against the sort_options returned by getAfterSchoolProgramFilterOptions. If valid, it becomes the current sort_by; if invalid or missing, the page uses a default sort_by derived from the first or otherwise designated default sort option from getAfterSchoolProgramFilterOptions. (6) After validating and normalizing all URL parameters, the page constructs a filters object for searchAfterSchoolPrograms, which always includes an is_active constraint and conditionally includes constraints for grade_levels, category, maximum fee, schedule_days, fee_frequency, and end time cutoff, depending on which controls are selected. (7) Whenever the user changes filters or sorting via the UI, the page controller updates the URL parameters to reflect the new filter and sort state using pushState or replaceState (so that browser navigation and link sharing reproduce the view) and then calls searchAfterSchoolPrograms with the updated filters and sort_by. (8) The is_favorited field for each program is initialized from the searchAfterSchoolPrograms response on every load or refresh. When saveAfterSchoolProgramFavorite is called and succeeds, the page updates the in-memory is_favorited value for that program, but does not store anything in localStorage; on a future load, is_favorited is refreshed from searchAfterSchoolPrograms again.

components.functionality: Coordinates initial load and ongoing updates of the After-School Programs page. On page load, parses URL parameters (gradeLevel, category, maxFee, endTimeBefore, sortBy), calls getAfterSchoolProgramFilterOptions, validates and applies any URL-based filters/sort, constructs the filters object, and calls searchAfterSchoolPrograms. Listens for filter, sort, and favorite events from child components; updates URL parameters and in-memory state; re-invokes searchAfterSchoolPrograms as needed; and distributes the resulting list of programs and filter metadata to other components.

Provides interactive controls for users to define search filters, including grade level, category, maximum fee, end time cutoff, schedule days, and fee frequency. The options for grade levels, categories, fee frequencies, and schedule days are populated dynamically from getAfterSchoolProgramFilterOptions. It reflects any initial state derived from URL parameters and allows the user to modify filters and then apply or clear them.

Lets the user choose how the list of programs is ordered. Displays the sort options from getAfterSchoolProgramFilterOptions.sort_options and indicates the currently selected sort key. When changed, it notifies the page controller, which updates the sortBy URL parameter and re-runs searchAfterSchoolPrograms with the new sort_by value.

Summarizes the current search results and active filters. Shows the total number of programs returned by searchAfterSchoolPrograms and a list of active filters (such as selected grade level, category, maximum fee, end time cutoff, chosen schedule days, and fee frequency). Each active filter appears as a removable chip that can be clicked to remove that filter and trigger a refreshed search.

Displays the collection of after-school programs returned by searchAfterSchoolPrograms as a list of program-card components. Receives the array of program objects from the page controller, applies any client-side slicing (for example, pagination), and renders one program-card per program.

Represents a single after-school program in the list. Displays key fields from the searchAfterSchoolPrograms result for that program, such as name, description, category_label, grade_level_labels, fee_amount, fee_frequency_label, fee_notes, schedule_days_label, start_time, end_time, location, instructor_name, max_enrollment, and is_favorited. Provides a control for saving/favoriting the program and a control to expand/collapse additional details. Emits events to request saving/favoriting and to toggle details visibility.

Handles favorite/save requests triggered from program-card components. When a program-card emits onFavoriteClick with a program id for a program that is not already favorited (is_favorited is false), this handler calls saveAfterSchoolProgramFavorite with that programId. It processes the response and updates the corresponding program’s is_favorited field in the in-memory results and can pass a success or error message to a feedback component.

Provides optional client-side pagination across the array of programs returned by searchAfterSchoolPrograms. It tracks the current page and items per page, and informs the program-list which slice of the results to display. Pagination state can be synchronized with URL parameters (for example, a page index) if desired, but does not cause additional API calls because it operates on the full result set already returned.

Displays a message and optional guidance when searchAfterSchoolPrograms returns an empty array for the current filters. It may provide a quick action to clear filters or adjust them (for example, a button that triggers the filter-bar’s onClearFiltersClick handler).

Shows short-lived notifications in response to certain actions, such as successfully saving a program via saveAfterSchoolProgramFavorite or encountering an error when searching or saving. It consumes messages passed from the page controller or favorite-action-handler based on API responses.

layout.grid_system: Centered fixed-width content column likely based on a 12-column grid (~1080–1200px wide) within full-bleed horizontal bands.
layout.layout_pattern: Within the existing site chrome (utility bar, global nav, banner, tabs), this page uses a single centered content column (~1080–1200px) below the active tab. Inside that column, the flow is strictly top-to-bottom: page title and brief intro (within the page controller), then a functional control band (filters, results summary, sort), followed by the main program list or empty state, and finally pagination. All components align to the same left content edge, with occasional right-aligned elements (sort control, favorite icons) to support the F-pattern.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Within the existing site chrome (utility bar, global nav, banner, tabs), this page uses a single centered content column (~1080–1200px) below the active tab. Inside that column, the flow is strictly top-to-bottom: page title and brief intro (within the page controller), then a functional control band (filters, results summary, sort), followed by the main program list or empty state, and finally pagination. All components align to the same left content edge, with occasional right-aligned elements (sort control, favorite icons) to support the F-pattern.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Roughly the upper 30–35% of the content column (below the tabs) is allocated to framing and controls: page heading, intro text, filter bar, and results/sort strip. The remaining 65–70% of the visible content area is given to the program list itself. Horizontal space is mostly dedicated to text content (70–80% of the column), with smaller right-aligned control elements (20–30%) such as sorting and favorite actions. Vertical spacing between major bands (title/intro, filters, list, pagination) uses 24–32px, while internal spacing within controls and cards follows the 8–16px rhythm.
layout_strategies.content_density: The page maintains moderate density: controls and text are compact enough to keep filters, summary, and at least 2–3 program cards visible above the fold on common desktop sizes. Line heights and padding are comfortable but not airy, with compact rows in the filter and results/sort areas and more generous padding inside program cards to support readability. The layout prioritizes legibility and efficient scanning over visual flair.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The user’s eye follows an institutional F-pattern: after the global header, it hits the page title at the top left of the content column, moves right to any secondary info or alignment, then drops to the filter bar (scanning its controls left to right). From there, the eye moves down to the left-aligned results summary and then to the right-aligned sort control. It then continues downward through the vertically stacked program cards, with attention periodically pulled to right-side elements like favorite icons, and finally reaches the centered pagination at the bottom. Toast notifications appear near the bottom-center of the viewport, briefly interrupting but not disrupting this flow.

layout.overall_description: Below the site’s header, banner, and tab bar, a single, centered content column anchors the After-School Programs page. At the top of this column, the page controller presents a breadcrumb (if used) and a large, left-aligned “After-School Programs” heading, with a short one- or two-line description beneath it. A modest vertical gap leads into a full-width filter bar that reads as a functional strip across the column. Immediately below, a lean results-and-sort row stretches from left to right: the results summary text sits on the left, and the sort control is aligned to the right on the same baseline. The main body of the page is a vertical stack of full-width program cards, each a flat, bordered panel with text-heavy details on the left and a discrete favorite icon tucked into the top-right corner. If there are no results, the card stack is replaced by a centered empty-state message in the same region. At the very bottom of the content column, pagination controls are centered, creating a clear endpoint to each page of results. Throughout, vertical spacing between bands is consistent and measured, maintaining the conservative, institutional look. A feedback toast occasionally appears anchored near the bottom center of the viewport, overlaying the content without shifting the layout, then fades away to restore the uninterrupted reading flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page controller defines the main content column and the initial framing of the page. It sits directly below the existing tabbed navigation band, sharing the same centered fixed-width container (~1080–1200px). At its top, a breadcrumb row (if present) is left-aligned and spans roughly 70–80% of the column, with 8–12px of padding above and below. Below this, the primary H1 'After-School Programs' appears as a strong left-aligned block, occupying the full column width but visually weighted on the left two-thirds, with 16–24px of spacing above and below. Any optional small supporting visual (e.g., a school emblem or icon) would align to the right of this H1 within the same horizontal row, occupying no more than 20–25% of the width, mirroring the mockup’s right-aligned school image behavior. Under the H1, a short introductory paragraph or a concise bullet list of 2–3 key facts about the programs is left-aligned and limited to ~70% of the column width, with 8–16px spacing between lines. There is a 24–32px vertical gap between the bottom of this intro area and the start of the filter bar, clearly separating structural framing from interactive filters.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, occupying the top portion of the main content column and setting the alignment and width constraints for all subsequent components.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The filter bar appears directly beneath the page controller’s introductory content, extending across the full width of the centered content column. It reads as a functional horizontal band, approximately 64–80px tall, with 16px of internal padding on all sides. Filter controls (e.g., grade level, day of week, activity type) are left-aligned within this band and laid out in one or two horizontal rows, each control respecting 8–16px horizontal spacing. Collectively, the filters occupy about 70–80% of the column width, leaving the far right side visually lighter to maintain the F-pattern. The bar’s background is a very light neutral (e.g., light grey or a low-tint blue) to gently separate it from the white page background without heavy decoration. There is 16–24px of vertical space between the bottom of the filter bar and the results-summary/sort strip, maintaining clear separation between these control layers.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static; it scrolls with the page as part of the content column, anchoring the top of the functional control area without any sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The sort control sits on the same horizontal row as the results summary, directly beneath the filter bar. It is right-aligned within the main content column, occupying roughly 20–25% of the width. The control is compact: a short 'Sort by' label followed by a dropdown or segmented control, aligned horizontally and centered vertically within a ~40–48px tall row. It sits flush with the right content edge, leaving at least 16px of whitespace between its left edge and the right edge of the results-summary text. On wider viewports it remains a small, neat block on the far right, visually balancing the left-aligned summary text. There is 8–12px of vertical spacing above (from the filter bar) and below (to the top of the program list) this shared row.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static; it aligns responsively with the results-summary. On significantly narrower widths it can stack below the results summary while maintaining right alignment, but within the standard fixed-width desktop column it remains on the same line.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The results summary is positioned immediately below the filter bar and forms the left side of a slim horizontal strip shared with the sort control. It is left-aligned and spans approximately 60–70% of the content column, starting at the same left margin as the page title above. The summary text (e.g., '32 programs found • Showing 1–10') is on a single line where possible, with a 40–48px row height to align with the sort control on the right. This strip has a white background and minimal decoration, relying on 8–12px of vertical whitespace above and below to separate it from the filter bar and the program list. If a secondary line is needed (e.g., 'Filters: Grade 3–5, Mondays'), it appears directly below the main summary line with 4–8px of spacing, still constrained to the left 60–70% of the column, maintaining the dominant left-weighted reading flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static; it serves as a transitional band between the filter controls and the main content list, maintaining its position relative to the sort control on the right.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The program list occupies the core of the page and begins directly beneath the results-summary/sort row, with 16–24px of vertical spacing separating them. It spans the full width of the content column, but the visual weight remains toward the left as program cards stack vertically in a single column. Each card takes up nearly the full width (allowing for consistent left and right internal padding), and cards are separated from one another by 16–24px of vertical space. The visible portion of the list is designed so that, on a typical desktop viewport, at least two full cards and part of a third can be seen without scrolling, reinforcing the moderate density. The list ends above the pagination controls with another 24–32px of vertical space to clearly distinguish the card stack from the navigation controls at the bottom.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static vertical stack within the main column; when there are no results, this region is replaced entirely by the empty-state component, occupying the same vertical slot.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each program card is a flat rectangular panel within the program list, aligned with the content column’s left and right edges. The card has a white background, a subtle 1px grey border, and 16–24px of internal padding on all sides, with a total height typically between 120–200px depending on content. Internally, the card layout follows a two-region structure: the left region (~70–75% of the card’s width) contains the program name as a bold title at the top, followed by key details (days, time, grade range, provider) in 1–3 compact text lines, and then a brief 1–2 line description; each of these text blocks is separated by 8–12px of vertical spacing. The right region (~25–30% of the width) is reserved for secondary metadata (such as capacity, fees, or status labels) and the favorite action, aligned to the top-right corner. Content in the right region is right-aligned and stacked vertically with 8px spacing, ensuring it does not visually compete with the main title. The favorite icon is visually tied to the top-right corner, with 8–12px of inset from the card’s right and top edges.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the program list; cards maintain consistent width and spacing and do not overlap. Their height expands naturally to accommodate variable text length while preserving internal padding and separation.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The favorite action handler manifests as a small, discrete control (typically an icon button such as a star or heart) within each program card. It is anchored in the card’s top-right corner, occupying a square area of approximately 24–32px, with 8–12px padding from the card’s top and right edges. Visually, it floats above the textual content to its left, but remains within the card’s border. The control aligns horizontally with the program title on the left, so that the user’s eye, after reading the title, can glance right to identify the favorite state. When activated, it provides immediate visual feedback (e.g., filled icon) without changing the card’s size or layout. Spatially, it does not push or pull any other content; it simply claims a reserved space in the upper-right of every card.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within each card but interactive; activation may trigger the feedback-toast component without affecting the card’s layout or the surrounding cards.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Pagination controls sit at the very bottom of the main content column, directly beneath the last program card (or empty-state), separated by 24–32px of vertical space. The pagination strip spans the full column width, but its interactive elements (Previous/Next buttons and page numbers) are grouped and horizontally centered, creating a clear end-of-page anchor. The row height is around 40–48px, with 8–12px of internal vertical padding and 8–16px horizontal spacing between individual page links. The styling is simple and flat, with the current page emphasized using a bold weight or filled pill, while other pages remain outlined or underlined. Below the pagination, an additional 32–48px of whitespace separates it from any global footer, maintaining the site’s banded hierarchy.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static; it remains at the bottom of the content list for the current page and scrolls off-screen naturally as the user returns upward.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The empty-state display occupies the same spatial region as the program list when filters yield no results. It is centered within the main content column, starting at the same vertical point where the first program card would normally appear, with 16–24px of spacing from the results-summary/sort strip above. The empty-state content is contained within a subtle panel or just a centered block of text, approximately 40–60% of the column width, aligned to the center horizontally but still respecting the column’s left and right margins. A short, clear headline (e.g., 'No programs match your filters') appears at the top, followed by 8–12px of spacing and a brief explanatory sentence, and then a primary action (such as 'Clear filters') positioned directly below with 16–24px of spacing. There is ample whitespace (at least 32px) above and below this block, making it visually distinct yet consistent with the conservative style.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Mutually exclusive with the program-list: it takes over the list’s vertical slot when there are zero results and relinquishes it when results are available.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The feedback toast is a small, floating notification that appears over the page content rather than within the fixed-width column. It is horizontally centered in the viewport and anchored near the bottom, approximately 16–24px above the lower edge of the browser window, spanning about 320–400px in width. The toast has a subtle rounded rectangle shape with a solid background using the brand blue or a neutral tone, light text, and 12–16px internal padding. It sits above all other content layers, slightly overlapping the main content column but not obscuring critical navigation or controls. When triggered (e.g., after favoriting a program), it fades in, remains visible for a few seconds, then fades out or can be dismissed via a small close icon in the top-right of the toast. It does not cause any reflow of surrounding content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed relative to the viewport; overlays the content without affecting layout, always appearing at the bottom-center regardless of scroll position.

name: Lunch Menu
title: Lunch Menu
description: View weekly school lunch menus, apply dietary filters, and add lunch entrées to your Meal Planner for specific days.

core_features: Load all available lunch menu weeks using getMenuWeeks and allow the user to select a week.
Use an optional URL parameter menuWeekId to pre-select the current menu week when the page loads.
Fetch the lunch menu for the selected week using getLunchMenuForWeek, showing results grouped by day.
Filter the displayed lunch menu by dietary preferences (e.g., Vegetarian, Nut-Free) by passing filter parameters into getLunchMenuForWeek.
Limit displayed items to lunch entries (meal_type = "lunch") and clearly distinguish primary entrées from sides and other items.
Let users add a specific entrée for a specific date to their Meal Planner by calling addMealToPlanner with menuWeekId, date, and menuItemId.
Display a lightweight summary of the user’s Meal Planner for the current week, based on the response returned from addMealToPlanner.
Provide navigation to open the same week directly in meal_planner.html using the selected menuWeekId in the query string.
Provide a Families-related link to supply_lists.html from the page content.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1: Landing on the Lunch Menu page
1) User opens lunch_menu.html (optionally with a menuWeekId query parameter).
2) Page reads the menuWeekId from the URL if present.
3) Page calls getMenuWeeks to retrieve all available MenuWeek options.
4) Page chooses the initially selected week: if the URL menuWeekId matches one of the returned weeks, that week is selected; otherwise, the page selects a default week (for example, the first available week returned by getMenuWeeks).
5) Page calls getLunchMenuForWeek with the selected menuWeekId and no dietary filters applied.
6) Page renders the weekly lunch menu grouped by day using the returned data.

Workflow 2: Changing the selected week
1) User opens the week selector control and chooses a different week.
2) Page updates its internal selectedWeekId state to the chosen MenuWeek.id.
3) Page updates the URL query string to include menuWeekId={selectedWeekId} (preserving any existing dietary filter parameters in the URL).
4) Page calls getLunchMenuForWeek with the new menuWeekId and the current filter settings.
5) Page replaces the current menu display with the newly fetched data for the chosen week.

Workflow 3: Applying or clearing dietary filters
1) User toggles dietary filter controls such as Vegetarian or Nut-Free.
2) Page updates its internal filter state (for example, vegetarian=true/false, nut_free=true/false).
3) Page updates the URL query string to reflect active filters (for example, veg=1 when vegetarian is active, nutfree=1 when nut-free is active); menuWeekId is preserved.
4) Page calls getLunchMenuForWeek with menuWeekId equal to the currently selected week and filters equal to the current filter state.
5) Page re-renders the daily menu list using the filtered entries returned by getLunchMenuForWeek.

Workflow 4: Reviewing daily lunch options
1) User scrolls within the weekly menu display.
2) For each day object returned from getLunchMenuForWeek, the page shows the date, day_of_week, and a list of entries.
3) The list is programmatically filtered to show only entries with meal_type = "lunch".
4) Within each day, primary entrées (is_primary_entree = true and item_type = "entree") are highlighted functionally (e.g., marked as Entrée) and are the only items that expose an "Add to Meal Planner" action.
5) Non-primary or non-entrée items (such as sides, desserts, drinks) are displayed without the add-to-planner action.

Workflow 5: Adding an entrée for a specific day to the Meal Planner
1) User identifies a primary entrée in a given day’s lunch list.
2) User clicks the "Add to Meal Planner" action associated with that entrée.
3) The page constructs a request to addMealToPlanner with the current menuWeekId, the date from the selected day object, and the menuItemId from the selected entry.
4) Page calls addMealToPlanner and awaits the response.
5) On success (response.success = true), the page:
   - Shows a confirmation message using the response.message.
   - Updates an in-page planner summary component using response.meal_plan_week and response.entries_by_day.
6) If response.success is false, the page displays the error message contained in response.message.

Workflow 6: Opening the same week in the Meal Planner
1) User wants to manage or review the plan in more detail.
2) User clicks a contextual button labeled functionally to open the current week in the Meal Planner.
3) The page reads the currently selected menuWeekId from its state.
4) The browser navigates to meal_planner.html?menuWeekId={currentMenuWeekId}, passing that id as a URL parameter.
5) meal_planner.html (outside this spec) can then use the provided menuWeekId to load or align its own view.

Workflow 7: Navigating to the School Supply Lists page
1) In a Families-related section of the page, the user clicks a link to view supply lists.
2) The browser navigates to supply_lists.html without additional parameters, as specified by the outgoing navigation config.
3) The current Lunch Menu page state does not need to be preserved, since it is encoded in the URL (users can use the Back button to return).
interactions: Week selection interaction: User changes the selected week in a dropdown or similar control; triggers a state update, URL update (menuWeekId), and a new getLunchMenuForWeek call.

Filter toggle interaction: User checks or unchecks Vegetarian and Nut-Free filter options; triggers state updates, URL filter parameter updates, and a new getLunchMenuForWeek call with updated filters.

Add entrée to planner interaction: User clicks an "Add to Meal Planner" control on an eligible entrée; triggers addMealToPlanner with the associated date and menuItemId, then updates the planner summary and shows feedback based on the response.

Open week in planner interaction: User clicks a contextual button to open the current menu week in the Meal Planner; triggers client-side navigation to meal_planner.html with the current menuWeekId in the query string.
Supply lists navigation interaction: User clicks "View School Supply Lists"; triggers navigation to supply_lists.html.

Pagination/scroll interaction within the weekly menu: User scrolls or navigates within the weekly menu component to inspect different days; the component uses already-fetched getLunchMenuForWeek data and does not re-call APIs unless the week or filters change.

Reload / direct-link interaction: User reloads the page or accesses a bookmarked URL containing menuWeekId (and possibly dietary filter parameters); the state manager rehydrates the initial state from the URL, calls getMenuWeeks and then getLunchMenuForWeek, and renders the appropriate week and filters without needing local storage.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The primary source of truth for page state is the URL query string, not localStorage. The page reads and writes the following URL parameters:
- menuWeekId: represents the currently selected MenuWeek.id. On initial load, if menuWeekId is present and corresponds to a MenuWeek returned by getMenuWeeks, that week is selected; otherwise, the page selects a default week (e.g., the first menu week returned). When the user selects a new week via the week selector, the page updates this parameter and re-fetches data using getLunchMenuForWeek.
- veg (optional): represents whether the Vegetarian filter is active. When veg is present and truthy (e.g., veg=1), the page includes a vegetarian filter flag in the filters argument passed to getLunchMenuForWeek. When the user toggles the Vegetarian filter, the page updates veg in the URL accordingly (adding it when active, removing it when inactive).
- nutfree (optional): represents whether the Nut-Free filter is active. When nutfree is present and truthy (e.g., nutfree=1), the page includes a nut-free filter flag in the filters argument passed to getLunchMenuForWeek. When the user toggles the Nut-Free filter, the page updates nutfree in the URL accordingly.

On page load:
1) Parse menuWeekId, veg, and nutfree from the URL.
2) Call getMenuWeeks to obtain the full list of weeks.
3) Determine the selected week id based on menuWeekId (or a default if missing/invalid).
4) Build a filters object from veg and nutfree flags and call getLunchMenuForWeek(menuWeekId=selectedWeekId, filters=currentFilters).

On state changes (week selection or filter toggles), the page:
1) Updates in-memory UI state for the selected week and filters.
2) Synchronizes the URL parameters to reflect the new state, preserving other parameters.
3) Calls getLunchMenuForWeek with the updated parameters.

State for the Meal Planner summary is maintained in memory in response to addMealToPlanner responses. This state is not persisted to the URL; instead, users navigate to meal_planner.html (with menuWeekId in the URL) for a full planner view.

components.functionality: Allows the user to choose which lunch menu week (MenuWeek) to view. On load, it populates its options from getMenuWeeks and pre-selects the week based on the menuWeekId URL parameter when possible.
event_handlers: onInit: Call getMenuWeeks, store the returned list of weeks, and determine the initial selected week based on the URL's menuWeekId or a default selection.

onChange: When the user selects a different week, update the selectedWeekId in page state, update the URL query string menuWeekId, and trigger a new getLunchMenuForWeek call via the weekly-menu component with the updated week and current filters.

components.functionality: Manages dietary preference filters (Vegetarian and Nut-Free) for the lunch menu. It controls which items are requested from getLunchMenuForWeek by updating filter parameters and the URL.
event_handlers: onInit: Read veg and nutfree parameters from the URL and set the initial states of the corresponding filter toggles.
onToggleVegetarian: Toggle the vegetarian filter state, update the veg parameter in the URL, and request updated lunch menu data from getLunchMenuForWeek with the new filters.
onToggleNutFree: Toggle the nut-free filter state, update the nutfree parameter in the URL, and request updated lunch menu data from getLunchMenuForWeek with the new filters.

components.functionality: Displays the lunch menu for the currently selected week, grouped by day, and filtered according to the active dietary filters. It calls getLunchMenuForWeek whenever the selected week or filters change and renders days and their lunch entries.
event_handlers: onWeekOrFilterChange: When notified by the week-selector or dietary-filter-controls that the week or filters changed, call getLunchMenuForWeek(menuWeekId, filters) and store the returned menu_week and days data.

onRender: For each day in the returned days array, filter entries to those where meal_type equals "lunch" before displaying them. Within each day, distinguish primary entrées (is_primary_entree true and item_type "entree") from sides and other items, and expose add-to-planner actions only on eligible entrées.
