onRefreshRequest: Provide a method to re-fetch the current week's menu explicitly (e.g., after an error), re-calling getLunchMenuForWeek with the most recent menuWeekId and filters.

components.functionality: Represents a single day within the weekly lunch menu. Renders the date, day_of_week, and a list of lunch entries for that day, including functional markers for entrées vs. sides and actions for eligible entrées.
event_handlers: onBindDayData: When the weekly-menu component passes a day object, bind date and day_of_week to heading elements and entries to a list.
onEntryClick: Optionally allow the user to expand/collapse an entry to see details such as description, allergen_labels, and calories without additional API calls.
onAddToPlannerClick: For applicable entries (primary entrée lunch items), forward add-to-planner requests with the day’s date and the entry’s menu_item_id to the add-to-meal-planner handler.

components.functionality: Handles the process of adding a selected lunch entrée to the Meal Planner for a specific date using the addMealToPlanner interface and updates the planner summary based on the response.
event_handlers: onAddClick: When triggered from a daily-menu-day-block for an eligible entrée, call addMealToPlanner with the current menuWeekId (from page state), the specific date for that day, and the menuItemId of the selected entry.
onAddSuccess: When addMealToPlanner returns success=true, update the planner-summary component’s data with meal_plan_week and entries_by_day and display a confirmation using the response message.
onAddFailure: If addMealToPlanner returns success=false, display the returned error message in a non-disruptive error area associated with the relevant day or entry.
onBusyStateChange: Manage a transient busy/disabled state for the specific entrée’s Add button while the addMealToPlanner request is in flight to prevent duplicate submissions.

components.functionality: Shows a condensed view of the user’s Meal Planner for the currently selected week, derived solely from the most recent addMealToPlanner response(s). It helps users confirm which days already have planned entrées.
event_handlers: onPlannerDataUpdate: When the add-to-meal-planner-handler receives a successful response from addMealToPlanner, it passes meal_plan_week and entries_by_day into this component to update the summary.

onWeekChange: When the selected week changes, reset or adjust the summary view so it remains consistent with the current week. (The component can either clear out-of-date data or only display entries whose dates fall within the newly selected week.)
onViewInPlannerClick: Optionally provide a button that delegates to the open-week-in-planner-link component to navigate to meal_planner.html for the current menuWeekId.

components.functionality: Navigates the user from the Lunch Menu page to the Meal Planner page, preserving the currently selected menuWeekId in the URL.
event_handlers: onClick: Read the currently selected MenuWeek.id from page state and navigate to meal_planner.html?menuWeekId={selectedWeekId} to open the corresponding week in the Meal Planner.

components.functionality: Provides contextual links for families-related tasks, including a link to the School Supply Lists page.
event_handlers: onSupplyListsClick: Navigate to supply_lists.html with no additional parameters.

components.functionality: Centralizes reading and updating of URL query parameters to keep page state (selected week and filters) synchronized with the URL for direct linking and browser navigation.
event_handlers: onInit: Parse the initial URL query parameters (menuWeekId, veg, nutfree) and expose them to other components (week-selector and dietary-filter-controls) for initial state configuration.
onWeekChange: When the selected week changes, update or add menuWeekId in the URL query string while preserving existing filter parameters.
onFilterChange: When Vegetarian or Nut-Free filter states change, update or add veg and nutfree parameters in the URL while preserving the current menuWeekId.

onPopState: Listen to browser history changes (e.g., Back/Forward). When detected, re-read the URL parameters and propagate updated week and filter state to the week-selector, dietary-filter-controls, and weekly-menu components so the UI stays in sync with browser navigation.

components.functionality: Handles generic loading and error states for calls to getMenuWeeks, getLunchMenuForWeek, and addMealToPlanner, so that other components can focus on their core logic.
event_handlers: onGetMenuWeeksStart: Display a loading indicator related to the week-selector while getMenuWeeks is in progress.
onGetMenuWeeksError: Display an error message if getMenuWeeks fails and expose a retry action that re-calls getMenuWeeks.
onGetLunchMenuForWeekStart: Display or update a loading indicator in the weekly-menu component while the lunch menu is being fetched.
onGetLunchMenuForWeekError: Show an error message in the weekly-menu area and provide a retry action that re-calls getLunchMenuForWeek with the current menuWeekId and filters.
onAddMealToPlannerStart: Optionally trigger a subtle per-entry loading state through the add-to-meal-planner-handler.
onAddMealToPlannerError: Display contextual feedback for failed addMealToPlanner calls using the message from the response or a generic error if needed.

layout.grid_system: Centered fixed-width content column likely based on a 12-column grid (~1080–1200px wide) within full-bleed horizontal bands.
layout.layout_pattern: The Lunch Menu page sits within the existing top-heavy shell (utility bar, main nav, banner, tabs). Inside the centered fixed-width content column, the flow is strictly top-to-bottom: breadcrumb and page H1 ('Lunch Menu') at the top of the column, followed by a short text intro or key notes (mirroring the bullet facts pattern from the mockup). Immediately below, a single horizontal row contains the week selector on the left and the dietary filters on the right, establishing the functional controls in an F-pattern sweep. The weekly menu data view follows as one large vertical section, with a heading row that pairs the menu title on the left and the planner summary on the right. Below that row, the daily menu day blocks stack in a single column. At the bottom of the content column, the Families links section provides tertiary navigation, mirroring related-links patterns seen on institutional sites.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The Lunch Menu page sits within the existing top-heavy shell (utility bar, main nav, banner, tabs). Inside the centered fixed-width content column, the flow is strictly top-to-bottom: breadcrumb and page H1 ('Lunch Menu') at the top of the column, followed by a short text intro or key notes (mirroring the bullet facts pattern from the mockup). Immediately below, a single horizontal row contains the week selector on the left and the dietary filters on the right, establishing the functional controls in an F-pattern sweep. The weekly menu data view follows as one large vertical section, with a heading row that pairs the menu title on the left and the planner summary on the right. Below that row, the daily menu day blocks stack in a single column. At the bottom of the content column, the Families links section provides tertiary navigation, mirroring related-links patterns seen on institutional sites.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The global header, nav, banner, and tabs continue to occupy roughly the top 40% of the initial viewport. The Lunch Menu content occupies the remaining ~60%, with the H1 and control row (week selector + filters) visible above the fold along with at least the top of the weekly menu section. The main content column is centered and spans about 70–80% of the available width (roughly 1080–1200px max width). Major content groups (H1 block, control row, weekly menu section, Families links section) are separated by 24–32px vertical spacing. Within each group, components and text elements are more tightly packed with 8–16px vertical spacing and 16–24px horizontal padding, preserving the moderate density seen in the mockup.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content density is moderate and information-focused. Controls (week selector, filters) are compact, with clear labels and 8–12px vertical padding to keep the header area of the content efficient but not cramped. The weekly menu uses reasonably tight stacking of daily blocks so that most or all of the week is visible with minimal scrolling on a standard laptop viewport. Line heights and intra-block spacing follow the 8px base system, keeping paragraphs and lists legible without overly airy gaps. The planner summary is compact and card-like, sized to show a concise overview rather than a long scrollable region.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye follows an F-pattern similar to the mockup: after scanning the site’s global header, attention lands on the 'Lunch Menu' H1 at the top-left of the content column, then moves right across any intro text. Next, users scan the functional control row: week selector on the left (primary starting point) and dietary filters on the right. From there, the eye drops to the weekly menu heading, again starting on the left, and then catches the planner summary card at the upper-right of that same horizontal row. The vertical flow continues down through the daily menu blocks. At the bottom, the Families links section creates a final horizontal band of navigation items that anchors the end of the page.

layout.overall_description: Within the existing institutional shell of stacked bars and tabs, the Lunch Menu page content sits in a centered fixed-width column that reads as a single, orderly vertical strip. At the top of this column, a breadcrumb and the large 'Lunch Menu' heading establish context, with a short explanatory line or key notes immediately below, spaced by about 16–24px. Roughly another 24px down, a single horizontal control band spans most of the column: the week selector control cluster occupies the left half, while dietary filter controls align to the right, together forming a balanced tools row that rewards left-to-right scanning. Below this, separated by about 24px, the weekly menu area begins with a header row: 'This Week’s Menu' on the left and a compact planner summary card right-aligned within the same row. The daily menu day blocks then stack vertically beneath this header row, each taking the full width of the content column, with clear day labels on the left and neatly aligned entrée rows with 'Add to Planner' actions on the right. Feedback for loading and errors overlays or slots into this main content column just under the H1, never breaking the centered, single-column structure. After the weekly menu, a generous 32px gap introduces the Families links section, which spans the same content width and presents a simple grid or list of links for parents, visually echoing the site’s structured, bar-based navigation style. The result is a clean, conservative layout that feels consistent with the mockup: top-heavy framing above a focused, linear content area where tools appear first, followed by structured data, then related navigation at the bottom.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The week selector is placed near the top of the main content column, directly below the introductory text under the 'Lunch Menu' H1 and any error/banner messages. It forms the left side of a horizontal control row that spans roughly 80–90% of the content column width. The week selector itself occupies about the left 50–60% of this row, starting flush with the content column’s left edge. It may include a label ('Week of'), a date range display, and previous/next arrows arranged in a single line. Vertical padding inside the control is 8–12px, with 16–24px horizontal padding from the container’s left edge. There is approximately 24px of vertical space above this control row (from H1/intro) and 16–24px below it before the weekly menu section begins, mirroring the mockup’s separation between major content bands.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block-level element within the document flow; it stays fixed within its position in the content column and does not scroll or stick independently.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The dietary filter controls share the same horizontal row as the week selector, aligned to the right side of the content column. They occupy the remaining right 40–50% of that row, aligning their baseline with the week selector so the entire tools row reads as a single bar of controls. Filters may present as a compact set of checkboxes or pill-style toggles in one or two inline rows, right-aligned within this area. The rightmost filter edge aligns with the content column’s right margin. The vertical height of the filter area roughly matches or slightly exceeds the week selector, but the overall row height remains visually compact, consistent with the moderate density and 8–12px vertical padding pattern. There is no full-width background bar; the visual grouping is achieved through alignment and spacing.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block-level content. On narrower viewports within the same fixed-width layout, the filters can wrap to a second line beneath the week selector while maintaining left alignment to avoid overflow, but they remain logically part of the same control band.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The weekly menu occupies the central, largest content section beneath the control row. It begins about 16–24px below the week selector/filters band and spans the entire content column width (roughly 70–80% of the page width, centered). At the top of this section, a header row runs full width: on the left, the section title (e.g., 'This Week’s Menu') is aligned with the content column’s left edge; on the right, the planner summary card sits within the same horizontal row, giving the impression of a dual-column header. A 16px gap separates this header row from the first daily menu day block. Below the header, the daily blocks stack vertically with 16–24px spacing between them, allowing most days to be visible without heavy scrolling. The overall weekly menu area reads as one continuous vertical band of information, similar in weight and size to the event details area in the mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard flowing section within the content column. Its height increases with the number of daily blocks, pushing content (like the Families links) down accordingly; it does not use internal scrolling regions.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each daily menu day block is a sub-section within the weekly menu, occupying the full width of the content column from left to right, with 16–24px of vertical spacing separating it from adjacent day blocks. The block has a slightly emphasized top (e.g., day name and date in bold) aligned flush left, with 8–12px padding above and below the day header text. Inside each block, entrée items are listed in rows. Each entrée row typically runs the full width: entrée name and details (including small dietary tags or icons) are aligned to the left, while the 'Add to Planner' action sits on the far right of the same row, maintaining a strong left-to-right reading line. There is 8–12px vertical spacing between entrée rows. Overall, a day block’s height is compact but sufficient to clearly separate different entrées; the blocks create a consistent rhythm down the page, akin to stacked information panels in the mockup’s content area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a standard stacked card within the weekly menu section—no sticky behavior or independent scrolling. The block width and alignment stay locked to the content column grid.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The add-to-meal-planner handler manifests as the call-to-action area within each entrée row inside a daily menu day block. Visually, this is a right-aligned button or small button cluster at the far right of the entrée row, aligned horizontally with the entrée name on the left. The action zone occupies approximately 15–20% of the row width, with the remaining 80–85% used for entrée details. Horizontal padding of about 16px separates the button from the row’s right edge, while 8–12px of vertical padding ensures the button feels tappable without dominating the row. The consistent positioning of this action at the right edge across all entrée rows reinforces the F-pattern: users scan entrée details from left to right and naturally encounter the action at the row’s end.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The handler is embedded inline within entrée rows and follows the document flow. On very narrow widths it may wrap onto a second line beneath the entrée text but remains right-aligned relative to the row’s text block; there is no floating or sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The planner summary appears as a compact, right-aligned card at the top of the weekly menu section, sharing a horizontal row with the 'This Week’s Menu' heading. It is positioned on the right half of that row, starting roughly at the midpoint of the content column and extending to the right edge, occupying about 35–45% of the column width. The card has 16–24px internal padding and a subtle border or light background to differentiate it from the surrounding white, in line with the conservative, flat style. Internally, it lists a concise summary: number of planned lunches, key dietary notes, and a brief caption about the current week. The top of the card aligns vertically with the menu section title on the left, and its bottom edge is slightly taller than the title line, but not so tall that it feels like a sidebar. A 16px gap separates the bottom of this header row (title + card) from the first daily menu day block beneath it.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard inline card within the weekly menu header row. It scrolls naturally with the page content and is not sticky. On narrow layouts, it can drop below the section title while remaining right-aligned within the content column, preserving the hierarchy but stacking vertically.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The 'Open week in Meal Planner' link is placed inside the planner summary card, typically aligned to the bottom-right corner of the card’s content area. It appears as a textual link or subtle button with clear affordance but low visual weight compared to the main controls at the top of the page. There is 8–12px of padding between this link and the card’s right and bottom edges. Its position allows users to read the summary (top and middle of the card) and then notice the link as a secondary action. The link aligns horizontally with other text elements in the card, maintaining the ordered appearance of the institutional style.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline within the planner summary card; it follows the card’s positioning and scroll behavior. No sticky or floating behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The Families links section sits at the bottom of the page’s main content column, below the weekly menu. It begins after a generous 32px vertical gap from the last daily menu day block, clearly signaling a transition from functional data to related navigation. The section spans the full width of the content column and may start with a small heading such as 'For Families' aligned to the left, followed by one or two columns of simple text links (e.g., to forms, policies, or other family resources). Links are arranged in a list format with 8–12px vertical spacing between each item. The entire section reads as a low-profile but clear navigation band, echoing the mockup’s pattern of simple text-based secondary navigation grouped at the bottom.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard bottom-of-content section; it scrolls into view after the weekly menu. No sticky behavior or overlays.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The URL state manager has no direct visual representation but influences how visible components appear on load and on interaction. When a user selects a different week or applies dietary filters, the URL state manager updates the page’s query parameters. Visually, this means that when a user returns to or shares the link, the week selector, dietary filter controls, weekly menu, and planner summary all initialize in a state that matches the URL parameters. The content column does not change its layout; only the data displayed within the existing components is updated based on the state.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual/background. It does not occupy space, have position, or affect spatial flow beyond triggering updates within existing components in their defined positions.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The error and loading handler manifests as transient overlays or banners within the main content column. During initial data loading or when switching weeks, a semi-transparent overlay may cover only the weekly menu section, with a centered spinner or 'Loading lunch menu…' message, aligned horizontally in the center of the content column and vertically within the visible portion of the weekly menu area. This preserves the overall page structure while clearly indicating a temporary state. For errors (e.g., failed data load), a full-width alert banner appears directly below the 'Lunch Menu' H1 and intro text but above the week selector/filters row. The banner spans the entire content column, with 12–16px of vertical padding and 16–24px horizontal padding, and uses a high-contrast but conservative color (e.g., muted red) consistent with an institutional alert style. When present, it pushes the control row and everything below it downward, maintaining the linear vertical flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Transient overlay and inline banner behavior. The loading overlay is positioned relative to the weekly menu section and scrolls with it; when active, it visually sits above the menu content but does not shift layout underneath. The error banner is an inline block that occupies vertical space and participates in the normal document flow when visible.

name: Meal Planner
title: Meal Planner
description: Shows a student’s planned school lunches for a selected week, lets parents see which days have meals planned, filter by dietary needs, remove items, and navigate back to the Lunch Menu for the same week.

core_features: Load list of available lunch menu weeks and let the user select which week’s meal plan to view, using getMenuWeeks.
Given a selected menuWeekId, load the corresponding Meal Planner data (meal_plan_week and entries grouped by day) using getMealPlanForWeek.
Display the selected week’s meal plan as a day-by-day list/table of planned lunch items, including indicators for vegetarian and nut-free meals based on the entry flags.
Allow users to filter the visible planned meals by dietary properties (e.g., show only vegetarian, only nut-free, or all) using client-side filtering of the loaded entries.
Allow users to remove individual planned meals from specific days, calling removeMealFromPlanner and updating the displayed plan on success.
Provide a "Back to Lunch Menu" navigation link that preserves the currently selected week by passing menuWeekId to lunch_menu.html.
Handle empty states (no weeks, no plan for the selected week, or days without entries) and loading/error feedback for each interface call.
user_workflows:
View planner for a week from Lunch Menu:
1) User clicks a link/button on lunch_menu.html that navigates to meal_planner.html with a menuWeekId URL parameter.
2) Meal Planner reads menuWeekId from the URL.
3) Page calls getMenuWeeks to load all available weeks and highlights the week matching menuWeekId in the week selector.
4) Page calls getMealPlanForWeek(menuWeekId) and displays the returned meal_plan_week and entries_by_day in the planner grid.
5) User reviews which days have meals planned, with vegetarian/nut-free indicators.

Change the week being viewed:
1) User opens the week selector (e.g., dropdown) and chooses a different week.
2) Week selector updates the URL’s menuWeekId parameter to the chosen week’s id (e.g., meal_planner.html?menuWeekId=<selectedId>) using navigation or history replacement.
3) Page reacts to the changed menuWeekId, calls getMealPlanForWeek with the new id, and refreshes the planner grid for that week.
4) The Back to Lunch Menu link dynamically updates to point to lunch_menu.html?menuWeekId=<selectedId>.

Filter planned meals by dietary need:
1) After the planner for a week is loaded, user clicks filter controls (e.g., checkboxes) such as "Show only vegetarian" or "Show only nut-free".
2) Filter component applies client-side filtering to entries_by_day based on each entry’s is_vegetarian and is_nut_free flags.
3) Meal planner grid re-renders to show only entries matching the active filters, without making additional API calls.
4) User can clear filters to see all planned meals again.

Remove a planned meal from a day:
1) In the planner grid, user selects a specific planned meal entry and clicks a "Remove" action for that item.
2) Component calls removeMealFromPlanner with the selected entry’s meal_plan_entry_id.
3) While the call is in progress, the UI shows a loading or disabled state for that entry.
4) If the call returns success=true, the entry is removed from the in-memory entries_by_day state and the grid updates to reflect the change.
5) If success=false or the call fails, the grid restores the entry and shows an error message to the user without reloading the entire page.

Go back to Lunch Menu for the same week:
1) User clicks the "Back to Lunch Menu" link visible on the planner page.
2) The link uses the currently selected menuWeekId (from the selected week in the planner) to build the URL lunch_menu.html?menuWeekId=<currentMealPlanWeekMenuWeekId>.
3) Browser navigates to lunch_menu.html with that parameter so the Lunch Menu page can show the corresponding week.
interactions: On initial page load, read URL parameter menuWeekId (if present) and trigger getMenuWeeks followed by getMealPlanForWeek for the appropriate week.
On change of the week selector control, update the menuWeekId URL parameter and reload the meal plan for the selected week using getMealPlanForWeek.
On click of filter controls (e.g., vegetarian or nut-free toggles), adjust the client-side filtered view of entries_by_day without calling any interface.
On click of a meal entry’s "Remove" button, call removeMealFromPlanner(mealPlanEntryId) and, on success, remove that entry from the local entries_by_day structure.
On click of the "Back to Lunch Menu" link, navigate to lunch_menu.html with the current menuWeekId passed as a query parameter.
On interface errors (getMenuWeeks, getMealPlanForWeek, removeMealFromPlanner), display non-technical messages and present retry options where applicable.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary state driver is the menuWeekId URL query parameter. On page load, the Meal Planner parses menuWeekId from the URL. The page always calls getMenuWeeks first to obtain the full list of available weeks. If menuWeekId is present and matches an id from getMenuWeeks, that id becomes the selected week. If menuWeekId is missing or does not match any returned week, the page selects a default week based on application rules (e.g., the first item from getMenuWeeks or another externally defined rule) and may update the URL accordingly. After a week is selected, the page calls getMealPlanForWeek(selectedMenuWeekId) to load the meal_plan_week and entries_by_day for that week. The selectedMenuWeekId is stored in page state while the user remains on the page, and is kept in sync with the menuWeekId URL parameter whenever the user changes the week through the UI. The "Back to Lunch Menu" link always uses the currently selectedMenuWeekId (or meal_plan_week.menu_week_id when available) to construct lunch_menu.html?menuWeekId=selectedMenuWeekId. Dietary filter choices (vegetarian/nut-free) and transient UI states (loading flags, error flags, active day sections) are maintained in in-memory page/component state only and are not persisted to URL parameters; they are reset when the page is reloaded or navigated to via a new URL.

components.functionality: Coordinates initial and subsequent data loading for the Meal Planner. On page load or when menuWeekId changes, it fetches available weeks and the meal plan for the selected week, then distributes this data to child components.
event_handlers: onInit: Read menuWeekId from URL, call getMenuWeeks, determine selectedMenuWeekId, then call getMealPlanForWeek(selectedMenuWeekId).
onMenuWeekIdChange: When the URL’s menuWeekId value changes (via navigation or week selector), call getMealPlanForWeek(newMenuWeekId) and update shared planner state.
onReloadRequested: When a child component requests a reload (e.g., after an error), re-invoke getMenuWeeks and/or getMealPlanForWeek with the current selectedMenuWeekId.

components.functionality: Allows the user to choose which lunch menu week’s meal plan is displayed. Shows a list of weeks retrieved from getMenuWeeks and indicates the currently selected week.
event_handlers: onOptionsLoaded: Receive the array from getMenuWeeks and map each item’s id and label to option values in the selector.
onSelectionChange: When a user selects a different week, update the browser URL query parameter menuWeekId to the selected MenuWeek.id and notify meal-planner-data-loader that the week has changed.
onSyncWithUrl: When the component initializes, read the current selectedMenuWeekId from shared state (derived from URL) and mark that option as selected in the UI.

components.functionality: Displays summary information for the currently selected meal plan week, such as the week label and date range, and provides quick context about which week is being viewed.
event_handlers: onMealPlanLoaded: When getMealPlanForWeek returns meal_plan_week, render its label and date range fields in the summary area.
onWeekChange: When the selected week changes (triggering a new getMealPlanForWeek call), update the displayed summary information accordingly.

components.functionality: Provides interactive controls to filter visible planned meals by dietary properties (such as vegetarian-only or nut-free-only) based on flags stored in MealPlanEntry.
event_handlers: onInitFilters: Initialize filter state (e.g., show all meals) when entries_by_day are first loaded.

onToggleVegetarianFilter: When the user toggles a vegetarian filter control, update filter state and emit a filter criteria object to the meal-plan-grid component (e.g., { vegetarianOnly: true/false }).
onToggleNutFreeFilter: When the user toggles a nut-free filter control, update filter state and emit updated criteria (e.g., { nutFreeOnly: true/false }).
onMealPlanReloaded: When getMealPlanForWeek loads a new set of entries_by_day, reset or reapply filters to the new dataset and notify meal-plan-grid to re-render.

components.functionality: Displays the planned meals for the selected week, grouped by date. For each day, shows the date and a list of planned meals with their name snapshot and dietary indicators. Applies active filters from meal-filter-controls and supports removing individual meals.
event_handlers: onMealPlanLoaded: Accept the entries_by_day array from getMealPlanForWeek and construct an internal representation grouped by date for display.
onFilterCriteriaChange: Receive filter criteria from meal-filter-controls and re-filter the entries displayed for each date based on is_vegetarian and is_nut_free values.
onRemoveEntryClick: When the user clicks remove on a specific entry, emit an event with that entry’s meal_plan_entry_id to the meal-remove-handler component.
onEntryRemoved: After a successful removal, update internal data to remove the entry from its corresponding date group and re-render; handle the case where a date group becomes empty.
onEmptyState: When entries_by_day is empty or all entries are filtered out, display an appropriate empty-state message without calling additional interfaces.

components.functionality: Handles the removal of individual meal plan entries by calling removeMealFromPlanner and coordinating UI updates or error handling in the grid.
event_handlers: onRemoveEntryRequest: Receive meal_plan_entry_id from meal-plan-grid when a user initiates a removal; call removeMealFromPlanner(mealPlanEntryId).
onRemoveSuccess: When removeMealFromPlanner returns success=true, notify meal-plan-grid to remove the entry with that id from its displayed data.

onRemoveFailure: When removeMealFromPlanner returns success=false or an error occurs, notify meal-plan-grid to restore or keep the entry visible and display an error message associated with that entry.
onRemoveInProgress: While the API call is pending, emit a status to meal-plan-grid so it can disable the specific remove button or show a loading indicator for that entry.

components.functionality: Provides navigation from the Meal Planner back to the Lunch Menu, preserving the currently selected week via URL parameter.
event_handlers: onMealPlanLoaded: After getMealPlanForWeek returns, read meal_plan_week.menu_week_id (or fallback to the selectedMenuWeekId from week-selector) and construct the href lunch_menu.html?menuWeekId=<id>.
onWeekChange: When the selected week changes via the week-selector, update the link’s href to use the new selectedMenuWeekId.
onClick: Standard navigation; no special handler needed other than ensuring the link uses the current menuWeekId in its query string.

components.functionality: Centralizes the display of loading, empty, and error messages for the Meal Planner’s data operations.
event_handlers: onWeeksLoading: When getMenuWeeks is in progress, show a global loading indicator and temporarily disable planner interactions.
onWeeksLoadError: If getMenuWeeks fails, display an error message and a retry action that triggers getMenuWeeks again.
onMealPlanLoading: When getMealPlanForWeek is in progress for the selected week, show a loading indicator in the planner area.
onMealPlanLoadError: If getMealPlanForWeek fails, show an error message with a retry option that recalls getMealPlanForWeek for the current selectedMenuWeekId.
onNoWeeksAvailable: If getMenuWeeks returns an empty list, show a message explaining that no menu weeks are available and hide week-dependent controls.

onNoEntriesForWeek: If getMealPlanForWeek returns a meal_plan_week but entries_by_day is empty or every day has an empty entries array, show an informational message indicating that there are no planned meals for this week and suggest using the Lunch Menu to add meals.

layout.grid_system: Centered fixed-width content column likely based on a 12-column grid (~1080–1200px wide) within full-bleed horizontal bands.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the same linear, top-to-bottom single-column structure as the mockup. Within the main content column (centered within the page), elements are stacked in this order: breadcrumb (existing pattern), large H1 'Meal Planner', then the week-selector as the first functional control, followed by a compact summary header row (with the back-to-lunch-menu link right-aligned in that row), then the filter controls as a horizontal strip, and finally the meal-plan grid occupying the main body of the page. Supporting behaviors (data loading, remove actions, empty/error states) either overlay or replace the grid area, but do not break the overall single-column flow. No sidebars are introduced; all secondary elements (links, smaller labels) are placed inline and right-aligned within existing horizontal rows, echoing how small imagery is treated in the mockup.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the same linear, top-to-bottom single-column structure as the mockup. Within the main content column (centered within the page), elements are stacked in this order: breadcrumb (existing pattern), large H1 'Meal Planner', then the week-selector as the first functional control, followed by a compact summary header row (with the back-to-lunch-menu link right-aligned in that row), then the filter controls as a horizontal strip, and finally the meal-plan grid occupying the main body of the page. Supporting behaviors (data loading, remove actions, empty/error states) either overlay or replace the grid area, but do not break the overall single-column flow. No sidebars are introduced; all secondary elements (links, smaller labels) are placed inline and right-aligned within existing horizontal rows, echoing how small imagery is treated in the mockup.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Consistent with the mockup, the persistent site header, nav, and banner consume the top ~40% of the viewport. The 'Meal Planner' content occupies the remaining ~60%, constrained to a centered content column of about 1080–1200px. Within that column, controls and text typically span 70–90% of the width, aligned left. Vertical separation between major functional blocks (H1, week-selector, summary header, filter bar, grid) uses 24–32px spacing, while items within those blocks (labels, inputs, buttons, icons) use tighter 8–16px spacing, matching the 8px spacing system. The meal-plan grid is the dominant visual block and takes up most of the vertical space below the fold, with the filters and summary area being thinner bands above it.
layout_strategies.content_density: The content density stays moderate: stacked but not cramped. The top of the content area (H1, week selector, summary header) is slightly denser to keep key context and navigation above the fold, similar to the header stack in the mockup. Line spacing for labels, summaries, and grid text is comfortable but compact enough to allow a full school week of meals to be visible without excessive scrolling. Controls (dropdowns, filter pills, buttons) use 8–12px vertical padding and 12–16px horizontal padding. The grid cells are reasonably tight to fit multiple meal items per day but maintain at least 8px internal padding so the layout feels orderly and institutional rather than crowded.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow respects the F-pattern: the eye starts at the breadcrumb and H1 on the top left of the content column, then moves right to the back-to-lunch-menu link within the same or adjacent row (mirroring the right-aligned logo treatment in the mockup). From there, attention drops down to the week selector controls aligned on the left, continues downward to the summary header, then to the horizontal filter band spanning left to right. Finally, the eye moves vertically through the meal-plan grid, scanning days from left to right and meals top to bottom. When active, loader and error/empty states take over the central grid area and become the primary focal point, but are still centered within the same content column so the flow remains predictable.

layout.overall_description: Below the existing institutional header, navigation, and tabs, the main content column for the 'Meal Planner' page appears centered on a light background, about 1100px wide. At the very top of this column you see the breadcrumb in small text, then a large, prominent 'Meal Planner' H1 aligned left, occupying roughly the left 70% of the column. On the same horizontal line, or slightly offset within the same band, the 'Back to Lunch Menu' link sits right-aligned, visually lighter but clearly accessible, echoing the mockup’s pattern of a small right-aligned element beside the main heading. A comfortable 24px gap separates the heading area from the functional tools. Immediately below, the week selector row stretches across most of the column, with week navigation controls and a week label left-aligned, making it the first interactive focal point. Another 16–24px gap leads into a summary header row that concisely describes the student’s planned meals for the selected week, again aligned left within the same column. Beneath this, a subtle yet full-width filter strip presents dietary filters horizontally, giving the sense of a secondary navigation bar. The main body of the page is occupied by a structured meal-plan grid: a five-column (Mon–Fri) table-like layout with flat, bordered cells, using strong blue accents for headers and active states. Remove actions live unobtrusively inside these cells as small right-aligned icons or links. When data is loading or when no meals exist, a centered message or spinner appears in the same grid area, keeping the overall structure intact. The entire page feels ordered, predictable, and institutional, mirroring the mockup’s stacked horizontal bands and F-shaped reading pattern while focusing on practical readability over decoration.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The data loader is not a persistent visual block in the normal flow; instead, when active, it overlays the main content area for the Meal Planner within the centered content column. Vertically, the overlay begins just below the page H1 and any inline back link, and extends down to cover the week selector, summary header, filter controls, and meal-plan grid regions. Horizontally, it aligns with the content column edges, spanning roughly 80–90% of the total page width and centered. At the center of this overlay, a spinner or progress indicator appears, accompanied (optionally) by a brief 'Loading meal plan…' message in medium-weight text. The background of the overlay is a semi-opaque light tint, dimming the underlying controls but still subtly visible, consistent with a conservative institutional feel. The vertical and horizontal centering of the loader content ensures it becomes the immediate focus without introducing new structural bands.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a temporary, full-column overlay positioned above the normal document flow, fixed relative to the content column (not the entire viewport), and dismissed once data is loaded.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The week selector sits directly below the page H1 and inline back link, separated by approximately 24px of vertical space. It forms a single horizontal row aligned with the left edge of the main content column and spans about 70% of the column width. On the far left, there is a bold label such as 'Week of' followed by a date range; immediately adjacent to the label is a central control group consisting of left/right arrow buttons and a week dropdown or date-picker, arranged left-to-right in a line. Each control uses 8–12px vertical padding and 12–16px horizontal padding, with about 8px gaps between controls. The rightmost 30% of this row is left mostly empty to preserve the simple, flat aesthetic and maintain breathing room between this functional band and the back-to-lunch-menu link above/right, reinforcing the single-column composition and F-pattern scan from left to right.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, inline block in the normal vertical flow; it scrolls with the page and does not stick to the top.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The meal-plan summary header appears as a compact horizontal band directly below the week selector, with a vertical gap of about 16px. It aligns with the same left and right margins as the week selector and spans roughly 80–90% of the content column. Within this band, the left two-thirds contain concise, high-level information such as the student’s name and a count of planned meals ('5 of 5 days planned'), laid out as one or two lines of text with clear typographic hierarchy (slightly larger label on the first line, supporting text below). On the right-hand third of this band, horizontally aligned with the main text baseline, sits the 'Back to Lunch Menu' navigation link, right-aligned to the column edge. This mirrors the mockup’s design where a small logo or element sits to the right of a key heading, giving the row a balanced, institutional look. The band itself has about 8–12px of vertical padding to keep it compact yet readable.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, inline band; it acts as a contextual summary and alignment row for the back link, remaining in the normal scroll flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The meal filter controls form a low-height horizontal strip beneath the summary header, separated by about 24px of vertical space to signal a new functional block similar to a secondary navigation bar. This strip spans nearly the full width of the content column (around 90%), aligned left, with 16px padding on all sides. Inside, filter options (e.g., checkboxes, pill-shaped toggles for dietary needs) are arranged left-to-right in a single row on larger screens, wrapping to a second line if necessary. Each pill or checkbox-label pair uses 8px vertical and 12–16px horizontal padding, and 8–12px gaps between items. The leftmost edge starts with a 'Filter by:' label in small caps or a subtle weight, grounding the controls. The right side of the strip is left mostly open to avoid visual clutter. The flat styling and subtle bottom border or slight background tint echo the gray tabbed navigation and band structure in the mockup without introducing heavy card visuals.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, inline band; it grows vertically if filters wrap, but does not overlap or float, simply pushing the grid further down when more filters are present.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The meal-plan grid occupies the main body of the page and sits directly below the filter controls, separated by approximately 24–32px to clearly distinguish the control band from the data display, consistent with the mockup’s generous spacing between major horizontal sections. The grid spans the full width of the content column (roughly 100% of that center column, still about 70–80% of the overall page width), with a flat table-like appearance. At the top of the grid, a header row uses strong blue backgrounds or blue underlines for each weekday column label (Mon–Fri), with white or dark text for high contrast. Below, rows represent meal slots (e.g., 'Main', 'Side', 'Snack') or separate meal entries. Each cell has around 12–16px internal padding and thin, light borders separating days and rows to maintain an orderly institutional feel. Meal items inside a cell are stacked top-to-bottom with 4–8px spacing between lines; any remove action is positioned consistently at the far right within the cell (typically as a small 'Remove' link or icon), so the text aligns to the left and actions to the right. The grid height will vary based on the number of meals but is designed to show the full week at once, emphasizing information density without feeling cramped.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, block-level component that anchors the lower half of the page; it expands vertically with content but remains within the content column, scrolling normally.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The meal-remove handler does not appear as a standalone section; instead, its visual representation is embedded directly within each relevant cell of the meal-plan grid. For every planned meal entry, a small, right-aligned action appears on the same horizontal line as the meal name—either a minimalist text link ('Remove') or a simple icon with an accessible label. The action aligns flush with the right inner edge of the grid cell, using about 4–8px of horizontal spacing from the meal name and 4–8px of vertical padding above and below to maintain tap target sizing without visually competing with the meal label. In cells with multiple items, remove controls stack vertically in line with each corresponding meal text, creating a tidy left-text/right-action pattern. The minimal visual weight (standard text color or a slightly muted tone, no large buttons) keeps these controls tertiary in appearance yet consistently discoverable at the end of the F-pattern line scan in each row.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline within grid cells; the remove controls flow with the cell content and do not affect the global layout, only the internal alignment within each cell.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The 'Back to Lunch Menu' navigation link is positioned within the same horizontal band as the meal-plan summary header, acting as a small but strategically placed navigation element. Horizontally, it is right-aligned to the content column’s right edge, mirroring the way a small school logo was right-aligned next to a heading in the mockup. Vertically, the link is aligned to the baseline of the main summary text on the left so that the row feels visually balanced. The link itself is styled as a simple text link (blue or underlined, consistent with site-wide link styles) with about 8–12px of padding or touch area, but no heavy button chrome, maintaining the conservative, flat aesthetic. This placement keeps the link visible above the fold, close to the contextual information about the current week, while the lower half of the page remains dedicated to the planner grid.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline within the summary header row; it scrolls with the content and does not stick to the viewport, acting as a conventional in-page navigation link.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Empty and error states occupy the same spatial footprint as the meal-plan grid and related controls, ensuring the page structure remains consistent. When there is no meal data for the selected week (empty state), the filters and week selector remain visible at the top, but the grid area is replaced by a centered message panel within the content column. This panel sits approximately 24px below the filter controls and spans 60–70% of the column width, centered horizontally. It uses a light background tint and thin border, with 16–24px of internal padding. At the top of the panel, a medium-weight heading such as 'No meals planned this week' appears, followed by a short explanatory paragraph and, optionally, a subtle call-to-action link to the Lunch Menu. For error states (e.g., failed data load), a similar panel appears in the same position but with a slightly stronger color accent (e.g., a red or amber border) and a concise error message plus a 'Try again' link or button. These panels push down any content that would normally sit where the grid goes, but do not alter the header, week selector, or filter band above, preserving the familiar vertical rhythm.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main flow but mutually exclusive with the grid: when an empty or error state is active, it replaces the grid content area. It does not overlay other elements, but instead occupies the same block-level space as the grid below the filter controls.

name: Bus Routes & Times
title: Bus Routes & Times
description: Search and filter school bus routes by street, grade range, and arrival time, then open a route to view detailed stop and time information.

core_features: Load available grade range, arrival time window, and sort options via getBusRouteFilterOptions and bind them to filter controls.
Accept an optional street name query to search bus routes via searchBusRoutes.
Filter search results by selected grade range and arrival time window using searchBusRoutes filters.
Sort search results by selected sort option (such as arrival time or route number) using searchBusRoutes sort_by.
Reflect and control all search state (query, gradeRange, arrivalWindow, sortBy) through URL parameters so searches are shareable and browser navigation-safe.
Display a list of bus routes returned by searchBusRoutes, including saved status, and allow navigation to a route’s detail page.
Indicate when no routes match the current search and filter criteria, with guidance to adjust filters.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1 – Find a route by street:
1) User opens bus_routes.html (possibly with no URL parameters).
2) Page initialization component reads URL parameters (query, gradeRange, arrivalWindow, sortBy).
3) Page calls getBusRouteFilterOptions to populate filter and sort controls.
4) Page calls searchBusRoutes with the current query (if any), filters (derived from gradeRange and arrivalWindow), and sort_by (from sortBy or default) to load results.
5) User types a street name into the search input.
6) User submits the form; the page updates the URL query parameter, preserving existing filter and sort parameters.
7) The page calls searchBusRoutes again with the updated query.
8) Matching routes appear in the results list; user clicks a route to navigate to route_detail.html with busRouteId set to the selected route’s id.

Workflow 2 – Filter by grade range and arrival window:
1) User arrives on bus_routes.html (directly, or from another page passing gradeRange and/or arrivalWindow in the URL).
2) Initialization logic maps gradeRange and arrivalWindow URL parameters to the selected values in filter controls.
3) Page calls searchBusRoutes with filters set based on those parameters.
4) User changes the grade range filter.
5) Filter component updates the gradeRange URL parameter, keeps other parameters unchanged, and triggers a new call to searchBusRoutes with updated filters.
6) User optionally adjusts the arrival time window filter; the arrivalWindow URL parameter is updated and searchBusRoutes is called again.
7) The results list updates to show only routes that match the new filters.

Workflow 3 – Change sorting and use browser navigation:
1) User performs a search using any combination of query and filters.
2) User selects a different sort option in the sort control.
3) Sort control updates the sortBy URL parameter and triggers searchBusRoutes with the new sort_by value.
4) User reviews the updated ordering of routes in the results list.
5) User presses the browser Back button; the URL reverts to the previous parameter set.
6) A listener detects the URL change, re-reads query, gradeRange, arrivalWindow, sortBy from the URL, and re-issues searchBusRoutes so the results and controls reflect the prior state.

Workflow 4 – Open a route’s detail page:
1) User sees a list of routes based on the current query, filters, and sort order.
2) User selects a specific route from the results list.
3) The results component navigates to route_detail.html with a URL parameter busRouteId equal to the selected route’s id.
4) route_detail.html can then use that busRouteId to show full stop and time information (handled on that page).
interactions: Typing into the street search field updates the local input state but does not trigger a search until the form is submitted.
Submitting the search form updates the query URL parameter and triggers a new searchBusRoutes call using the updated query and existing filter/sort parameters.
Changing the grade range control updates the gradeRange URL parameter and triggers a new searchBusRoutes call with filters.grade_range set accordingly.
Changing the arrival time window control updates the arrivalWindow URL parameter and triggers a new searchBusRoutes call with filters.arrival_window set accordingly.
Changing the sort control updates the sortBy URL parameter and triggers a new searchBusRoutes call with sort_by set to the selected option.
Clicking on a bus route in the results list navigates to route_detail.html with busRouteId equal to the clicked route’s id.
The page listens for URL changes (for example, via browser Back/Forward) and re-applies query, gradeRange, arrivalWindow, and sortBy to controls and to a new searchBusRoutes call.
If searchBusRoutes returns no results for the current state, the results area displays a no-results message and suggests modifying the search or filters.
page_functionality.state_logic: On initial load, the page reads query, gradeRange, arrivalWindow, and sortBy from the URL. These parameters define the initial state of the search input, filter controls, and sort control. If a parameter is missing, the corresponding control is initialized with a neutral or default state (for example, no grade filter, no arrival window filter, and a default sort derived from the first option returned by getBusRouteFilterOptions). After fetching filter metadata via getBusRouteFilterOptions, the page calls searchBusRoutes using: query (from URL or empty), filters (constructed from gradeRange and arrivalWindow URL parameters), and sort_by (from sortBy or default). Whenever the user submits the search form or changes a filter/sort control, the page updates the URL parameters to reflect the new state and then calls searchBusRoutes again with values derived solely from the URL. A listener for URL changes ensures that using browser navigation restores the corresponding state by re-reading the parameters and re-triggering searchBusRoutes. No persistent state is stored outside of the URL and in-memory component state needed to display the current search results.

components.functionality: Coordinates initial data loading and synchronization between URL parameters, filter options, and the initial search results.
event_handlers: onPageLoad: Read URL parameters (query, gradeRange, arrivalWindow, sortBy) and store them in page state.
onPageLoad: Call getBusRouteFilterOptions and populate grade range, arrival time window, and sort option controls.
onPageLoad: Determine default sortBy from URL or from the first sort option returned by getBusRouteFilterOptions if sortBy is not present.
onPageLoad: Construct filters object (grade_range from gradeRange, arrival_window from arrivalWindow) and call searchBusRoutes with { query, filters, sort_by }.
onUrlChange: Re-read query, gradeRange, arrivalWindow, sortBy from URL, update control states, reconstruct filters and sort_by, and call searchBusRoutes again to refresh results.

components.functionality: Provides a text input for street-based route search and submits the query to update URL state and trigger a search.
data_binding: searchBusRoutes (invoked after form submission via page logic)
BusRoute (results indirectly affected by query parameter)
event_handlers: onInputChange: Update local search input state to match the user’s text.

onSubmit: Prevent default form submission, update the query URL parameter based on current input, preserve gradeRange, arrivalWindow, and sortBy from the current URL, and trigger a searchBusRoutes call via shared search logic using the updated query.

components.functionality: Displays and manages grade range and arrival time window filters using options returned by getBusRouteFilterOptions, updating URL parameters and triggering searches when changed.
data_binding: BusRoute (results indirectly affected by filter changes)
event_handlers: onInit: Bind grade range and arrival time window controls to options from getBusRouteFilterOptions and set their selected values based on gradeRange and arrivalWindow URL parameters.

onGradeRangeChange: Update gradeRange URL parameter to the newly selected value, preserving query, arrivalWindow, and sortBy parameters, and trigger a new searchBusRoutes call with filters.grade_range set to this value.

onArrivalWindowChange: Update arrivalWindow URL parameter to the newly selected value, preserving query, gradeRange, and sortBy parameters, and trigger a new searchBusRoutes call with filters.arrival_window set to this value.

components.functionality: Allows the user to choose how routes are ordered (such as by arrival time or route number) using options from getBusRouteFilterOptions, and updates URL state and search results accordingly.
event_handlers: onInit: Populate the sort control with options from getBusRouteFilterOptions and set the selected value based on the sortBy URL parameter or a default derived from the metadata.
onSortChange: Update sortBy URL parameter to the new sort value, preserving query, gradeRange, and arrivalWindow parameters, and trigger a new searchBusRoutes call with sort_by set to this value.

components.functionality: Displays a concise summary of the current search state (e.g., number of results and whether filters are active) based on searchBusRoutes output and URL parameters.
data_binding: searchBusRoutes (array length and any relevant metadata available from results)
URL parameters (query, gradeRange, arrivalWindow, sortBy to describe current state)
event_handlers: onResultsUpdate: When new results are returned from searchBusRoutes, update the summary with the current result count and an indication of active filters.
onUrlChange: Recompute and update the summary text to stay in sync with the latest query and filter/sort parameters.

components.functionality: Renders the list of bus routes returned from searchBusRoutes, including saved status, and handles navigation to route_detail.html when a route is selected.
data_binding: searchBusRoutes (array of route result objects including id, route_number, name, description, grade_range, grade_range_label, arrival_time_at_school, school_name, served_streets, is_active, is_saved)
event_handlers: onResultsUpdate: Receive updated route array from searchBusRoutes and re-render the list.
onRouteClick: When a user clicks a route item, navigate to route_detail.html with the URL parameter busRouteId set to the clicked route’s id.
onRenderRouteItem: For each result, display saved/unsaved status based on the is_saved property as a read-only indicator (no write operations, since no saving interface is assigned).

components.functionality: Provides user feedback when no bus routes are returned for the current query and filters, suggesting adjustments.
data_binding: searchBusRoutes (used to detect when the result array is empty)
URL parameters (query, gradeRange, arrivalWindow to tailor the message to the current search state)
event_handlers: onResultsUpdate: If searchBusRoutes returns an empty array, display a no-results message; if results are non-empty, hide this message.
onFilterOrQueryChange: Update contextual wording (if needed) to reflect the most recent query or filter values from the URL.

layout.grid_system: Centered fixed-width content column likely based on a 12-column grid (~1080–1200px wide) within full-bleed horizontal bands.
layout.layout_pattern: All components sit within the main centered content column beneath the existing header, banner, and tab strip. The flow is strictly top-to-bottom in a single column: page title and intro (outside this scope), then the search form, then filter controls, then a compact bar with results summary on the left and sort control on the right, followed by the results list or the no-results message. Everything aligns to the left edge of the content column, with no sidebars; controls and lists stretch across most of the column width while still leaving comfortable side margins.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: All components sit within the main centered content column beneath the existing header, banner, and tab strip. The flow is strictly top-to-bottom in a single column: page title and intro (outside this scope), then the search form, then filter controls, then a compact bar with results summary on the left and sort control on the right, followed by the results list or the no-results message. Everything aligns to the left edge of the content column, with no sidebars; controls and lists stretch across most of the column width while still leaving comfortable side margins.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The global header, nav, and tabs occupy the top ~40% of the initial viewport, consistent with the mockup. Within the remaining ~60%, these components share the main content column (~1080–1200px wide, centered in the viewport). The search form and filters occupy roughly the upper half of the visible content area, with the results summary and list occupying the lower half. Individual components generally span 70–90% of the content column width, with 24–32px vertical separation between major sections and 8–16px between tightly related elements. The results list is the largest single block, consuming most of the vertical space once data is present.
layout_strategies.content_density: Density is moderate and practical. Controls are grouped tightly enough to keep the core search/filter/sort interactions visible without scrolling but still honor an 8px spacing rhythm for readability. The search form and filters are compact, with 8–12px internal padding and 8–16px gaps between fields. The area above the results list (summary + sort) is slightly denser to visually connect these controls to the list. The results list has comfortable 12–16px vertical spacing between items, avoiding a cramped table-like feel while keeping multiple routes visible at once.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow follows an F-pattern anchored by left-aligned text. The eye drops from the page title straight to the left-aligned search form label and primary input, then scans right across associated form fields and the search button. Next, attention moves down to the left-aligned filter controls, then to the results summary on the left and the sort control on the right, establishing a left-to-right scan line just above the results. From there, the eye moves vertically down through the results list. When no routes are found, the no-results message appears in place of the list and is centered horizontally but still anchored within the same content column to preserve the flow.

layout.overall_description: Within the established institutional frame of the site (utility bar, main navigation, banner, and tabs stacked at the top), the “Bus Routes & Times” content sits in a centered, fixed-width column that follows a disciplined, linear flow. Just beneath the page title and any brief intro text, a left-aligned search form stretches across roughly three-quarters of the column, presenting key inputs in a single, compact band. Directly below, a row of filter controls tightens in the layout, maintaining the same left alignment and moderate spacing. A little further down, a narrow horizontal strip holds the results summary on the left and a sort control on the right, forming a strong visual line that leads into the main content mass. Under this bar, the results list occupies the full width of the column, stacking bus route entries one after another with consistent rhythm and clear separation. If there are no routes, a centered feedback message appears in this same area, framed by generous vertical padding. Overall, the page feels orderly and utilitarian: horizontal bars of function stacked from top to bottom, clear typographic hierarchy, and a predictable F-shaped reading pattern that supports quick scanning of routes and times.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component operates entirely behind the scenes and does not render any visible UI. It sits conceptually at the very top of the main content column, initializing data and state before any visible components are shown. It occupies no space in the layout and does not affect spacing or alignment; the visual layout begins with the page title and then the search form as the first visible interactive area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual, zero-footprint logic layer; it does not participate in layout flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The search form is the first visible functional block in the main content area, placed directly beneath the page title and any short introductory text with approximately 24–32px of vertical gap (three to four 8px units). It is left-aligned within the centered content column and spans about 70–80% of that column’s width, leaving a stable right margin that keeps the layout from feeling overly wide. The form fields are arranged in a single horizontal band where possible: a prominent street/address input aligned to the left, followed by grade-range and arrival-time controls, and finally a primary “Search” button aligned toward the right edge of the form. The form’s content is enclosed by subtle vertical padding (12–16px top and bottom) and horizontal padding (16–24px) inside an invisible or very lightly outlined container, maintaining the site’s flat, institutional look. Vertically, the search form presents as a compact strip roughly 1.25–1.5 times the height of a standard nav bar, ensuring it feels important but not bulky. The bottom edge of the form sits about 16px above the filter controls, visually linking them as a single functional cluster while still separating them clearly.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level element that participates normally in the vertical document flow. On narrower screens, individual form fields wrap to the next line in logical order (street input first, then grade/arrival selectors, then the search button), but the component remains left-aligned and does not become full-bleed.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The filter controls sit immediately below the search form, separated by a modest 12–16px gap to indicate close functional relationship but distinct grouping. They are left-aligned with the search form’s left edge, preserving a clean vertical alignment line. This component spans roughly 80–90% of the content column width, slightly wider than the search form if necessary to accommodate multiple filters but still respecting side margins. Individual filter elements (e.g., checkboxes, additional dropdowns, or toggles) are laid out in a horizontal row where screen width allows, with 8–16px horizontal gaps between controls and 8px vertical padding above and below to keep the strip visually light. If the number of filters is large, they wrap to a second line, maintaining left alignment and equal spacing so the block reads as a compact cluster rather than a tall panel. The entire filter area is visually subordinate to the search form: it has slightly smaller labels and no heavy borders, presenting as a quiet, functional band. A slightly larger vertical gap of about 24px separates the bottom of the filter controls from the results summary/sort bar, signaling the transition from search/filter input to output.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level element flowing directly after the search form. On narrower viewports, filters gracefully wrap to multiple lines while maintaining left alignment and consistent 8px spacing. It does not stick or float; it scrolls away with the rest of the content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The sort control shares a horizontal row with the results summary, forming a slim bar just above the results list. This bar is positioned approximately 24px below the filter controls, spanning the full width of the content column. Within this bar, the sort control is right-aligned: it occupies the rightmost 20–30% of the width, sitting on the same baseline as the summary text on the left. The control itself is compact—a dropdown or segmented control with a label such as “Sort by”—with 8–12px internal horizontal padding and minimal vertical padding (about 8px top and bottom), matching the conservative style of the tabs and nav items from the mockup. There is a small horizontal gap (8–12px) between the label and the interactive control, so the whole sort area reads as a single unit. The presence of the sort control on the right side balances the left-heavy weight of the summary and subtly draws the eye across the top of the results, reinforcing the F-pattern scan line before the user moves downward into the list.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within its row, aligned to the right edge of the content column. On narrow screens, the horizontal bar becomes vertical: the results summary stacks on top and the sort control moves below it with an 8px vertical gap, both left-aligned to maintain coherence. The sort control does not stick to the top of the viewport; it scrolls away with the summary.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The results summary appears as a concise line of text in the same row as the sort control, directly above the results list. This strip sits roughly 24px below the filter controls, forming a clear separation between input controls and output data. The summary is left-aligned with the main content column and occupies approximately the left 60–70% of the row, leaving space on the right for the sort control. Typical content might read “12 routes found” or similar, presented in a regular-weight type slightly smaller than the H1 but larger than body text, ensuring quick readability. Vertical padding in this row is modest (8–12px top and bottom), making the bar feel like a slim informational header to the list rather than a heavy section divider. A subtle increase in line height and a bit of breathing room above the first result item (about 12–16px) ensures the summary is visually distinct from the list itself while clearly associated with it.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static text block that flows inline with the sort control in a shared row. On smaller viewports, the summary becomes the first item in a vertical stack, with the sort control shifting below it while preserving left alignment. It does not have any fixed or sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The results list occupies the largest continuous block of the page’s main content, beginning approximately 12–16px below the results summary/sort bar. It is left-aligned to the content column and spans nearly the full width (about 90–100% of the column, depending on design preference for side padding around list items). Each route entry is presented as a row or simple card stacked vertically, with 12–16px of vertical space between items and 16–24px of internal padding per item to keep the layout readable yet compact. The list maintains a flat, table-like feel without heavy drop shadows—relying instead on simple dividers or subtle alternating row backgrounds to separate items, echoing the conservative, institutional aesthetic. The list extends downward as far as needed, potentially occupying the entire lower portion of the viewport and beyond. At the bottom of the list, if pagination or a “load more” control exists, it is centered or left-aligned within the same width and separated from the last item by about 24px. This component visually anchors the page’s functional purpose, forming the main content block that users scroll through, framed above by the summary and sort strip and below by the page’s natural footer spacing.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, vertically scrolling list that grows with the number of results. It does not have fixed headers or pinned columns by default, though individual rows may be clickable. When there are no results, this component’s space is effectively replaced by the no-results message component in the same position, preserving overall layout structure.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The no-results message occupies the exact vertical slot where the results list would normally begin—immediately beneath the results summary/sort bar, with the same 12–16px top spacing. It is centered horizontally within the content column but constrained to about 60–70% of the column width so that the message remains compact and easy to read. The text is left-aligned within this narrower block, often accompanied by a short title line (e.g., “No routes found”) and a brief explanatory sentence or two. Vertical padding around the message is more generous than a standard list row—approximately 32–48px top and bottom—to give it a distinct, framed feel without using overt borders or illustrations, in keeping with the restrained aesthetic. If supplementary actions are provided (e.g., “Clear filters” link), they appear directly beneath the message text, left-aligned, with an 8–12px gap. Overall, the message feels calmly informative rather than alarming, sitting as a well-defined, self-contained panel in the space where results would appear.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Mutually exclusive with the results list: it appears only when there are no matching routes and otherwise takes up no space. When visible, it behaves as a standard block element in the vertical flow, pushing subsequent content (if any) downward. It does not stick or float and maintains its centered position within the main content column across viewport sizes.

name: Bus Route Detail
title: Bus Route Detail
description: Displays detailed information for a selected school bus route, including all stops, times, eligible grades, and an option to save the route to My Routes.

core_features: Read the busRouteId URL parameter and load the selected route’s details using getBusRouteDetail(busRouteId).
Display key route information, including route number, route name, school name, grade eligibility, arrival time at school, and any route notes.
Visually highlight the route’s arrival_time_at_school so parents can quickly confirm it meets their timing needs.
List all associated stops for the route (from getBusRouteDetail.stops), ordered by stop_order, showing stop name, street, optional cross street, pickup time, and optional dropoff time.
Indicate whether the route is currently saved to the user’s My Routes using the route.is_saved flag from getBusRouteDetail.
Allow the user to save the current route to My Routes by calling saveBusRouteToMyRoutes(busRouteId), and immediately reflect the saved state in the UI.
Provide a clear navigation link back to the Bus Routes & Times page (bus_routes.html).
user_workflows: View a route’s details: 1) User opens route_detail.html with a busRouteId URL parameter (from bus_routes.html or my_routes.html). 2) On page load, the page reads busRouteId from the URL. 3) The page calls getBusRouteDetail(busRouteId). 4) While data is loading, the page shows a loading state. 5) When data is returned, the page displays the route summary (route number, name, school, grades, arrival time, notes, served streets) and the ordered list of stops with times. 6) The arrival_time_at_school field is visually emphasized in the summary section so the user can confirm school arrival timing.

Save a route to My Routes: 1) After route details are loaded, the page checks route.is_saved. 2) If route.is_saved is false, a Save to My Routes action is available. 3) The user clicks the save action. 4) The page calls saveBusRouteToMyRoutes(busRouteId). 5) While waiting for the response, the save control enters a loading/disabled state. 6) On success (success = true), the page updates its local route state to treat the route as saved (equivalent to route.is_saved = true), and may update the URL (for example by adding a savedStatus=success parameter) to reflect that the save just occurred. 7) If the interface returns success = false, the page surfaces the message from the response and keeps the route as unsaved in the UI.

Navigate back to bus route search: 1) The user has finished reviewing the route details. 2) The user activates the Back to Bus Routes & Times navigation control. 3) The page navigates to bus_routes.html without requiring additional URL parameters, as defined in the outgoing navigation configuration.
interactions: On initial page load or when the busRouteId URL parameter changes, the page triggers getBusRouteDetail(busRouteId) to retrieve the route and stop data.
If the busRouteId URL parameter is missing or empty, the page shows an error state with guidance and a navigation link back to bus_routes.html, and does not call getBusRouteDetail.
If getBusRouteDetail returns route.is_active as false, the page displays the route details but also shows a status message indicating that the route is not currently active.

The user can click a Save to My Routes control when route.is_saved is false; this triggers saveBusRouteToMyRoutes(busRouteId) and, on success, disables or changes the save control to reflect that the route is now saved.
