If saving fails (success is false), the page shows an inline error message using the message returned by saveBusRouteToMyRoutes and re-enables the save control so the user can try again.
The user can click Back to Bus Routes & Times to navigate to bus_routes.html at any time, regardless of whether data has loaded successfully.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page reads the busRouteId URL parameter on load to determine which route to display. If busRouteId is present and non-empty, the page calls getBusRouteDetail(busRouteId) and stores the returned route and stops in page state. If busRouteId is missing or invalid, the page does not call getBusRouteDetail and instead displays an error state. The route.is_saved flag from getBusRouteDetail determines the initial state of the save control: if true, the control indicates that the route is already in My Routes and does not call saveBusRouteToMyRoutes; if false, the control is active. When the user initiates a save, the page calls saveBusRouteToMyRoutes(busRouteId); on success, the page updates its in-memory state to mark the route as saved and may optionally update the URL (for example, appending a savedStatus=success parameter) to allow the page to show a one-time saved confirmation on reload. All persistent state is derived from the backend interfaces and URL parameters only; no localStorage or other client-side persistence is used. Navigation away from the page (for example, via Back to Bus Routes & Times) simply changes the browser location to bus_routes.html, clearing the route_detail.html-specific state.

components.functionality: Initializes the page by reading the busRouteId URL parameter, validating it, and calling getBusRouteDetail(busRouteId). Manages loading, success, and error states, and passes the loaded route and stops data to child components.

Displays primary route information: route_number, name, grade_range_label, school_name, arrival_time_at_school, is_active status, notes, and served_streets. Emphasizes the arrival_time_at_school field so users can quickly verify the arrival time at school.

Provides a Save to My Routes action based on route.is_saved. If the route is not yet saved, it allows the user to save the route by calling saveBusRouteToMyRoutes(busRouteId). After a successful save, it updates the UI to indicate the saved state and prevents duplicate save attempts.

Displays the full list of stops for the current route in order of stop_order. Each row shows stop_name, street_name, cross_street (if available), pickup_time, and dropoff_time (if available). The table updates whenever new stop data is loaded.

Shows contextual messages related to loading, errors, route activity, and save operations. For example, it can display errors from getBusRouteDetail or saveBusRouteToMyRoutes, indicate that the route is inactive when route.is_active is false, or confirm that a route has been saved when a save operation succeeds.
Provides a Back to Bus Routes & Times control that navigates the user to bus_routes.html when activated.

layout.grid_system: Centered fixed-width content column likely based on a 12-column grid (~1080–1200px wide) within full-bleed horizontal bands.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the same single-column, top-to-bottom flow as the mockup. All bus route information is stacked within the main centered content column beneath the global header, banner, and tabs. Within this column, the in-page order is: a subtle back navigation link at the top left, then the route summary header (with route title and key facts) occupying the upper portion of the content area, with the save control aligned to the right in the same horizontal band. Directly below this, route status messages appear as a full-width panel, followed by the route stops table taking up the majority of the vertical space. The data loader is not part of the linear flow; instead, it overlays this entire main content area when loading.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the same single-column, top-to-bottom flow as the mockup. All bus route information is stacked within the main centered content column beneath the global header, banner, and tabs. Within this column, the in-page order is: a subtle back navigation link at the top left, then the route summary header (with route title and key facts) occupying the upper portion of the content area, with the save control aligned to the right in the same horizontal band. Directly below this, route status messages appear as a full-width panel, followed by the route stops table taking up the majority of the vertical space. The data loader is not part of the linear flow; instead, it overlays this entire main content area when loading.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The main content is centered in a fixed-width column (~1080–1200px) with generous vertical separation (~24–32px) between major blocks (back nav, summary header, status messages, table). The route summary header and save control together occupy roughly the top 20–25% of the main content area, the status messages a small band directly below, and the route stops table occupies the bulk of the remaining vertical space. Horizontal content (heading, bullets, table) generally uses about 70–80% of the width of the centered content column, with right-aligned items (like the save button) taking up the remaining 20–30%. Padding inside components follows the 8px base grid, with 8–16px internal padding.
layout_strategies.content_density: Density is moderate: headings and key facts are clearly separated but not overly spaced out. The summary header has comfortable line spacing and modest gaps between the title and bullet-like facts. The message panel is compact but readable, with 8–16px inner padding. The table rows are relatively tight to keep many stops visible at once, but each row still has enough vertical padding (~8–12px) for legibility. Overall, the content feels practical and informational, avoiding both cramped and overly airy layouts.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow retains an F-pattern. After the global header and tabs, the eye lands at the top-left of the main content on the back navigation link, then moves right across the route summary header to the save control on the right edge. From there, the eye drops down to the route status messages band spanning the content width, then continues vertically into the route stops table where the left-aligned stop information guides scanning down the column. When the data loader is active, it becomes the dominant focal element centered in the content column, temporarily overriding other visual priorities.

layout.overall_description: Below the site’s stacked header, banner, and tab bars, the main content column begins with a subtle back navigation link at the top left, aligned with the overall grid. A generous ~24–32px gap separates this link from the page framing above, marking the start of the Bus Route Detail content. Directly beneath, a strong route summary header anchors the page: on the left, a large, bold route title (e.g., "Route 12 – Fish Creek AM") spans about 70–75% of the column width, with any supporting key route facts arranged just below in a concise list. On the right, aligned with the baseline of the route title, a prominent "Save to My Routes" button sits in the remaining 25–30% of the row, visually mirroring the right-aligned logo behavior seen in the mockup. Below this header band, a full-width status message panel provides any alerts or important notes about the route. Beneath that, with a clear ~24px separation, a structured stops table occupies nearly the full width of the content column and extends vertically as the main data element, listing all stops and times in a tidy, grid-like format. When loading, a semi-opaque data loader overlays this entire central content block, dimming the underlying elements and centering a spinner or loading message, while the header bars and global navigation remain visible above.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The data loader appears as a semi-opaque overlay that sits on top of the entire main content column for the Bus Route Detail page, starting just below the tabbed navigation band and extending down to the bottom of the viewport or content, whichever is longer. Horizontally, it matches the fixed-width centered content container (approximately 1080–1200px wide) and is centered in the viewport, aligning with the route header and table beneath it. The background of the loader is a light, translucent white or very pale grey, slightly dimming the content below while keeping the overall page structure visible. In the center of this overlay, both horizontally and vertically, a compact loading indicator (spinner plus short text like "Loading route details…") is placed. This central loading cluster occupies a small area (roughly 20–25% of the content column width and minimal height) with 16–24px internal padding, following the base unit spacing. When active, it visually dominates the main content but does not cover the global header or tabs above.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Positioned as an absolutely or fixed-positioned overlay relative to the main content region; it covers all in-page components (back navigation, header, messages, table) while active, without affecting their layout, and scrolls with the page if the content is taller than the viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The route summary header is the first substantial content block in the main column, positioned directly below the back navigation link with approximately 16–24px of vertical spacing. It spans the full width of the centered content container but organizes its content into a left-dominant and right-supporting layout. On the left side (about 70–75% of the width), the route title is rendered as a large H1-style heading, aligned flush left with the content column. Below the title, within the same width, a short list of key route facts (e.g., service days, eligible grades, AM/PM, operator) is presented as a concise bullet list or stacked label-value rows, each line separated by 8–12px. On the right side (about 25–30% of the width), aligned vertically with the top of the route title, there is reserved space for the save control, which sits flush to the right edge of the content column. The overall block has 16–24px of top and bottom padding internally, keeping it visually distinct from the status messages below while maintaining the moderately dense feel of the mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level element in the normal document flow; it defines the main visual anchor for the page’s content region and adapts to content height while maintaining its left-right structure within the fixed-width center column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The route save control is a primary action button visually attached to the route summary header. It is positioned on the right side of the same horizontal row as the route title, aligning its top edge roughly with the first baseline of the H1 text. The button is right-aligned to the content column, leaving a small margin (16px) from the column’s right edge, and it occupies approximately 15–20% of the total content width, enough to accommodate a short label like "Save to My Routes" on a single line. Vertical padding inside the button follows the 8–12px range, with 16–24px of horizontal padding, giving it a compact but clearly tappable size. There is a subtle 8–12px horizontal gap between the right edge of the route title text area and the left edge of the button, ensuring the two elements feel related but not cramped. On narrower widths within the fixed column, if the title wraps to multiple lines, the button remains aligned to the top-right, maintaining its prominence. Visually, it mirrors the right-side emphasis seen with the school logo in the mockup header row.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, inline-block element within the route summary header; it participates in the header’s horizontal layout, staying docked to the right edge of the content column and moving down only if the header content wraps to multiple lines.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The route stops table is placed below the route status messages, separated by a clear 24px vertical gap. It occupies almost the full width of the centered content column (about 90–100% of the column), starting flush left with the route title and extending nearly to the right edge, with standard 16px inner padding from the table boundary to its content. The table header row is visually distinct, with a slightly stronger background or bottom border, defining columns for stop name, intersection, time, and any notes. Each data row has moderate vertical padding (8–12px) and tight but readable line spacing to present several stops on screen at once, reflecting the practical density of the mockup. Columns are left-aligned where text-heavy (stop names) and center- or right-aligned where numeric/time-based, maintaining a clear reading order down the left side of the table. The table extends vertically as the main body of the page, with a 24–32px margin at the bottom before any footer content begins, giving the entire center of the page a structured, data-focused appearance.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level element in the normal flow; it expands vertically with the number of stops and will be the primary scrollable content in the main column, while remaining anchored to the 12-column-like grid within the fixed-width container.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The route status messages panel sits directly below the route summary header, separated from it by approximately 16–24px of vertical space, and directly above the route stops table, with another 16–24px gap below. It spans the full width of the centered content column, aligning left and right with the route header and table. Internally, the panel has 12–16px of padding on all sides, with the message text left-aligned and wrapping within about 80–90% of the panel width to avoid overly long lines. If multiple messages are present, they are stacked vertically with 8–12px spacing between each. The panel may use a subtle colored border on the left edge and a light background to denote alert, info, or success states, but remains visually modest to fit the conservative style. Its height is adaptive based on message length, but it generally remains a low-profile band that does not dominate the page, instead acting as an important but secondary read after the route header.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level element in the normal flow, flexibly growing in height as needed; it does not stick or float, but maintains consistent vertical spacing between the header above and the table below.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The back navigation appears as a small, text-based link (potentially with a left-pointing arrow icon) at the very top of the main content area for this page. It is aligned to the left edge of the centered content column, starting approximately 24–32px below the bottom of the tabbed navigation bar, visually echoing the position of a breadcrumb in the mockup. The link itself is compact, with minimal horizontal intrusion—occupying only as much width as its label text requires—and a touch of vertical padding (4–8px) above and below to make it easily clickable without feeling like a button. There is a clear 8–12px gap between this back link and the start of the route summary header below, so it reads as a navigational meta-layer rather than part of the route data. It sets up the left-side anchor for the F-pattern scan, drawing the eye before the user moves down into the larger header content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static inline or inline-block element at the top of the content flow; it remains fixed in its position in the document and scrolls offscreen as the user scrolls down the page.

name: My Routes
title: My Routes
description: Page where families can view, open, and remove school bus routes they have saved for quick reference.

core_features: Retrieve and display the user’s saved bus routes using the getMyRoutes interface.
Show each saved route in a compact list with key information: route number, route name, primary streets, school name, arrival time at school, and when it was saved.
Allow users to open the full route detail page for any saved route by clicking on it, navigating to route_detail.html with the appropriate busRouteId URL parameter.
Allow users to remove a bus route from their saved list by calling the removeSavedRoute interface with the corresponding savedRouteId.
Provide optional client-side sorting of the saved routes (e.g., by arrival time, route number, or saved date) based on a URL query parameter.
Highlight a specific saved route (for example, one that was just saved from a route detail page) based on a URL query parameter.
user_workflows:
Viewing all saved routes:
1. User navigates to my_routes.html from the site header or a confirmation link on another page.
2. On page load, a state manager parses URL parameters (e.g., sort, highlightBusRouteId).
3. The data loader component calls getMyRoutes with no parameters.
4. While loading, the page shows a loading indicator.
5. When getMyRoutes returns, the list component receives the array of saved routes and the current sort preference.
6. The list component sorts the routes according to the sort URL parameter and renders each as a row.
7. If a highlightBusRouteId URL parameter matches one of the returned bus_route_id values, that route row is visually highlighted via component state.

Opening a route’s detail page:
1. User reviews the list of saved routes.
2. User clicks anywhere in a route row’s main clickable area (excluding the remove control).
3. The row’s click handler reads that row’s bus_route_id from the getMyRoutes data.
4. The browser navigates to route_detail.html with a URL such as route_detail.html?busRouteId={bus_route_id_from_row}.
5. The route detail page uses its own logic to display full route information.

Removing a saved route from My Routes:
1. User locates a route they no longer need.
2. User clicks the remove control on that route’s row.
3. The click handler opens a confirmation modal and stores the selected saved_route_id in page state.
4. If the user cancels, the modal closes and no changes are made.
5. If the user confirms, the confirmation modal’s handler calls removeSavedRoute(savedRouteId={selected_saved_route_id}).
6. While the request is in progress, the modal shows a busy state to prevent duplicate submissions.
7. On success (success=true in the response), the route is removed from the in-memory list of saved routes, the modal closes, and a success notification is shown using the message returned by removeSavedRoute (or a generic fallback if no message is provided).
8. If the removal results in an empty list, the list component switches to an empty-state view.
9. On failure (success=false or network error), the modal or notification component shows an error message and leaves the list unchanged.

Arriving on My Routes after saving a route elsewhere:
1. On another page (for example, a route detail page), the user saves a route.
2. That page shows a confirmation message with a link to my_routes.html, optionally including a query parameter such as highlightBusRouteId={bus_route_id_just_saved}.
3. User clicks this link and navigates to my_routes.html?highlightBusRouteId={id}.
4. On load, the state manager reads the highlightBusRouteId value from the URL and stores it in page state.
5. After getMyRoutes completes, the list component compares each route’s bus_route_id to the highlightBusRouteId.
6. The matching route row is rendered with a highlight state, helping the user find the newly saved route quickly.

Changing sort order of saved routes:
1. User views the My Routes page and notices a sort control (for example, a dropdown) in the toolbar.
2. User selects a different sort option (e.g., by arrival time, by route number, by saved-at timestamp).
3. The toolbar component updates the URL query parameter sort to the chosen value without reloading the page (or triggers a reload that preserves this parameter, depending on implementation).
4. The state manager detects the updated sort parameter and updates the current sort setting in page state.
5. The list component re-sorts the in-memory array of routes received from getMyRoutes according to the new sort setting and re-renders the list.
6. The current sort setting is always derived from the URL, so reloading or sharing the URL preserves the sort order.
interactions: Page load interaction: When my_routes.html loads, onPageLoad triggers URL parsing (e.g., reading sort and highlightBusRouteId), then triggers the getMyRoutes interface call. The page shows a loading indicator until data is available or an error occurs.
Route row click: Clicking on a route row’s main area triggers onRouteClick, which reads the route’s bus_route_id from the bound data and navigates to route_detail.html?busRouteId={bus_route_id}.
Remove button click: Clicking a route’s remove control triggers onRemoveClick, which opens the confirmation modal and sets the currently selected savedRouteId in the shared page state.

Removal confirmation: Clicking the confirm button in the confirmation modal triggers onConfirmRemove, which calls removeSavedRoute(savedRouteId) using the stored value. The response is handled to update the local list and show a success or error notification.
Removal cancellation: Clicking a cancel/close control in the confirmation modal triggers onCancelRemove, which closes the modal and clears the pending savedRouteId from state.
Sort selection change: Changing the sort control in the toolbar triggers onSortChange, which updates the sort query parameter in the URL and re-sorts the displayed routes accordingly.
Notification dismissal: Clicking a close control on a notification banner triggers onDismissNotification, which hides the notification and clears any related message from page state.
Retry after error: If getMyRoutes fails, a retry control in the error state triggers onRetryLoad, which calls getMyRoutes again and re-renders the list based on the new response.
page_functionality.state_logic: The My Routes page uses URL query parameters to control and preserve view-related state, without relying on localStorage or other persistent client storage. The primary URL parameters are: (1) sort: determines how the saved routes are ordered in the list. Valid values are interpreted by the client (for example, arrival_time, route_number, or saved_at). The state manager reads this parameter on page load and on URL changes. The list component sorts the routes from getMyRoutes according to this value. When the user changes the sort via the toolbar, the toolbar updates the sort parameter in the URL, ensuring that the current sort state is shareable and survives reload. (2) highlightBusRouteId: optionally indicates a specific bus_route_id that should be visually highlighted in the list. The state manager reads this parameter on load and stores it in state. After getMyRoutes returns, the list or row components compare each item’s bus_route_id with highlightBusRouteId to determine which row, if any, should be highlighted. This parameter is view-only: it does not affect what data is requested, only how it is presented. Additional transient state such as the loading status, error messages, the array of routes returned from getMyRoutes, and the currently selected savedRouteId for removal are maintained in in-memory page state (for example, through a central state manager) and are not reflected directly in the URL. No assumptions are made about the presence or values of specific IDs or routes; all displayed content comes directly from the dynamic data returned by getMyRoutes and responses from removeSavedRoute.

components.functionality: Coordinates global page state for the My Routes page. On load, it parses URL query parameters (such as sort and highlightBusRouteId) and initializes state values including currentSort, highlightBusRouteId, routes, isLoading, loadError, pendingRemoveSavedRouteId, and notifications. It exposes methods for updating state in response to user actions and URL changes and ensures that components receive consistent state props.

Responsible for fetching the list of saved routes. On initialization, it calls getMyRoutes with no parameters. It sets a loading flag before the call, clears any previous errors, and stores the returned array of objects (each containing saved_route_id, saved_at, bus_route_id, route_number, name, arrival_time_at_school, school_name, primary_streets) into shared state. On error, it records an error state so that the UI can show an error message and a retry option.

Displays controls and summary information for the My Routes page. It shows the total number of saved routes (derived from the current routes array) and a sort control bound to the currentSort state. When the user selects a different sort option, it updates the sort parameter in the URL and notifies the state manager so that the list can be re-sorted. The toolbar may also provide a control to clear any current highlight, which removes highlightBusRouteId from state and optionally from the URL.

Renders the collection of saved routes returned from getMyRoutes. It receives the routes array, the currentSort value, and highlightBusRouteId from state. The component applies client-side sorting based on currentSort and maps each route object to a saved-route-row component. If the routes array is empty and no loading error is present, it shows an empty-state panel. If there is a loadError, it shows an error message and a retry control.

Represents a single saved route in the list. For each item from getMyRoutes, it displays key information such as route_number, name, school_name, arrival_time_at_school, primary_streets, and saved_at. It checks whether the route’s bus_route_id matches highlightBusRouteId; if so, it renders in a highlighted state. Clicking the main row area triggers navigation to route_detail.html with the bus_route_id in the URL. Clicking the remove control triggers the removal confirmation flow via the state manager.

Handles confirmation of route removal. When opened, it reads the currently selected savedRouteId and associated route information from state to display a context-aware confirmation prompt. If the user confirms, it calls removeSavedRoute with the selected savedRouteId, shows a busy state while waiting, and then updates the shared routes array: on success, it removes the item whose saved_route_id matches the provided id; on failure, it leaves the list unchanged and forwards an error message to the notification component. After a successful removal, it clears the selected savedRouteId and closes the modal.

Displays transient messages about operations on the My Routes page. It listens to events from the loader and removal modal to show success messages (e.g., route removed successfully, routes loaded) or error messages (e.g., failure to load saved routes, failure to remove a route). The content of these notifications may use the message field from the removeSavedRoute response when available. Notifications can be dismissed by the user and do not persist beyond the current page session.

Shown by the list component when getMyRoutes returns an empty array and there is no loading error. It informs the user that they currently have no saved routes and may suggest how to add routes (for example, by visiting route pages and using their save functionality). It does not load any data itself and does not call any interfaces.

layout.grid_system: Centered fixed-width content column likely based on a 12-column grid (~1080–1200px wide) within full-bleed horizontal bands.
layout.layout_pattern: The page sits inside the existing institutional template: stacked full-width bands for utility bar, main nav, page banner, and section tabs occupy the top ~40% of the viewport. The My Routes page content then appears as a single centered column below this chrome, following a linear top-to-bottom flow. Within this main column, the order is: breadcrumb and page title (from the template, not part of these components), optional short explanatory text, then the notification banner (when present), followed by the My Routes toolbar, and then the primary content area that is either the My Routes list (with stacked saved-route rows) or the empty-state panel when there are no routes. The remove-route confirmation modal appears centered over the entire page as an overlay, independent of the main flow.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page sits inside the existing institutional template: stacked full-width bands for utility bar, main nav, page banner, and section tabs occupy the top ~40% of the viewport. The My Routes page content then appears as a single centered column below this chrome, following a linear top-to-bottom flow. Within this main column, the order is: breadcrumb and page title (from the template, not part of these components), optional short explanatory text, then the notification banner (when present), followed by the My Routes toolbar, and then the primary content area that is either the My Routes list (with stacked saved-route rows) or the empty-state panel when there are no routes. The remove-route confirmation modal appears centered over the entire page as an overlay, independent of the main flow.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The fixed-width content column (about 1080–1200px wide) is centered within the viewport. Header and navigation bars consume the top ~40% of the viewport height. The remaining ~60% is dedicated to this page’s content: the title and any intro copy take roughly the top 10–15% of this area, the notification banner and toolbar together occupy another ~10–15%, and the remaining ~70–80% is reserved for the list of saved routes or the empty-state panel. Within the column, components span most of the content width (about 70–80% of the container’s inner width for text blocks), with comfortable left and right margins. Vertical spacing between major blocks (notification, toolbar, list/empty-state) uses 24–32px, while spacing between rows and controls uses 8–16px, consistent with the 8px base spacing scale.
layout_strategies.content_density: Density is moderate and utilitarian. The toolbar and list rows are compact but not cramped, with 12–16px of internal padding and 8–12px gaps between rows. The notification banner and empty-state panel use slightly more breathing room to distinguish them as special states. Overall, the content is designed to keep several routes visible at once without feeling heavy, matching the conservative, information-first tone of the existing mockup.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow continues the F-pattern from the template: the user’s eye comes down from the global nav and tabs to the breadcrumb and large H1 on the left, then moves rightward across the top of the content column. The notification banner, when present, forms a strong horizontal bar just under the title, then the eye moves down to the toolbar’s left-aligned controls, scanning horizontally to any right-aligned actions. From there, attention drops vertically through the stacked saved-route rows, each of which has a left-to-right emphasis: route name and details on the left, and open/remove actions on the right. The modal, when triggered, interrupts this flow by centering a dialog over a dimmed background, making the decision to remove a route a focused, high-salience step.

layout.overall_description: Below the site’s blue navigation and grey page banner, the My Routes page appears as a clean, centered column framed by generous white margins on the left and right. At the top of this column, the breadcrumb and a large 'My Routes' heading anchor the page, echoing the strong typographic hierarchy of the mockup. Just beneath this, a subtle notification bar may appear as a full-width tinted strip, stretching across the main text width and signaling any recent changes, such as a route being removed. A compact toolbar follows, spanning the width of the column and aligning its primary controls to the left and any secondary actions, like bulk removal or refresh, to the right. Below a clear separation of about 24–32px, the main functional area fills most of the screen: either a vertical stack of simple, flat route rows with left-aligned route details and right-aligned action links, or, if there are no routes, a centered empty-state panel occupying about two-thirds of the column width with a gentle message and a call-to-action. All elements keep to the 8px spacing rhythm and maintain the practical, slightly formal school-district feel. When a route is removed, the background content softens under a translucent scrim and a compact confirmation dialog appears in the center of the viewport, reinforcing the institutional, careful handling of actions that affect students’ transportation.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page-state-manager is non-visual and conceptually sits at the very top of the My Routes content area, just below the page template’s breadcrumb and H1. It does not render a box or occupy visible height; instead, it wraps and coordinates the toolbar, notification, list, empty-state, loader, and modal. In the layout flow, everything the user sees inside the main content column is effectively a child of this invisible manager. It has no padding or margins and does not alter the visual spacing defined by the other components.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Invisible wrapper within the normal document flow; it does not render any DOM box that affects layout and simply controls which of the child components are visible at any time.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The my-routes-loader appears only while data is being fetched and is positioned to clearly overlay the primary My Routes content area without covering the global navigation or page banner. Visually, it presents as a light, flat panel that sits directly over the section where the toolbar and list/empty-state would be. The panel aligns with the main content column’s left and right edges and begins roughly where the toolbar would normally appear, extending down enough to comfortably center a spinner and short loading message in the middle of the visible column. The underlying content is either not yet rendered or subtly dimmed, but remains within the same spatial footprint. The loader’s vertical padding is generous (around 48–64px), creating an obvious transient state while maintaining the conservative, minimal styling seen in the mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Temporarily overlays the toolbar and list/empty-state region within the content column, centered and non-scrolling; disappears once data has loaded, returning control to the normal content flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The my-routes-toolbar is the first clearly interactive element in the My Routes content area. It is positioned directly below the page title and any brief explanatory text, with about 16–24px of vertical space separating it from the text above. The toolbar spans nearly the full width of the content column (approximately 90–100% of the inner column width), aligned flush to the left with the main text, and leaving equal white margins on both sides relative to the page’s fixed-width container. Its height is roughly 56–64px, driven by 12–16px of vertical padding around its contents. Inside, controls are arranged in a simple horizontal bar: on the left, primary elements such as a 'Saved routes' count label or filters; on the right, a small set of secondary actions such as 'Remove all' or 'Refresh', aligned with the right edge of the column. The toolbar has either a subtle bottom border or a very light background tint to distinguish it from the list below, with 16–24px of space before the first list row begins.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element within the normal document flow; stays anchored near the top of the My Routes content area and scrolls off the screen with the page content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The my-routes-list sits directly beneath the toolbar, separated by a clear 16–24px vertical gap that marks the transition from controls to content. It occupies the full available width of the content column, aligned to the same left and right text edges as the H1 and toolbar, so the stack of rows forms a solid vertical line down the page. Within this region, saved-route-row components are arranged in a single column, each row spanning essentially 100% of the list width with a narrow, consistent gutter (8–12px) between them. The list can extend vertically below the fold, with vertical scrolling of the page revealing more rows, while the left and right margins relative to the browser window remain constant due to the fixed-width, centered layout. When populated, this list area visually dominates the lower 60–70% of the content region, making it clear that saved routes are the main focus of the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static stacked container within the document flow; it grows vertically as more rows are present and scrolls normally with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each saved-route-row is a horizontally oriented, full-width item inside the list that reads like a simplified, flat card. It stretches from the left text edge of the content column to the right text edge, with 16–24px of left and right internal padding and 12–16px of vertical padding, giving an overall height of roughly 64–80px depending on content. Rows are separated by 8–12px of vertical space or a subtle 1px divider line to align with the conservative, institutional style. Inside each row, content follows an F-pattern: on the left, the route’s primary identifier (e.g., route name/number) appears in a bold or slightly larger type; directly beneath it, in smaller text, a single line of key details such as school name, stop location, and AM/PM designation. On the right-hand side of the row, aligned vertically centered with the text, are the main actions: first the primary 'Open' or 'View details' control, rendered as a simple flat button or prominent text link, and to its right or just below, a secondary 'Remove' link styled as a lower-emphasis text button. This right-aligned action cluster sits flush with the row’s right padding, visually balancing the left-aligned text. When the user hovers or focuses the row, the background may shift to a slightly darker tint of grey or blue, and the row’s border (if present) may become subtly more pronounced, maintaining the understated UI while reinforcing interactivity.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static list item within the my-routes-list; rows stack vertically and respond to hover/focus states but do not alter the layout flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The remove-route-confirmation-modal appears on top of all other content when the user chooses to remove a route. It is centered in the browser viewport both horizontally and vertically, regardless of scroll position. Behind it, the entire page (including header, toolbar, and list) is dimmed with a semi-transparent dark overlay that covers the full width and height of the viewport, ensuring the dialog is the only focal point. The modal itself is a compact rectangle, about 400–480px wide (roughly one-third to one-half of the content column width), with a white background, modest 24–32px internal padding, and subtle drop shadow consistent with conservative institution styling. At the top, a clear title such as 'Remove saved route?' is left-aligned; below it, a short explanatory sentence wraps to about 70–80% of the modal width. At the bottom-right, the primary actions (e.g., 'Cancel' and 'Remove') are horizontally aligned, with the safer option visually emphasized and the destructive action clearly labeled, using about 8–16px of spacing between buttons. The vertical spacing inside the modal follows the 8–16px rhythm, making the dialog feel ordered and deliberate.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed-position overlay centered in the viewport with a full-screen scrim; it remains in place while the background page content does not scroll or is visually de-emphasized until the modal is dismissed.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The notification-banner is positioned near the top of the My Routes content column so that state changes are immediately visible without overshadowing the page title. It appears directly below the H1 'My Routes' (and any brief subtitle) with about 8–16px of vertical separation, and directly above the my-routes-toolbar with another 16–24px gap below it. The banner spans the same width as the main text column—roughly 70–80% of the overall page width—forming a solid horizontal bar across the content area. Its background is a soft, tinted color (e.g., pale green for success, yellow for warning, red for error) with 12–16px of vertical padding and 16–24px horizontal padding. Inside, an icon or status label aligns on the left, followed by one or two lines of concise text; a close or dismiss icon, if present, is right-aligned but remains within the same horizontal band. The banner’s height is compact, around 48–64px, and it clearly separates from surrounding content so the message is noticeable but doesn’t feel intrusive.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element within the main content flow; it pushes the toolbar and list downward when present and collapses out of the layout entirely when not needed (no reserved empty space).

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The empty-state-panel occupies the same positional slot as the my-routes-list when there are no saved routes, appearing directly below the toolbar with 24–32px of vertical separation. Instead of a full-height list, it renders a centered panel that is narrower than the full column, taking up about 60–70% of the content column’s width and remaining horizontally centered within it. This creates comfortable white space on both sides, reinforcing the sense of emptiness. The panel itself has a light border or slightly tinted background, consistent with the site’s conservative visual style, and 32–48px of internal padding to feel welcoming and not cramped. Inside, a small illustrative icon or simple school-related symbol may sit at the top center, followed by a concise heading like 'No routes saved yet' and a short explanatory sentence. A clear call-to-action (for example, a link or button 'Find routes to save') is centered beneath the text with 16–24px of vertical spacing. The entire panel sits roughly in the upper half of the available content area, leaving additional whitespace below so that the page does not feel overly dense when there is little information.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static component that replaces the my-routes-list region when there are no saved routes; it is centered within the content column and scrolls normally with the page, though in most cases it fits comfortably above the fold.

name: School Calendar
title: School Calendar
description: Interactive school-wide calendar where parents can choose a month, filter events by audience and time of day, view matching events in a calendar and agenda list, and open detailed event pages.

core_features: Load calendar filter options (audiences, time-of-day ranges, sort options) from getCalendarFilterOptions on page initialization.
Determine the initial month, year, and optional filters from URL parameters and use them to search for events via searchCalendarEvents.
Allow users to change the visible month and year, triggering a new searchCalendarEvents call while updating the URL parameters.
Provide filter controls for audience and time-of-day range using options from getCalendarFilterOptions, and re-query events via searchCalendarEvents when filters change.
Display a month-style calendar view that highlights days with events returned by searchCalendarEvents.
Display an agenda/list view of the same events returned by searchCalendarEvents, synchronized with the currently selected month and filters.
Provide optional sort behavior for events (e.g., by start date/time) using sort options from getCalendarFilterOptions and passing the selected sort to searchCalendarEvents.
Enable navigation to event_detail.html with the selected event’s id as a URL parameter when a user clicks an event title in either the calendar or agenda list.
Respect any incoming audience and timeRange URL parameters (from other pages) to pre-apply filters and reflect them in the filter controls.
user_workflows:
View calendar for a specific month:
1. User navigates to calendar.html (optionally with month and year URL parameters).
2. On load, the page parses URL parameters for month, year, audience, and timeRange.
3. The page calls getCalendarFilterOptions to populate audience and time-of-day range options.
4. The page resolves the effective month and year (URL values if present, otherwise the current date) and effective filters (URL audience/timeRange if present).
5. The page calls searchCalendarEvents with the resolved month, year, filters, and default sort option.
6. The returned events are bound to the calendar grid and agenda list components.
7. The user sees the selected month’s calendar with event indicators, and the agenda list shows the same events in chronological order.

Filter events by audience and time-of-day:
1. User opens calendar.html, with or without pre-applied filters in the URL.
2. The filter controls are populated from getCalendarFilterOptions and initialized to match any incoming audience and timeRange URL parameters.
3. The user selects or changes an audience filter.
4. The state manager updates the internal filter state and updates the audience URL parameter to the selected value (or removes it if cleared).
5. The page calls searchCalendarEvents with the current month, year, new audience filter, existing time-of-day filter (if any), and current sort option.
6. The calendar grid and agenda list re-render to show only events matching the new audience.
7. The user optionally selects a time-of-day range filter, triggering the same sequence (URL update, searchCalendarEvents call, UI refresh).

Switch months while preserving filters:
1. User is viewing events for a given month with specific audience and/or time-of-day filters applied.
2. The user clicks a next/previous month control or selects a month/year from a picker.
3. The state manager updates the month and year state and synchronizes the month and year URL parameters.
4. The page calls searchCalendarEvents with the new month and year, preserving the current audience, timeRange, and sort selection.
5. The calendar grid and agenda list re-render with the events returned for the new month.
6. If the user shares or bookmarks the resulting URL, reopening it will restore the same month/year and filters.

Open an event detail page from calendar or agenda list:
1. User identifies an event of interest in either the calendar grid (e.g., clicking on a day’s event entry) or the agenda list.
2. The user clicks the event title or a dedicated “View details” action associated with that event.
3. The click handler reads the event’s id from the bound Event data.
4. The browser navigates to event_detail.html with a URL parameter eventId set to the selected event id (e.g., event_detail.html?eventId={id}).
5. The event detail page uses the eventId parameter to show full event information (outside the scope of this page).

Use calendar via deep link from another page:
1. User clicks a link on index.html or my_events.html that points to calendar.html with predefined URL parameters (e.g., a specific month/year, audience, or timeRange).
2. On load, calendar.html reads the provided month, year, audience, and timeRange URL parameters.
3. The filter controls are initialized to reflect these parameters, and the month/year switcher reflects the selected period.
4. The page calls searchCalendarEvents using the incoming parameters.
5. The user sees the calendar and agenda list already focused on the target time period and audience/time filter without needing extra input.
interactions: On page load: parse URL parameters (month, year, audience, timeRange); call getCalendarFilterOptions; then call searchCalendarEvents with resolved state; render calendar and agenda views.
On audience filter change: update internal audience state and audience URL parameter; trigger searchCalendarEvents with current month, year, timeRange, and sort; refresh calendar and agenda list.

On time-of-day range filter change: update internal timeRange state and timeRange URL parameter; trigger searchCalendarEvents with current month, year, audience, and sort; refresh calendar and agenda list.

On sort option change: update internal sort state (and optional sort URL parameter if used); call searchCalendarEvents with current month, year, audience, and timeRange; refresh agenda list and any time-ordered views.

On month navigation (next/previous month or direct month/year selection): update month/year state and URL parameters; call searchCalendarEvents with new month/year plus current filters and sort; refresh calendar and agenda list.

On event click in calendar grid: determine which event instance was clicked (from bound Event data); navigate the browser to event_detail.html with eventId set to the clicked event’s id; optionally in the same or a new tab depending on link attributes.
On event click in agenda list: same behavior as calendar grid event click—navigate to event_detail.html with eventId URL parameter based on the selected event.

On browser back/forward navigation: detect URL changes (e.g., via popstate); re-parse URL parameters into month/year/audience/timeRange (and sort, if used); call searchCalendarEvents to keep the displayed data consistent with the URL.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page uses URL parameters as the single source of truth for externally visible state. On initialization, it reads month, year, audience, and timeRange from the URL query string. If month and year are not present, the page computes the current month and year from the client’s current date at runtime (not hardcoded) and uses those as defaults without adding them immediately to the URL unless a state change occurs. If audience and/or timeRange are missing, filters start in an unselected state. Whenever the user changes month, year, audience, timeRange, or sort, the state manager updates the in-memory state and synchronizes that state to the URL parameters using the History API (e.g., pushState/replaceState) or by setting window.location.search. This ensures that reloading the page or sharing the URL reproduces the same calendar view and filters. Each state change that affects what events should be shown triggers a new call to searchCalendarEvents with parameters derived solely from the current state (month, year, filters, sort_by). The returned events array is the single data source for both the calendar grid and the agenda list. The is_saved field in each event is read-only and is used only to display saved status; no saving actions occur on this page since there is no interface for modifying SavedEvent. The getCalendarFilterOptions response is cached in memory for the life of the page to populate and label filter controls, but it is not persisted in any external storage. URL parameters are never read or written from localStorage or similar; only direct URL parsing and setting is used to manage sharable state.

components.functionality: Central controller for the School Calendar page. On load, it parses URL parameters (month, year, audience, timeRange), calls getCalendarFilterOptions to obtain available audiences, time-of-day ranges, and sort options, and then calls searchCalendarEvents with the resolved state. It holds in-memory state for month, year, selected audience, selected timeRange, selected sort option, and the current events list. It exposes methods to update this state in response to user interactions (month navigation, filter changes, sort changes) and ensures that every relevant change results in a synchronized URL update and a new call to searchCalendarEvents.

Provides controls for users to move between months and years (e.g., previous/next month buttons and/or a month/year selector). It displays the current month/year based on state supplied by calendar-state-manager. When the user changes the month or year, it notifies the state manager, which updates the URL and requests new events via searchCalendarEvents.

Displays audience and time-of-day range filters plus a sort selector. Options for these controls come from getCalendarFilterOptions through the state manager. The component reflects any currently active filters and sort selection derived from URL parameters. When the user selects or clears an audience or time-of-day range, or changes the sort option, it triggers the corresponding state-manager handlers to update state, sync URL parameters, and fetch filtered events.

Renders a month-style grid for the currently selected month and year. For each day in the displayed month, it uses the events data supplied by the state manager (from searchCalendarEvents) to determine which events occur on that date and displays them in a compact form. It may show a limited subset of event information per day (e.g., titles or time ranges). Clicking on an event within a day triggers navigation to the event detail page.

Displays the same events returned by searchCalendarEvents in a chronological list for the currently selected month and filters. It uses the sort option determined by the state manager to order events. Each list item shows key details such as title, date/time, audience label, and location (all derived from the Event fields and related labels returned by searchCalendarEvents). Clicking the title or a dedicated link on an item navigates to event_detail.html with the event’s id in the URL.

Handles navigation from the calendar page to the event detail page for a selected event. It listens for event-click actions from both calendar-grid-view and calendar-agenda-list. When triggered, it reads the event id from the clicked Event object and constructs a URL to event_detail.html with eventId set appropriately, then performs the navigation in the current or a new tab as configured.

Shows appropriate feedback when searchCalendarEvents returns no events for the current month and filters or when an error occurs. For empty results, it displays a simple, data-driven message indicating that no events match the selected criteria. For errors (e.g., failed API calls), it displays a recoverable error message and can expose a retry action that triggers the state manager to re-call searchCalendarEvents.

Encapsulates logic for reading and writing calendar-related URL parameters. On initialization, it parses month, year, audience, and timeRange (and optionally a sort parameter if used) from the query string and passes them to calendar-state-manager. When notified of state changes (month/year navigation or filter/sort changes), it updates the URL query string accordingly without reloading the page. It also listens for browser navigation events (e.g., back/forward) and re-applies state from the changed URL, prompting the state manager to re-fetch events.

layout.grid_system: Centered fixed-width content column likely based on a 12-column grid (~1080–1200px wide) within full-bleed horizontal bands.
layout.layout_pattern: Below the already existing stacked header (utility bar, main nav, banner, tabs) and the standard breadcrumb + large H1 row, the calendar page continues the single-column pattern seen in the mockup. All calendar-related components are arranged in a vertical sequence within the centered fixed-width content column: month/year switcher at the top, then a horizontal filter bar, followed by the month grid, and finally the agenda-style list. Any conditional states (empty/error messaging) appear in place of the grid+agenda, centered within the same column. There are no sidebars; all interactive elements are left-aligned within the main column, with occasional right-aligned inline details (like counts or labels) that never form a distinct second column.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Below the already existing stacked header (utility bar, main nav, banner, tabs) and the standard breadcrumb + large H1 row, the calendar page continues the single-column pattern seen in the mockup. All calendar-related components are arranged in a vertical sequence within the centered fixed-width content column: month/year switcher at the top, then a horizontal filter bar, followed by the month grid, and finally the agenda-style list. Any conditional states (empty/error messaging) appear in place of the grid+agenda, centered within the same column. There are no sidebars; all interactive elements are left-aligned within the main column, with occasional right-aligned inline details (like counts or labels) that never form a distinct second column.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The global header stack still occupies roughly the top 40% of the initial viewport. The calendar page content starts just below the tab bar. Inside the main content column (about 1080–1200px wide, centered), the first 10–15% of vertical space is used by the breadcrumb, H1 'School Calendar', and a short intro line. The next 10–15% is dedicated to controls: the month/year switcher and the filter bar. The calendar grid then occupies the largest block, approximately 40–50% of the visible content area, stretching nearly the full width of the content column. The agenda list sits below the grid and uses the remaining 20–30% of initial scroll, with list items occupying roughly 70–80% of the column width to mimic text paragraph width. Vertical separation between each of these bands follows the 24–32px pattern, with tighter 8–16px spacing within each component.
layout_strategies.content_density: Density is moderate and practical. Control rows (month/year switcher, filter bar) are compact but not cramped, with 8–12px vertical and 12–16px horizontal padding inside each interactive element. The calendar grid cells display a small number of events per day with clear line spacing, avoiding overcrowding by truncating or summarizing if necessary. The agenda list presents events as compact rows with 8–12px vertical spacing between items. Overall, everything is close enough to minimize scrolling but padded enough to feel orderly and readable in line with an institutional, government-style school website.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye lands first on the existing page H1 'School Calendar' near the top-left of the content column, then quickly drops to the month/year switcher aligned on the left. From there, attention moves horizontally across the filter bar, scanning from left to right through the different filter controls. The top row of the calendar grid (month label and day-of-week headers) becomes the main visual anchor in the middle of the page. Users then scan along the top of the grid (F-pattern horizontal stroke) and naturally move down the days (vertical stroke). After processing the grid, the flow continues straight down to the agenda list, where the user reads event rows from left to right. If the calendar is empty or an error occurs, the eye is drawn to a centered message panel that replaces the grid+agenda region but still sits within this same vertical flow below the filters.

layout.overall_description: Inside the already established CBE-style frame (utility bar, main navigation, grey/blue banner, and tabs), the School Calendar page presents a single, centered content column. At the top of this column, the breadcrumb and large 'School Calendar' heading mirror the mockup’s typography, with optional right-aligned school branding imagery sharing the same row. Beneath a short explanatory line, a compact control zone begins: first a left-aligned month/year switcher sitting in its own narrow horizontal band, then a slightly taller, full-width filter bar band with dropdowns and toggles for audience and time-of-day. Generous 24–32px spacing separates this control area from the main calendar canvas. The monthly grid then fills most of the column’s width, framed by subtle boundaries and day headers, forming the core visual anchor of the page. Directly below, separated by another 24–32px, the agenda list continues the information in a more linear, text-centric format, with each event row aligned to the left and occupying about three-quarters of the column width to maintain comfortable reading lines. When there are no matching events or an error, a centered message card appears where the grid and agenda would normally be, preserving the same structural bands. The result is a clean, top-heavy, institutionally styled layout that guides parents from high-level date selection into detailed event information without visual clutter.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The calendar-state-manager is not visually represented as a distinct UI element; it behaves as an invisible layer encompassing the entire calendar zone, starting just below the page’s main H1 and ending after the agenda list. Conceptually, it wraps the month-year switcher, filter bar, grid view, agenda list, and empty/error state area, ensuring they appear and update in a coordinated manner. It does not consume any visible width or height itself and aligns with the boundaries of the main content column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual; it occupies no physical space and simply governs the state of all visual calendar components within the normal document flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The month-year-switcher sits near the top of the calendar content area, directly below the page’s breadcrumb, H1 'School Calendar', and any short explanatory text. It forms its own thin horizontal band within the centered content column, aligned flush-left with the heading text. The switcher controls (e.g., previous/next arrows and a month-year dropdown) together occupy roughly 35–45% of the content column width, leaving the remaining right-hand side mostly empty or available for a small right-aligned secondary label such as 'Showing all events'. Vertically, this band has about 12–16px padding above and below the controls, with around 24px of space separating it from the heading above and 16–24px separating it from the filter bar below. This keeps the control compact yet visually distinct, echoing the top-of-content utility row seen in the mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the standard document flow; it scrolls away with the content and does not stick to the viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The calendar-filter-bar appears immediately beneath the month-year switcher, spanning the full width of the main content column. It forms a low, horizontal strip similar in height to the main navigation bar from the mockup but visually lighter, with a white or very light grey background and a subtle bottom border or shadow to delineate it from the calendar grid below. Inside this strip, filter controls (for audience, grade, time of day, event type) are aligned from left to right, each with 12–16px horizontal padding and 8–12px vertical padding, following the 8px spacing rhythm. The left edge of the first filter aligns with the left edge of the H1 and month/year switcher above, preserving a strong content rail. The filter bar’s total height is approximately 56–72px, depending on the number of controls. There is about 16–24px of vertical space above (from the month-year switcher band) and 24–32px below, leading into the calendar grid. This positions the filter bar as a clear, functional divider between high-level date selection and the main calendar display.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, full-width within the content column; it scrolls with the page and does not overlay or float above the grid.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The calendar-grid-view occupies the central, most visually dominant portion of the page within the content column. It is positioned directly beneath the filter bar, separated by approximately 24–32px of vertical whitespace. The grid spans nearly the entire width of the content column, with 16–24px of internal padding on the left and right to keep cell content from touching the edges, and aligns left with the components above. The top of the grid contains a row of day-of-week headers forming a horizontal bar that serves as a strong visual anchor, mirroring the horizontal emphasis seen in the mockup’s tab bar and navigation. Below, the month grid is laid out in 7 columns and 5–6 rows of days. Each day cell uses modest internal padding (8–12px), with date numbers aligned to the top-left of each cell and event titles listed underneath. The grid’s total height is substantial, typically filling 40–50% of the visible content area on initial load, making it the dominant element users see once they scroll slightly past the header stack. There is another 24–32px of vertical spacing below the grid before the agenda list begins, clearly separating the two representations of the same data while maintaining a linear top-to-bottom flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block element in the normal flow; it expands vertically as needed based on the number of weeks displayed but always stays within the content column’s fixed width.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The calendar-agenda-list is located directly beneath the calendar grid view, separated by 24–32px of whitespace. It sits within the same centered content column but is slightly more text-driven and narrower in perceived width: individual event rows occupy about 70–80% of the column’s width, aligned to the left, leaving a comfortable band of empty space on the right consistent with how paragraphs and bullets are treated in the mockup. A simple section label such as 'Agenda View' or 'Upcoming Events' may appear as a small heading above the list, aligned with the left rail and spaced about 8–16px above the first row. Each event row is compact but readable, with 8–12px vertical padding inside and 8–12px gap between rows. Key information (date, time, title, audience) is arranged left-to-right in a single line or two-line block, maintaining the F-pattern reading direction. The agenda list typically extends less vertically than the grid but can grow as more events are shown, pushing the page downward while preserving the overall single-column structure.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, full-width section within the main column; it extends vertically as events are added and scrolls normally with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The event-detail-navigator has no standalone visual block; instead, it is expressed through inline interactive affordances within the calendar-grid-view and calendar-agenda-list. For example, event titles in the grid cells and agenda rows behave as links, often styled with the brand blue used elsewhere in the site. Small right-pointing chevrons or 'More details' labels may sit to the far right within an agenda row, aligned on the same horizontal line as the event title, reinforcing the direction of navigation. These clickable areas align with the left and right padding conventions of their parent components but do not require extra vertical space. When activated, they transition the user to a separate event detail page or view, following the site’s standard navigation pattern.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-block, inline behavior embedded within other components; it does not define its own layout region and moves with the grid and agenda items as they scroll.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The calendar-empty-or-error-state appears only when there are no events matching the current filters or when an error prevents events from loading. It occupies the same vertical region where the calendar grid and agenda list would normally appear, starting immediately below the filter bar. In this state, instead of the grid and list, a centered message card is displayed within the content column. This card spans roughly 50–60% of the column width and is horizontally centered, with 24–32px padding inside and 24–32px of whitespace above and below it. A concise heading (e.g., 'No events found') is aligned left within the card, with explanatory text below, and potentially a 'Reset filters' button aligned left or right within the same card. The top of the card aligns with the left rail established by the H1 and filters, while its right edge leaves a margin of empty space similar to a medium-width text block. This preserves the page’s linear flow and spacing rhythm even when there is no calendar data to show.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Conditional; it replaces the calendar grid and agenda list in the normal flow but occupies the same relative position and scroll behavior within the content column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The url-state-sync component is invisible and does not present any independent UI. It conceptually spans the same region as the calendar-state-manager, listening to and updating the state of the month-year switcher, filter bar, grid, and agenda list. It does not introduce any spacing, borders, or visual elements and does not alter the layout of surrounding components; it simply ensures that the current calendar view and filters are reflected in the URL and restored when the page is revisited or shared.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual, background behavior tied to the entire calendar experience; it occupies no layout space and has no scrolling or positioning of its own.

name: Event Detail
title: Event Detail
description: Displays full information about a single school event and lets families save it to their My Events list or return to the calendar.

core_features: Load and display complete details for a single event based on the eventId URL parameter using getEventDetail.
Show key event metadata such as date, time, location, audience, grade-level relevance, category, and all-day status.
Present the full event description and any special instructions contained in the description field.
Indicate whether the event is already saved to the user's My Events list using the is_saved flag from getEventDetail.
Allow the user to save the event to My Events by calling saveEventToMyEvents with the current eventId.
Provide a clear navigation link back to the main calendar page (calendar.html).
Optionally show a link to an external resource (such as registration or additional info) when external_link is provided.
user_workflows:
View event details from the calendar:
1. User clicks an event on calendar.html, which navigates to event_detail.html with ?eventId=... in the URL.
2. On page load, event_detail.html reads eventId from the URL.
3. The page calls getEventDetail(eventId).
4. While data is loading, the page shows a loading state for the event detail area.
5. When getEventDetail returns successfully, the page displays title, date/time, location, audience, grade-level labels, category, and description.
6. The Save to My Events button state is set based on the returned is_saved flag.
7. The user may click Back to Calendar to return to calendar.html.

View event details from My Events:
1. User clicks an event in my_events.html, which navigates to event_detail.html with ?eventId=... in the URL.
2. The page behavior is the same as when coming from the calendar: it reads eventId, calls getEventDetail, and renders the details.
3. Because the event was previously saved, getEventDetail is expected to return is_saved = true, and the save control reflects that state (e.g., showing that it is already saved).
4. The user may still use Back to Calendar to return to the main calendar overview.

Save an event to My Events:
1. User is on event_detail.html with a successfully loaded event and sees that the event is not saved (is_saved = false).
2. User clicks the Save to My Events button.
3. The page disables the button or shows a saving state to prevent duplicate submissions.
4. The page calls saveEventToMyEvents(eventId).
5. If saveEventToMyEvents returns success = true, the page:
   - Updates the local event state to mark is_saved = true.
   - Optionally shows a confirmation message using the returned message and saved_at.
6. If success = false or the call fails, the page:
   - Leaves is_saved = false.
   - Shows an error message using the returned message (if provided) and re-enables the button.

Open an external event link:
1. User is on event_detail.html and the loaded event data includes an external_link value.
2. The page displays an external link action (for example, a button or link labeled according to the UI copy) that uses external_link as its target URL.
3. When the user activates this link, the browser navigates to the external_link URL in the configured manner (same tab or new tab, depending on design/implementation).

Handle missing or invalid eventId:
1. User navigates to event_detail.html without an eventId parameter, or with an eventId that does not correspond to an existing event.
2. On page load, the data loader detects that eventId is missing or getEventDetail fails (e.g., not found).
3. The page displays an error state explaining that the event could not be loaded.
4. The Save to My Events button and other event-specific controls are disabled or hidden.
5. A Back to Calendar link remains available so the user can return to calendar.html.
interactions:
Page load / URL change:
- Trigger: event_detail.html is opened or the eventId query parameter changes.
- Behavior: The data loader component reads eventId from the URL. If present, it calls getEventDetail(eventId) and sets the page into a loading state until the response is received or an error occurs.

Save to My Events button click:
- Trigger: User clicks the primary save control when an event is successfully loaded and is_saved is false.
- Behavior: The button enters a saving state. The page calls saveEventToMyEvents(eventId). On success, the page updates the event detail state to is_saved = true and shows a confirmation message. On failure, it shows an error message and restores the button to its initial (unsaved) state.

Saved state indicator interaction:
- Trigger: Event detail is loaded (or a save operation completes) and is_saved is true.
- Behavior: The save control displays a saved state (for example, a non-clickable label or a secondary action like “Saved to My Events”). If the design allows unsaving, that would need another interface, which is not available here, so the saved state is informational-only on this page.

Back to Calendar link click:
- Trigger: User clicks the Back to Calendar link.
- Behavior: Browser navigates to calendar.html with no additional parameters defined by this page. The user’s browser navigation history remains intact so the Back button can return them to their previous filtered state on calendar.html if that page manages its own filters via its URL.

External event link click:
- Trigger: User clicks the external resource link when external_link is defined in the event data.
- Behavior: Browser navigates directly to the URL specified in external_link.

Error or empty state acknowledgment:
- Trigger: The event load fails (no eventId or an error from getEventDetail).
- Behavior: The page displays an error message and suggests returning to the calendar. The Back to Calendar link remains interactive; other controls (save button, external link) are hidden or disabled because there is no valid event context.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page state is primarily controlled by the eventId URL parameter and the responses from getEventDetail and saveEventToMyEvents. On initial load, the page reads eventId from the query string (e.g., event_detail.html?eventId=...). If eventId is missing, the page enters an error state and does not call getEventDetail. If eventId is present, the page calls getEventDetail(eventId) to obtain event data. The returned object drives all renderable fields: title, description, location, start_datetime, end_datetime, audience_label, grade_level_labels, category_label, is_all_day, external_link, and is_saved. The is_saved property determines the initial state of the save control. When the user invokes saveEventToMyEvents(eventId), the response does not change the URL; it only updates in-memory state for this page (is_saved becomes true on success). The saved_event_id and saved_at values from saveEventToMyEvents are used only for optional feedback display and are not written to the URL. The URL remains the single source of truth for which event is shown, so any navigation away from or back to this page must include the correct eventId parameter to re-load the same event. The page does not use localStorage or other client-side persistence; instead, it relies solely on the URL parameter and server responses to reconstruct state whenever the page is loaded.

components.functionality: Reads the eventId from the URL query string, orchestrates the call to getEventDetail(eventId), and manages loading, success, and error states for the entire page.
data_binding: SavedEvent (represented through the is_saved field in getEventDetail result)
event_handlers: onPageLoad: Parse eventId from the URL and, if present, call getEventDetail(eventId).
onUrlChange: If the eventId query parameter changes while the page is active, re-call getEventDetail(eventId) and reset loading state.
onGetEventDetailSuccess: Store the returned event object in page state and clear any previous error state.
onGetEventDetailError: Enter an error state indicating that the event could not be loaded.

components.functionality: Displays the event title and a concise summary of the event’s date and time, using is_all_day, start_datetime, and end_datetime to determine the correct text.
event_handlers: onEventDataChange: When the Event data from event-detail-loader is updated, re-render the title and date/time summary.

components.functionality: Shows structured information about the event, including location, audience, grade-level relevance, and category labels.
event_handlers: onEventDataChange: When the Event data is updated, re-render each metadata field and hide any sections for which data is not provided (for example, no location or no category_label).

components.functionality: Displays the full event description text, which may include special instructions for families.
event_handlers: onEventDataChange: When Event.description changes, update the displayed text.
onEmptyDescription: If description is not provided, hide this section or display a simple placeholder based on overall design rules.

components.functionality: Provides a clickable link to an external resource related to the event when available (for example, a registration form or detailed information page).
event_handlers: onEventDataChange: If external_link is present, render an actionable link element using this URL; if not present, hide this component.
onClick: Navigate the browser to the URL specified by external_link.

components.functionality: Offers a control to save the currently viewed event to the user’s My Events list and reflects whether the event has already been saved.
event_handlers: onEventDataChange: Initialize the button’s state (enabled/disabled and label) based on Event.is_saved.
onClick: If Event.is_saved is false and the event has loaded successfully, call saveEventToMyEvents(Event.id) and set a temporary saving state.
