onSaveSuccess: When saveEventToMyEvents returns success = true, update Event.is_saved in local state to true, store saved_event_id and saved_at for messaging, update the button label to reflect the saved state, and clear the saving state.

onSaveFailure: When saveEventToMyEvents returns success = false or errors, show an error message via the feedback component, keep Event.is_saved as false, and clear the saving state so the user can retry.

components.functionality: Displays informational or error messages related to saving the event and optionally confirms successful save operations.
event_handlers: onSaveSuccess: Show a success banner using the message and optionally include the saved_at timestamp.
onSaveFailure: Show an error banner using the message if provided, or a generic error message if not.
onDismiss: Allow the user to dismiss the banner, which clears the visible message state.

components.functionality: Provides a navigation control that returns the user to the main calendar page.
event_handlers: onClick: Navigate the browser to calendar.html. The component does not modify the URL beyond this target and relies on the calendar page to manage any of its own filter or view state.

components.functionality: Communicates loading progress while event details are being fetched and shows an error state if the event fails to load or eventId is missing.
data_binding: Loader state from event-detail-loader (loading, success, error)
Error details from getEventDetail failure (if available)
event_handlers: onPageLoad: When event-detail-loader begins fetching data, display a loading indicator and hide event content sections.
onGetEventDetailSuccess: Hide the loading indicator and show event content components.
onGetEventDetailError: Hide the loading indicator, show an error message, and ensure save-to-my-events and external link components are disabled or hidden because no valid event data is available.

layout.grid_system: Centered fixed-width content column likely based on a 12-column grid (~1080–1200px wide) within full-bleed horizontal bands.
layout.layout_pattern: Below the existing global frame (utility bar, main nav, banner, tabs), the Event Detail page uses a single centered content column (~1080–1200px wide) following a strict top-to-bottom flow. Inside this column, components stack in this order: event-loading-and-error-state or event-detail-loader (when applicable), then the main event-header row (title + primary action), followed by the event-meta-section as a compact key-facts block, then the event-description-section as longer narrative text. Beneath the narrative, the event-external-link appears as an optional related link, and finally the back-to-calendar-link sits near the bottom as the exit path. The save-status-feedback banner appears directly under the header area whenever the save-to-my-events-button is used. All elements are left-aligned in the content column, with occasional right-aligned elements (like the primary button) echoing the mockup’s pattern of a small right-side element next to a large left-aligned heading.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Below the existing global frame (utility bar, main nav, banner, tabs), the Event Detail page uses a single centered content column (~1080–1200px wide) following a strict top-to-bottom flow. Inside this column, components stack in this order: event-loading-and-error-state or event-detail-loader (when applicable), then the main event-header row (title + primary action), followed by the event-meta-section as a compact key-facts block, then the event-description-section as longer narrative text. Beneath the narrative, the event-external-link appears as an optional related link, and finally the back-to-calendar-link sits near the bottom as the exit path. The save-status-feedback banner appears directly under the header area whenever the save-to-my-events-button is used. All elements are left-aligned in the content column, with occasional right-aligned elements (like the primary button) echoing the mockup’s pattern of a small right-side element next to a large left-aligned heading.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: As in the mockup, the site header, global nav, and banner collectively occupy roughly the top 40% of the initial viewport. The Event Detail content occupies the remaining ~60%, starting just below the active tab bar. Within this content column, the event-header (title + primary action) uses roughly the top 20% of the content region; the event-meta-section (key facts) takes about 15–20%; the event-description-section dominates with ~40–50% depending on text length. The external link and back-to-calendar-link share the remaining vertical space. Horizontal content width for text-based sections is about 70–80% of the content column, leaving comfortable side margins. Component padding and gaps follow an 8px base: 24–32px vertical separation between major sections, 8–16px between closely related elements (title to meta, bullets to paragraphs, buttons to feedback).
layout_strategies.content_density: The page maintains moderate density: blocks are clearly separated, but there is no excessive whitespace. The key information (title, date, time, location) is tightly grouped at the top for quick scanning, while the narrative description is set in compact paragraphs with consistent line spacing. Bulleted meta details are close enough together (8–12px between lines) to feel efficient but still readable. Buttons and feedback banners use 8–16px internal padding and are separated from other elements by 16–24px, matching the practical, information-first tone of the mockup.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Visual flow follows an F-pattern mirroring the mockup. The eye first lands on the left-aligned event title in the event-header, then scans right to the save-to-my-events-button at the same horizontal level, echoing the original logo/banner behavior. From there, the user’s gaze drops down and left to the event-meta-section bullets (date, time, location), then continues vertically into the event-description-section paragraphs. Any event-external-link beneath the description is encountered next as a left-aligned text link. Finally, the back-to-calendar-link at the bottom-left provides a clear exit path. When visible, the save-status-feedback banner appears just below the header/action area, intersecting this vertical flow without disrupting the main scan path.

layout.overall_description: The Event Detail page sits within the existing institutional frame of stacked horizontal bars: utility strip, deep blue global navigation, a muted gradient or solid banner, and a secondary tab bar with the active 'Events' or 'Open House' tab. Below this framing, a single centered content column presents the event. At the top of this column, a strong, left-aligned event title (H1) anchors the page while a right-aligned 'Save to My Events' button shares the same horizontal band, echoing the mockup’s pattern of a large text block paired with a smaller right-side element. Underneath, a compact list of event facts—date, time, location, and possibly grade range—runs as a short bullet list spanning most of the column width. This condensed key-facts block transitions into a series of narrative paragraphs describing the event in more detail, still left-aligned and using a comfortable but efficient line length. If present, an external resource link appears just below the description, clearly separated but visually subordinate. At the bottom of the column, a simple 'Back to calendar' text link allows users to return, aligned to the left to close the vertical reading path. Throughout, vertical spacing between sections (roughly 24–32px) and tighter intra-section spacing (8–16px) retain the conservative, government-like feel: orderly, predictable, and information-led. Loader and error states temporarily occupy the same central column, replacing the content area with a status panel that still respects the page margins and typographic hierarchy.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The loader appears in the main content column directly beneath the existing page tabs and breadcrumb area, centered horizontally within the fixed-width content container. It occupies roughly the same vertical space that the event-header and meta-section would use, forming a compact block in the upper half of the content area. A spinner or skeleton lines sit centered, with 24–32px top margin from the content column’s top edge and 24px bottom margin before any placeholder or additional messaging. No other event-specific components (header, meta, description, actions) are shown while the loader is active; the loader effectively replaces the event-detail content region within the column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline within the scrollable content column, not fixed; it expands only as needed for its messaging and remains vertically centered within the visible content area when possible, but does not overlay the global header or nav.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The event-header is the first event-specific block in the content column, positioned directly below any breadcrumb or page intro text, with approximately 24–32px of vertical space separating it from the tab bar or preceding band. It spans the full width of the content column, but its main elements follow an F-pattern: the event title (H1) is left-aligned and occupies roughly 70–75% of the content width, while the save-to-my-events-button sits on the same horizontal row, right-aligned within the remaining 25–30%. The H1 uses a large, bold style consistent with the mockup’s primary heading, and has 8–12px of space below before any optional subtitle or date line. The vertical padding within the header block is about 16–24px top and bottom, creating a distinct but compact band of emphasis at the top of the event content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static inline block within the column; no sticky or floating behavior. It establishes the top of the event content area and pushes subsequent sections downward.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The event-meta-section sits directly beneath the event-header, separated by 16–24px of vertical space to indicate close relationship but distinct grouping. It spans roughly 70–80% of the content column’s width, aligned to the left edge with a small right margin so that the text block feels compact and scannable. Inside, key details (e.g., date, time, location, audience) appear as a short bullet list or labeled rows, with each item separated by 8–12px of vertical spacing. The overall block height is relatively short—about 15–20% of the content region—reinforcing its role as a quick facts panel before the more extended description. There is 24–32px of space below this section before the event-description-section begins.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline, left-aligned block with no special behavior; it flows directly after the header and before the narrative content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The event-description-section appears immediately below the event-meta-section with about 24–32px of vertical separation, marking a transition from summary facts to narrative content. It spans approximately 70–80% of the content column’s width, aligned to the left, mirroring the mockup’s main text area. The section begins with an optional smaller heading (e.g., 'Event details') using a secondary heading style, followed by one or more paragraphs of text. Each paragraph is separated by 8–16px of vertical space, with line length designed for readability (roughly 60–80 characters per line). The description area occupies the largest vertical portion of the event content—often 40–50% or more—forming a solid block of text in the center of the page. There is 16–24px of vertical space after the last paragraph before the event-external-link, if present.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static inline content block that grows vertically with text length; it pushes subsequent elements downward and does not alter layout behavior of the surrounding sections.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The event-external-link is positioned directly under the event-description-section, separated by about 16–24px of space to show it is related but distinct. It is left-aligned with the text content, occupying at most 60–70% of the content column to maintain a compact line length. The link appears as a single line or short sentence (e.g., 'Visit the PTA website for more information'), using the standard institutional link styling (blue text, underlined or on hover). If accompanied by an external-link icon, the icon sits immediately to the right of the link text, without affecting overall alignment. There is 24–32px of vertical space below this link before the back-to-calendar-link section at the bottom.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline text-level element within the column; appears only when an external resource is available and does not change the layout of other components.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The save-to-my-events-button is integrated into the event-header’s first row. Horizontally, it is right-aligned within the content column, positioned directly opposite the left-aligned event title. The button has 12–16px of horizontal padding and 8–12px of vertical padding, giving it a compact, flat appearance consistent with the site’s conservative styling. Vertically, the button is centered relative to the height of the H1 line so the two feel like part of a single band. There is at least 16px of space between the button’s outer edges and both the top and bottom edges of the header block. When the viewport narrows, the button can wrap to a second row below the title while staying right-aligned, maintaining a clear separation from the body text below. This placement mirrors the mockup’s small right-aligned image near the heading but replaces it with an action element.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline within the header band; it does not stick on scroll. On narrow layouts, it may shift to its own row but remains visually attached to the event-header section rather than floating independently.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The save-status-feedback banner appears directly below the event-header (and therefore effectively just under the save-to-my-events-button), separated from the header by approximately 8–16px of vertical space. It spans the full width of the content column, forming a low-profile horizontal strip that is visually distinct via a subtle background tint but not as bold as the main nav bars. Inside, the feedback text (e.g., 'Saved to My Events') is left-aligned with 12–16px of horizontal padding on both sides and 8–12px vertical padding. If there is an icon (checkmark or error symbol), it appears to the left of the text. The banner pushes the event-meta-section downward, preserving a 16–24px gap between the bottom of the banner and the top of the meta-section. When not active, no space is reserved for it, maintaining a compact page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline dismissible or auto-hiding band within the column; it does not overlay other content and simply appears/disappears, causing a small vertical reflow of subsequent sections.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The back-to-calendar-link is placed at the bottom of the event content stack, acting as the final element in the vertical flow. It sits left-aligned within the content column, approximately 24–32px below the last preceding item (either the event-external-link or, if that is absent, the final paragraph of the event-description-section). The link is styled as a simple text link, optionally prefixed with a left-pointing chevron icon to emphasize backward navigation. The link’s width is only as wide as its text, leaving ample whitespace to the right and bottom, which visually signals it as a secondary exit rather than a primary call to action. There is at least 32px of vertical space below this link before the end of the content column or any footer content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static inline element at the bottom of the event content; it scrolls naturally with the page and does not stick to the viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The event-loading-and-error-state component occupies the same content column area that would normally hold the header, meta, description, and actions, but is shown only when the event cannot be loaded or an error occurs. It is centered within the content column with 32–48px of top margin from the tab bar or breadcrumb area, forming a self-contained panel approximately 50–60% of the column’s width and auto height based on its message. The panel may have a light bordered or subtly shaded background to differentiate it from the plain page background, with 16–24px internal padding. The error heading is left-aligned at the top of the panel, followed by a brief explanation and one or two action links or buttons (e.g., 'Try again' or 'Back to calendar') stacked vertically with 8–16px spacing. No event-header, meta, description, or save controls appear while this panel is active, ensuring a clear and focused state.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline status panel that temporarily replaces the normal event content within the scrollable column; it does not overlay the global navigation or use fixed positioning.

name: My Events
title: My Events
description: Page where families can view, manage, and navigate to school events they have saved. Events can be seen in different date groupings, removed from the saved list, and opened in event details or the school calendar.

core_features: Load all saved events for the current user using getMyEvents and display them ordered by start date.
Allow users to switch between viewing saved events in a simple list, grouped by week, or grouped by month, with the selected view controlled by URL parameters.
Provide navigation from each saved event to its event detail page (event_detail.html) using the eventId from the event data.
Provide navigation from each saved event to the calendar page (calendar.html), passing the event's month and year as URL parameters.
Allow users to remove an event from their saved list by calling removeSavedEvent with the savedEventId and updating the page state based on the result.
Optionally filter and sort the saved events (e.g., by category_label or audience_label) using client-side logic, with filter and sort state stored in URL parameters.
Handle loading, empty, and error states for the saved events list without using any static or hardcoded event data.
user_workflows:
View upcoming saved events in list view:
1. User navigates to my_events.html (optionally with URL parameters).
2. The page controller parses URL parameters (e.g., view, category, audience, sort, page).
3. The controller calls getMyEvents with no parameters.
4. While waiting, a loading state is shown.
5. On success, events are sorted by start_datetime. Client-side logic can distinguish upcoming vs. past using the current date.
6. The toolbar reads the loaded events to populate available filter values from category_label and audience_label (no hardcoded categories).
7. The list view renders events that match the current filters and sort order, showing for each event the title, formatted date/time range from start_datetime/end_datetime, audience_label, location, and category_label.
8. If no events match (either because the user has none saved or filters exclude all events), the empty-state component displays a contextual message.

Switch between list, week, and month views:
1. User clicks a view mode control (e.g., "List", "By Week", "By Month") in the toolbar.
2. The toolbar updates the URL parameter view to one of the allowed values (e.g., list, week, month) without reloading data.
3. The page controller observes the URL change and updates view mode in its internal state.
4. For list view, the simple list component renders events in a flat list.
5. For week view, the grouped-view component groups events client-side into week ranges based on start_datetime.
6. For month view, the grouped-view component groups events client-side by the calendar month and year derived from start_datetime.
7. Pagination (if applied) is recalculated per view mode, and the current page is controlled by the page URL parameter.

Navigate from a saved event to event details and back:
1. In any view, the user clicks on an event title.
2. The list/grouped component reads the event_id for that saved event.
3. The browser navigates to event_detail.html?eventId={event_id}.
4. If the user returns to My Events (e.g., via navigation header or back button), any existing URL parameters on my_events.html (view, filters, page, highlightEventId) are used to restore the prior state.
5. If another page links back to my_events.html with a highlightEventId URL parameter, the highlight handler component scrolls to and visually highlights that event, if it exists in the loaded data.

View a saved event in the calendar:
1. User clicks a "View in Calendar" action for a specific saved event.
2. The component derives the month and year from the event's start_datetime.
3. The browser navigates to calendar.html?month={derivedMonth}&year={derivedYear}.
4. The My Events page itself does not call any additional interfaces for this navigation; it only constructs the URL parameters from the loaded event data.

Remove an event from My Events:
1. User clicks a remove/delete control for a saved event in the list or grouped view.
2. The remove confirmation component opens, showing the selected event's title (fetched from the current in-memory data, not hardcoded).
3. If the user cancels, the dialog closes and no API calls are made.
4. If the user confirms, the component calls removeSavedEvent with the savedEventId for that saved event.
5. While waiting for the response, the UI can show a local pending state for that event (e.g., disabling the remove button) without altering the underlying data yet, or can optimistically hide the event, depending on implementation.
6. On success (success === true), the controller removes that saved event from the in-memory list and re-renders the current view, reapplying filters, sort, grouping, and pagination.
7. On failure (success === false), the controller restores the event to the list if it was removed optimistically and displays the returned message in the error notification component.

Use filters and pagination via URL parameters:
1. User adjusts filters (e.g., selects a category_label or audience_label) in the toolbar.
2. The toolbar sets or updates URL parameters such as category and audience. It also resets page to the first page when filters change.
3. The controller reads the updated URL, recalculates filtered results client-side from the already loaded events, and re-renders the views.
4. User changes pages using the pagination component; this updates the page URL parameter.
5. On page parameter changes, the controller calculates which subset of filtered events belongs to that page and displays only those events.
6. When the user shares or bookmarks the URL, the same filters, view mode, sort, and page state are restored when the page is opened again.
interactions:
Initial load:
- onInit (page load): Parse URL parameters (view, category, audience, sort, page, highlightEventId).
- Call getMyEvents().
- onGetMyEventsSuccess: Store the array returned by getMyEvents in memory and trigger the rendering pipeline.
- onGetMyEventsError: Show an error notification with a retry action.

Toolbar interactions:
- onChangeViewMode (click view toggle): Update the view URL parameter and re-render the appropriate view component (list or grouped).
- onChangeFilter (select/deselect filters): Update category and/or audience URL parameters, reset page, and re-render with filtered events.
- onChangeSort (change sort order control): Update sort URL parameter and re-render the event lists with new ordering.
- onClearFilters (click "clear filters"): Remove filter-related URL parameters and re-render all events.

List and grouped view item interactions:
- onClickEventTitle: Navigate to event_detail.html?eventId={event_id}.
- onClickViewInCalendar: Derive month and year from the event's start_datetime and navigate to calendar.html?month={month}&year={year}.
- onClickRemove: Open the remove-event-confirmation component for the targeted saved event.
- onPageChange (pagination control): Update the page URL parameter and re-render the currently selected view with the events for that page.

Remove event confirmation interactions:
- onOpen: Receive saved_event_id and associated event data from the list/grouped view.
- onConfirm: Call removeSavedEvent(savedEventId) and enter a loading/pending state within the dialog or associated event row.
- onRemoveSuccess: Close the dialog, update the in-memory list of events by removing the deleted saved event, and re-render the views.
- onRemoveError: Keep the event in the list or restore it if optimistically removed, display the error message returned by removeSavedEvent, and allow the user to retry or cancel.

Error and retry interactions:
- onGetMyEventsError: Display an error notification with the option to retry.
- onClickRetry (error notification): Call getMyEvents again.
- onRemoveErrorDismiss: User dismisses a remove-error message; no further action is required unless they retry the removal.

Highlight and deep-link interactions:
- onUrlChange or onInit: If highlightEventId is present as a URL parameter, the highlight handler searches the in-memory events for the matching saved_event_id or event_id (based on the chosen convention).
- If a matching event is found, the view scrolls to that event and applies a temporary highlight state (purely client-side behavior).
- If no matching event is found, no highlight is applied, and the page behaves normally.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The My Events page uses URL query parameters to manage view, filtering, sorting, pagination, and optional highlighting, without relying on localStorage or other persistent client storage. On every load or URL change, the page controller re-derives its state solely from the query string and the data returned by getMyEvents.

Key URL parameters and effects:
- view: Controls how events are displayed. Expected values can include `list`, `week`, or `month`. If omitted, an implementation-defined default is used (not hardcoded here).
  - view=list: The saved-events-list-view component renders a flat, sorted list of all matching saved events for the current page.
  - view=week: The saved-events-grouped-view component groups events in memory by week ranges derived from each event's start_datetime, then displays those groupings with pagination applied at the group or event level (implementation choice).
  - view=month: The saved-events-grouped-view component groups events by calendar month and year derived from start_datetime.

- category: Optional filter by category_label. The toolbar reads all category_label values present in the loaded events to build filter options dynamically (no hardcoded categories). When category is set, only events whose category_label matches the parameter value are included in the rendered result set.

- audience: Optional filter by audience_label. Like category, available values are derived from the loaded data. When audience is set, only events whose audience_label matches are included.

- sort: Optional sort configuration. The implementation can interpret values such as `date_asc` or `date_desc` (or similar), but the exact tokens are not hardcoded here. When sort is not provided, a default sort (e.g., by start_datetime ascending) is applied in memory. Changing sort updates this parameter and triggers a re-render without additional API calls.

- page: Controls which subset of the filtered results is displayed. The pagination component and controller compute the total number of pages based on the number of filtered events and a configured page size. When page is missing, the first page is used by default. When the user changes pages, the page parameter is updated so that reloading or sharing the URL returns the same slice of results.

- pageSize (optional): If supported, this parameter can define how many events are displayed per page. If omitted, a system-level default is used, not specified here to avoid hardcoded values.

- highlightEventId (optional): Used for deep-link scenarios to focus on a specific saved event. When present, after getMyEvents completes and the current view is rendered, the event-highlight-handler attempts to find the corresponding event and brings it into view. This parameter does not affect which events are loaded or filtered; it only affects focus and temporary visual highlighting.

Data loading and mutation flow:
- The page never encodes event data directly in the URL; it only uses identifiers and state parameters. All event details (title, dates, labels) come from getMyEvents.
- The only mutation operation on this page is removeSavedEvent, which removes a single saved event by its savedEventId. After a successful removal, the controller updates the in-memory data and re-applies the same URL-derived state (view, filters, sort, page) to recalculate the visible subset.
- The page does not cache results across sessions using localStorage or similar; every full load calls getMyEvents to obtain fresh data. Any consistency between reloads is maintained by reusing URL parameters, not by client-side persistence.

components.functionality: Central controller for the My Events page. On initialization and on any URL change, it parses URL parameters (view, category, audience, sort, page, pageSize, highlightEventId), calls getMyEvents to retrieve the user's saved events, stores the returned array in memory, and orchestrates filtering, sorting, grouping, pagination, and highlighting logic. It passes derived subsets of events and current state down to child components and receives events back (e.g., remove requests) to call removeSavedEvent and update the in-memory data accordingly.

Provides controls for view mode selection (list/week/month), filtering (category_label and audience_label), sorting (e.g., by date), and clearing filters. Does not call APIs directly. Instead, it derives available filter values from the event data supplied by the controller and updates URL parameters when the user changes any control, which in turn causes the controller to recompute visible events.

Renders the currently visible slice of saved events as a flat, ordered list when view=list. For each saved event, it displays the title, formatted date and time range derived from start_datetime and end_datetime, location, audience_label, and category_label. It also exposes actions to open event details, view the event in the calendar, and remove the event from My Events. It receives from the controller the already filtered, sorted, and paginated subset of events relevant to the current page.

Displays saved events grouped either by week or by month, depending on the current view state (view=week or view=month) provided by the controller. It organizes the in-memory list of filtered events into groups using start_datetime and renders group headings (e.g., a week label or month/year label) with the events underneath. Each event row supports the same interactions as in the list view: open details, view in calendar, and remove. Pagination is applied across groups or events based on configuration, controlled by the controller.

Shows a confirmation dialog when the user attempts to remove a saved event. Receives the saved_event_id and associated display fields (such as title) from the invoking view component. On confirmation, it triggers the controller to call removeSavedEvent with the given savedEventId. It manages a local loading state while awaiting the result and displays error feedback if removal fails, based solely on the message returned by the interface.

Displays contextual messaging when there are no events to show in the current state. It is shown either when getMyEvents returns an empty array (no saved events at all) or when filters result in zero matching events. It receives from the controller information about whether the emptiness is due to filters or genuinely no saved events, and can conditionally suggest that the user visit other pages (e.g., the main calendar) via simple navigation links without any additional interfaces.

Handles display of errors when getMyEvents fails or when removeSavedEvent returns success=false. For load errors, it shows a non-blocking error message with an option to retry loading. For removal errors, it can display the error message returned by removeSavedEvent and allow the user to retry the removal from the same event row or dialog.

Manages pagination for both list and grouped views. Receives from the controller the total number of items or groups and the current page derived from URL parameters. When the user selects a different page, it updates the page URL parameter so that the controller recalculates which events to show and so that the current page is preserved when the URL is shared or reloaded.

Implements deep-link highlighting behavior based on the highlightEventId URL parameter. After getMyEvents has succeeded and the appropriate view component has rendered, it searches the currently loaded events for a matching identifier and, if present, scrolls that event into view and applies a temporary highlight state. This component does not fetch data or mutate the backend; it only operates on the client-side state provided by the controller.

layout.grid_system: Centered fixed-width content column likely based on a 12-column grid (~1080–1200px wide) within full-bleed horizontal bands.
layout.layout_pattern: Within the existing institutional header/nav stack, the My Events page content sits in a centered fixed-width column (≈1080–1200px). Inside this column, components follow a strictly linear top-to-bottom flow: breadcrumb and global page banner (from the system) sit above, then the page H1 'My Events', followed by a short explanatory line, then the my-events-toolbar as a horizontal control bar. Below the toolbar, the main data region displays either the saved-events-list-view or the saved-events-grouped-view (never both), or, alternatively, the empty-state-message or load-error-notification in the same slot. The events-pagination is anchored at the bottom of the content column. The remove-event-confirmation appears as an overlay centered on top of all content. Invisible controllers (my-events-page-controller, event-highlight-handler) don’t occupy visible space.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Within the existing institutional header/nav stack, the My Events page content sits in a centered fixed-width column (≈1080–1200px). Inside this column, components follow a strictly linear top-to-bottom flow: breadcrumb and global page banner (from the system) sit above, then the page H1 'My Events', followed by a short explanatory line, then the my-events-toolbar as a horizontal control bar. Below the toolbar, the main data region displays either the saved-events-list-view or the saved-events-grouped-view (never both), or, alternatively, the empty-state-message or load-error-notification in the same slot. The events-pagination is anchored at the bottom of the content column. The remove-event-confirmation appears as an overlay centered on top of all content. Invisible controllers (my-events-page-controller, event-highlight-handler) don’t occupy visible space.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Roughly the top 40% of the viewport is already consumed by the utility bar, global nav, banner, and tabs inherited from the mockup. The My Events content occupies the remaining ~60%. Inside that content portion, the H1, explanatory text, and toolbar collectively use about the top 20–25% of the content area, while the main events display area (list or grouped view) occupies ~60–70%, with pagination and status messages at the bottom using the remaining ~10–15%. All components align to the left edge of the content column and span 80–100% of its width, leaving consistent side margins. Vertical spacing between major sections (H1, toolbar, main list, pagination) follows the 24–32px rhythm; spacing between smaller elements (labels, buttons, rows) follows 8–16px steps.
layout_strategies.content_density: The header stack remains relatively dense; the My Events content balances clarity and efficiency. The toolbar is compact but legible, with controls arranged in one row where possible, using 8–16px padding and tight but readable gaps. The event rows in the list and grouped views are moderately compact to keep several items visible above the fold, but with enough vertical padding (8–12px) to avoid crowding. Status messages and the confirmation dialog are more spacious, with greater padding to distinguish them as special states.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The user’s eye lands on the global banner/tab area, then drops to the left-aligned breadcrumb and the 'My Events' H1. From there the eye moves down to the short explanatory text and directly into the my-events-toolbar, scanning left to right across its controls (view toggle, date filters, etc.). Below this, the eye follows a vertical scan through event items in either list or grouped view. Pagination anchors the bottom of the column, giving a clear sense of completion. When present, the remove-event-confirmation interrupts this flow as a centered modal, while inline error or empty states are visually centered within the content column but still align text to the left, maintaining the institutional, F-pattern reading flow.

layout.overall_description: Inside the familiar, top-heavy institutional frame of utility bar, blue nav, gray banner, and tabs, the My Events page content forms a clean, single-column stack centered on the page. Just below the breadcrumb and large 'My Events' heading, a brief line explains what this section does, followed immediately by a horizontal toolbar that stretches nearly the full width of the content column. This toolbar provides view toggles and filters in a compact, blue-accented bar that feels like a functional counterpart to the site’s primary navigation. Below it, the main body is a solid block of event content: either a straightforward vertical list of saved events or a grouped-by-date display with subtle group headers. Each event row spans most of the column width with consistent left alignment, while secondary metadata and controls (like remove or open details) sit to the right within the same row. If there are no events or if something goes wrong, a clearly framed message takes over this central area without breaking the overall grid. At the very bottom of the column, pagination controls sit as a final horizontal strip, mirroring the top-heavy bar pattern in a lighter, less dominant way. When a user chooses to remove an event, a centered modal dialog appears on a dimmed backdrop, temporarily overriding the linear flow but still maintaining the conservative, rectangular, bar-based rhythm of the design.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This is an invisible data-loader/controller that does not render any visual UI. Spatially, it is considered to wrap the entire My Events content column, controlling what is shown inside the main content area (toolbar, list/grouped view, status messages, pagination). It introduces no extra padding or margins and does not alter the page’s visual layout beyond determining which of the child components is visible at any moment.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual wrapper; no footprint in the layout. It only orchestrates which visual components occupy the main content region.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The toolbar appears directly below the page’s H1 'My Events' and a short explanatory line, all within the centered content column. It occupies a single horizontal strip spanning approximately 100% of the content column width, leaving the standard left and right page margins. Vertically, it has 12–16px of padding above and below its controls, creating a compact but substantial bar. The toolbar content is aligned in one line on larger screens: primary controls (e.g., view toggle between 'List' and 'Grouped', and a date range selector) are left-aligned, while secondary controls (e.g., sort, clear filters) are right-aligned, creating a subtle tension similar to the header’s logo-left/search-right pattern. There is 24px of vertical space above the toolbar (from the explanatory text) and 24–32px below it before the list/grouped view begins, visually anchoring it as the functional header for the events area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the scrollable content; it scrolls away with the page and does not stick to the top.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When active, the saved-events-list-view occupies the main body of the content column directly below the toolbar, replacing any grouped view or status messages. It spans roughly 100% of the column width, with each event row aligning to the left margin and extending toward the right margin. Each event row is a horizontal bar with 12–16px vertical padding and 16–24px horizontal padding inside its own container, echoing the flat, rectangular cards seen in institutional layouts. The event title and date sit on the left, forming a strong vertical reading line; secondary actions such as 'View details' and a 'Remove' control are right-aligned in the same row, matching the left-to-right F-pattern. Rows are separated by 8–12px of vertical spacing or a thin divider line to keep density moderate. The list block continues downward until the bottom of the viewport and beyond, with 24–32px of space below the last row before the pagination component, preserving the clear hierarchy of stacked sections.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Occupies a single, continuous vertical region; it scrolls naturally as the main content. It never overlaps other components and is fully replaced by grouped view or status displays when those states are active.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When the user selects a grouped mode, this component takes over the same main content region used by the list view, sitting directly below the toolbar and above the pagination. It spans the full width of the content column and mirrors the list’s left alignment and margins. Events are organized into vertical groups by date or timeframe (e.g., 'This Week', 'Next Month'). Each group begins with a group header line: a left-aligned label in a slightly stronger weight and perhaps a muted blue background strip, with 8–12px of padding above and below, separated from the previous group by 24px of vertical space. Under each header, individual events appear as rows similar in height and padding to those in the list view, aligned left with titles and dates, and right-aligned actions. The grouping headers create a stepped visual rhythm down the page, but the overall block still reads as one continuous vertical column, respecting the institutional F-pattern and maintaining moderate density.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Occupies the same vertical slot as the list view. Only one of the two (list or grouped) is visible at a time. It scrolls with the page and does not introduce any fixed or floating elements.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The remove-event-confirmation appears as a modal dialog centered within the viewport, overlaying the entire My Events content and slightly dimming the background while preserving the sense of the fixed-width content column beneath. The dialog box itself occupies roughly 30–40% of the viewport width on desktop and is vertically centered, with 24–32px of padding inside. The title and confirmation message are top-aligned within the dialog, left-justified, and separated by 8–16px of vertical spacing. Action buttons ('Cancel', 'Remove') are aligned horizontally at the bottom right of the dialog with 16px spacing between them, mirroring the right-aligned controls pattern in the rest of the page. There is ample whitespace around the text inside the dialog so it feels calm and deliberate compared to the denser list content below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed-position overlay centered in the viewport with a semi-opaque backdrop covering the entire page. It temporarily blocks interaction with underlying content but does not shift or resize the underlying layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The empty-state-message is displayed in the main content region when the user has no saved events, occupying the same position as the list/grouped view—directly below the toolbar and above where the pagination would normally appear. The message block is centered within the content column horizontally but keeps all text left-aligned for an institutional feel. It uses generous padding (24–32px) within a subtle, light-gray bordered box or lightly tinted background that spans about 70–80% of the column width, leaving comfortable side margins. A clear, bold heading (e.g., 'No saved events yet') sits at the top of the box, followed by a short explanatory paragraph and, optionally, a primary action link or button (e.g., 'Browse events') aligned left with 12–16px vertical spacing between lines. There is 32px of vertical space above and below this box to emphasize that it is a distinct state, not just another row.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Replaces the list or grouped view entirely in the content area. It scrolls normally with the page and does not overlap other elements.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The load-error-notification appears in the same main content slot beneath the toolbar whenever event data cannot be loaded. It spans roughly 100% of the content column width but is visually contained within a horizontal alert strip or box using an institutional error color (e.g., muted red) for the left border or icon, while keeping the background mostly neutral to stay consistent with the conservative design. The text is left-aligned, with a short, bold error title and a single line of guidance stacked vertically with 8px spacing. The entire alert has 12–16px of vertical padding and 16–24px horizontal padding, mirroring the proportions of nav bars and list rows. It sits with about 24px of vertical spacing from the toolbar above and from any subsequent fallback content (like a 'Retry' action) below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears inline within the content column, pushing other components down if present. It does not float or overlay; in an unrecoverable error, it may be the only visible component in the main content area besides the toolbar.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The events-pagination component sits at the very bottom of the My Events content column, directly beneath the list view or grouped view (or is hidden when empty/error states represent the terminal view). It is a compact horizontal strip spanning approximately 100% of the content column width, with its controls (page numbers, previous/next) centered or right-aligned according to the site’s pagination pattern. There is 24–32px of vertical space separating it from the event rows above, making it clearly distinct but still visually tied to the list. The hit areas for page links use 8–12px vertical and 12–16px horizontal padding, creating small but usable rectangular buttons that echo the navigation items’ sizing. Below the pagination, there is at least 32px of bottom padding before the end of the main content area, ensuring comfortable scroll termination.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static at the bottom of the content area; it scrolls out of view as the user moves up the page. It does not stick to the viewport edge.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This behavior controller has no visible structure of its own. It subtly affects the appearance of items inside the list or grouped views—for example, by adding a light blue hover background, a left border accent on the focused row, or a brief highlight on newly saved events. These effects are confined within the existing dimensions of each event row and do not alter layout spacing or alignment. The visual pattern remains flat and rectangular, consistent with the institutional aesthetic.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual; it modifies styles of existing elements without taking up space or changing the structural layout.

name: Staff Directory
title: Staff Directory
description: Searchable directory of school staff where families can filter by grade level and subject/role, sort results, and open individual staff profile pages.

core_features: Load available grade, subject, and sort options using getStaffDirectoryFilterOptions and bind them to filter controls.
Read optional URL parameters (gradeLevel, subject, sortBy) and initialize the filter and sort state from them.
Search the staff directory using searchStaffDirectory with the currently selected filters and sort option.
Display a list of matching staff members with key fields such as name, role title, grade levels, primary subject, years of experience, room, and email when available.
Allow users to adjust filters (grade level, subject) and sort order, then re-run searchStaffDirectory and update the URL parameters to reflect the new state.
Provide clickable navigation from each staff member entry (name or photo) to teacher_profile.html with the staffMemberId URL parameter.
user_workflows:
Workflow: Initial page load without preset filters
1. User navigates to staff_directory.html without URL parameters.
2. The page calls getStaffDirectoryFilterOptions to retrieve available grade_levels, subjects, and sort_options.
3. The page initializes internal state: grade filter = none selected, subject filter = none selected, sort option = the first option returned in sort_options.
4. The page constructs filters from this state (no gradeLevel, no subject) and sort_by from the chosen default sort option.
5. The page calls searchStaffDirectory with these filters and sort_by.
6. The staff results list displays all returned staff members.
7. User can scroll through results; clicking a staff member’s name or photo opens teacher_profile.html with staffMemberId set to that staff member’s id.

Workflow: Initial page load with filters from another page
1. User clicks a link on index.html which navigates to staff_directory.html with some combination of gradeLevel, subject, and/or sortBy URL parameters.
2. On load, the page reads gradeLevel, subject, and sortBy from the URL query string.
3. The page calls getStaffDirectoryFilterOptions.
4. Once filter options are loaded, the page matches the URL values (if present) against the available grade_levels, subjects, and sort_options:
   - If a URL value matches an available option, that option is selected in the corresponding control.
   - If a URL value is missing or does not match any option, the corresponding control falls back to an unselected state (for grade/subject) or to the first sort_options entry (for sortBy).
5. The page constructs a filters object using the selected gradeLevel and subject (if any) and a sort_by value from the resolved sort option.
6. The page calls searchStaffDirectory with these resolved filters and sort_by.
7. Results display according to the pre-selected filters and sort order, matching the intent of the link from index.html.

Workflow: User refines search using filters
1. While viewing the Staff Directory, the user opens the grade selector and chooses a grade level from the options populated by getStaffDirectoryFilterOptions.
2. The user optionally opens the subject selector and chooses a subject from the provided options.
3. When the user clicks a "Search" or "Apply Filters" button in the filter form:
   - The page updates the URL query string to include gradeLevel and/or subject corresponding to the selected options.
   - The page also updates or adds the sortBy parameter to represent the current sort option.
   - The page constructs a filters object from the selected gradeLevel and subject and identifies the active sort_by.
   - The page calls searchStaffDirectory with these filters and sort_by.
4. The staff results list updates to show only staff matching the selected grade and subject.
5. The filter controls remain in sync with the current URL parameters, so reloading or sharing the URL preserves the filter state.

Workflow: User changes sort order
1. The user opens the sort dropdown control populated from sort_options returned by getStaffDirectoryFilterOptions.
2. The user selects a different sort option (for example, a different order based on experience or name as defined by the options returned).
3. On change or on submission of the filter form (depending on implementation), the page:
   - Updates the sortBy parameter in the URL to the selected sort option’s value.
   - Constructs filters using the currently selected gradeLevel and subject (if any).
   - Calls searchStaffDirectory with the current filters and the new sort_by value.
4. The staff results list re-renders in the new sort order.
5. If the user reloads the page or shares the URL, the selected sort option is restored based on the sortBy parameter.

Workflow: Clearing filters
1. The user clicks a "Clear Filters" control.
2. The page resets the grade and subject selections in the filter controls to their unselected states.
3. The page updates the URL to remove gradeLevel and subject parameters while leaving sortBy intact (or optionally resetting sortBy to the default first sort option).
4. The page constructs filters with no grade and no subject and uses the active sort_by.
5. The page calls searchStaffDirectory with these filters and sort_by.
6. The staff results list refreshes, showing all staff members according to the current sort order.

Workflow: Opening an individual staff profile
1. In the staff results list, the user identifies a staff member they want to view.
2. The user clicks on either the staff member’s name or photo in the result item.
3. The click event handler reads the staff member’s id from the searchStaffDirectory result item.
4. The browser navigates to teacher_profile.html with a query parameter staffMemberId set to this id (e.g., teacher_profile.html?staffMemberId=<id>).
5. The teacher profile page (outside this spec) uses staffMemberId to display detailed info and contact options.

Workflow: Handling empty or error states
1. After calling searchStaffDirectory, if the returned array has no items:
   - The staff results component displays a functional "no results" message and may provide a control to clear filters or modify them.
2. If getStaffDirectoryFilterOptions or searchStaffDirectory encounters an error (e.g., network or interface failure):
   - The page displays a functional error message and a "Try Again" action.
   - If the user clicks "Try Again", the page re-invokes the last attempted interface call using the current filter and sort state derived from the URL and in-memory state.
interactions:
Interaction: Page initialization
- Trigger: DOMContentLoaded or equivalent page-load event.
- Behavior: Read URL parameters (gradeLevel, subject, sortBy); call getStaffDirectoryFilterOptions; once options are loaded, resolve selected filters and sort based on URL; call searchStaffDirectory; render results.

Interaction: Grade filter change
- Trigger: onChange on the grade selector within the filter form.
- Behavior: Update internal selected gradeLevel state; wait for form submission (or trigger immediate search, depending on UX design); this value is later written to the URL and passed in filters to searchStaffDirectory.

Interaction: Subject filter change
- Trigger: onChange on the subject selector.
- Behavior: Update internal selected subject state; wait for form submission (or trigger immediate search); later synced to URL and filters for searchStaffDirectory.

Interaction: Sort option change
- Trigger: onChange on the sort selector.
- Behavior: Update internal sortBy state; immediately update the URL’s sortBy parameter; call searchStaffDirectory with current filters and the new sort_by; update results.

Interaction: Filter form submission
- Trigger: onSubmit of the filter form (e.g., user clicks "Search" or presses Enter).
- Behavior:
  1. Prevent default form submission behavior.
  2. Construct a new URL query string with gradeLevel and subject based on selections, and sortBy based on the current sort selection.
  3. Use pushState/replaceState or a full reload to apply the new URL.
  4. Call searchStaffDirectory with a filters object derived from these values and sort_by from the selected sort option.
  5. Update the staff results list with the returned data.

Interaction: Clear filters
- Trigger: onClick on a "Clear Filters" button.
- Behavior: Reset internal gradeLevel and subject selections to unselected states; update URL to remove gradeLevel and subject params (and optionally reset sortBy); call searchStaffDirectory with no gradeLevel or subject filters; refresh results.

Interaction: Retry loading data
- Trigger: onClick on a "Retry" button shown when an error occurs on getStaffDirectoryFilterOptions or searchStaffDirectory.
- Behavior: Re-invoke the failed interface using the same filters and sort_by derived from current URL and internal state; update components based on response.

Interaction: Open staff profile
- Trigger: onClick on a staff member’s name link or photo within a result item.
- Behavior: Navigate browser to teacher_profile.html with staffMemberId set to the clicked staff member’s id as returned by searchStaffDirectory.

Interaction: Direct URL editing / navigation
- Trigger: User manually edits URL parameters (gradeLevel, subject, sortBy) or visits a saved/bookmarked URL.
- Behavior: On next page load, these values are read and used in exactly the same way as if they came from prior filter interactions: filter controls and results are initialized based on them.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The page’s authoritative state is derived from URL query parameters and data returned by the assigned interfaces; no persistent localStorage or similar mechanisms are used.

URL Parameters and State Mapping:
- gradeLevel (string, optional):
  - Represents a selected grade level filter.
  - On load: If present, the page attempts to match its value to one of the grade_levels options returned by getStaffDirectoryFilterOptions.
  - If matched: The corresponding option is selected in the grade filter control, and filters.grade_level is set when calling searchStaffDirectory.
  - If not matched or absent: The grade filter control is left unselected, and no grade level is passed in filters.

- subject (string, optional):
  - Represents a selected subject filter.
  - On load: If present, the page attempts to match its value to one of the subjects options returned by getStaffDirectoryFilterOptions.
  - If matched: The corresponding option is selected in the subject filter control, and filters.subject is set when calling searchStaffDirectory.
  - If not matched or absent: Subject filter control is left unselected, and no subject is passed in filters.

- sortBy (string, optional):
  - Represents the selected sort key.
  - On load: If present, the page attempts to match its value to one of the sort_options returned by getStaffDirectoryFilterOptions.
  - If matched: The corresponding option is selected in the sort control, and sort_by is set to this value when calling searchStaffDirectory.
  - If not matched or absent: The sort control defaults to the first entry in sort_options, and sortBy in the URL may be updated to that value when the user first submits or changes filters.

Derived State and Re-computation:
- The page maintains in-memory state for the selected gradeLevel, subject, sortBy, and the current results array.
- Any change in filters or sort triggers:
  1. A URL update reflecting the new gradeLevel, subject, and sortBy.
  2. A call to searchStaffDirectory with:
     - filters: an object including only those filter properties that are currently selected (e.g., grade_level, subject).
     - sort_by: the selected sortBy value.
  3. A re-render of the staff results based on the new data.

Error and Empty States:
- If getStaffDirectoryFilterOptions fails, filter controls display an error state and a retry action; no searchStaffDirectory call is made until filters are available.
- If searchStaffDirectory returns an empty array, the results list component displays a functional "no results" message based on the current filters (derived from the URL) but does not modify the URL itself.

Navigation State:
- Clicking on a staff member does not alter filters or sort state; it only triggers navigation to teacher_profile.html with staffMemberId from the selected result item.
- Navigating back from the profile page to the Staff Directory (e.g., browser back button) returns the user to the prior filter and sort state because it is encoded in the URL query parameters and re-applied on load.

components.functionality: Handles initial page setup: reads URL parameters, loads filter options via getStaffDirectoryFilterOptions, resolves selected filters and sort, and triggers the initial searchStaffDirectory call.
Provides interactive controls for selecting grade level, subject, and sort order, and applies these selections to search the directory and update the URL parameters.
Displays the list of staff members returned by searchStaffDirectory, including key fields and actions. Handles empty and loading states.

Represents a single staff member entry, showing name, role_title, grade_level_labels, primary_subject_label, years_of_experience, email, room_number, and photo_url when available, and providing a navigation link to the teacher profile.
Displays a functional message and optional action when searchStaffDirectory returns no staff members for the active filters.
Handles and displays errors from getStaffDirectoryFilterOptions or searchStaffDirectory and provides retry actions.

layout.grid_system: Centered fixed-width content column likely based on a 12-column grid (~1080–1200px wide) within full-bleed horizontal bands.
layout.layout_pattern: Below the existing stacked header bars (utility bar, main nav, banner, tabs), the Staff Directory page continues the linear top-to-bottom flow. The main content stays in a centered fixed-width column (~1080–1200px) within full-bleed background bands. Inside this column, the order is: breadcrumb and page title block (page-init), then a single, compact horizontal filter bar, followed by the results list area, which either shows staff result items in a single column or, alternatively, an empty or error state message occupying the same space. No sidebars are used; any secondary visual (such as a small school logo) remains right-aligned within the same row as the H1, mirroring the mockup pattern.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Below the existing stacked header bars (utility bar, main nav, banner, tabs), the Staff Directory page continues the linear top-to-bottom flow. The main content stays in a centered fixed-width column (~1080–1200px) within full-bleed background bands. Inside this column, the order is: breadcrumb and page title block (page-init), then a single, compact horizontal filter bar, followed by the results list area, which either shows staff result items in a single column or, alternatively, an empty or error state message occupying the same space. No sidebars are used; any secondary visual (such as a small school logo) remains right-aligned within the same row as the H1, mirroring the mockup pattern.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Roughly the top 40% of the viewport is occupied by the inherited header stack (logo bar, nav, banner, tabs). The remaining 60% shows the Staff Directory content. Within this content area, the page-init section (breadcrumb, H1, optional intro) uses about the top 20–25% of the content height above the fold, the filter bar sits just below as a low-height band, and the rest of the visible area is dedicated to the results list. Horizontal content sits within the central column at about 70–80% of the viewport width, leaving generous left/right margins. Vertical separation between major bands (page-init, filter, results) is ~24–32px, while spacing between text elements and list items is tighter (~8–16px), following the 8px base grid.
layout_strategies.content_density: Density remains moderate and practical. The page-init section is open enough for the H1 to feel prominent without large hero-style whitespace. The filter bar is visually compact but organized, with controls placed in a single horizontal row where possible. The results list uses card-like rows that are tall enough for comfortable legibility but keep information compact so several staff entries are visible on first scroll. Line height and padding follow the 8–16px rhythm to balance readability with efficient scanning.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow preserves the F-pattern. After the visitor’s eye completes the header region, it lands on the left-aligned breadcrumb and large 'Staff Directory' page title. Any right-aligned logo in that row completes the top horizontal scan. The eye then drops to the filter bar, scanning from left (grade/role filters) to right (keyword search and sort). From there, attention moves vertically down the list, starting consistently at the left edge of each staff row (photo and name), and then skimming horizontally across to secondary details and the 'View Profile' action on the right. Empty and error states still respect this pattern by anchoring their main message to the upper part of the results area, slightly left-of-center but visually balanced horizontally.

layout.overall_description: Below the existing institutional header stack, the Staff Directory lives inside a centered, fixed-width content column that sits on a neutral white or very light background. At the top of this column, a narrow breadcrumb aligns to the left, followed by a strong, left-aligned 'Staff Directory' H1 that occupies most of the column width. A small, right-aligned school crest or logo can share this row, taking up about one quarter of the width and reinforcing the formal identity without overwhelming the title. A brief introductory sentence or two sits below the title, flush left, tightening the context. About 24–32px beneath this block, a full-width but low-profile filter bar stretches across the column, rendered as a subtle light-grey or bordered strip. Within it, filters start on the left and progress rightward to a keyword search and sort control, forming a single, orderly horizontal band. Below another 24–32px of vertical space, the main results area unfolds as a vertical stack of staff entries, each row spanning the column with a photo and name aligned on the left and a right-aligned 'View Profile' action, separated by clear but not excessive spacing. When no staff match the filters or when an error occurs, a message panel appears in this same area, centered in the content column but anchored higher rather than dead-center in the viewport, ensuring it’s seen immediately after reading the filter bar. Overall, the page feels structured, predictable, and information-first, echoing the conservative school/government aesthetic of the original mockup.

component_layouts.layout_narrative:
The page-init section is the first element within the main content column, positioned directly below the tabbed navigation band from the global layout, with approximately 24–32px of vertical whitespace above it. It sits inside the centered fixed-width column (~1080–1200px), which occupies roughly 70–80% of the viewport width on desktop, leaving balanced margins on both sides.

At the very top of this section, a single-line breadcrumb (e.g., 'Home > Our School > Staff Directory') is left-aligned, using a smaller, subdued text style. It spans about 60–70% of the content column horizontally and has 8–12px of space below it before the page title.

Immediately beneath the breadcrumb, the large 'Staff Directory' H1 appears, left-aligned and visually dominant. The H1 text block occupies roughly 70% of the content column width. On the same horizontal row, right-aligned and vertically centered to the H1 baseline, there is optional space for a small school logo or crest image. This image occupies about 20–25% of the column width and is constrained in height so it does not exceed the height of the title line; there is a 16–24px horizontal gap between the end of the H1 text block and the logo to keep them visually distinct.

Below the title row, separated by approximately 8–16px of vertical space, a brief descriptive paragraph or two (e.g., explaining what the directory is and how to use it) runs across roughly 70–80% of the column width, aligned left and wrapping normally. The bottom of this introductory text has 24–32px of vertical spacing before the filter bar begins, keeping this section clearly separated as a framing block.

Overall, this component reads as a unified block that anchors the page, with a clear top-left starting point (breadcrumb), a strong visual anchor (H1), and optional right-side emblem, echoing the composition seen in the original mockup’s event page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static; it scrolls normally with the page and is not fixed or sticky. It always appears immediately after the global header and tab bars.

component_layouts.layout_narrative:
The filter bar sits directly beneath the page-init section, separated from the introductory text by approximately 24–32px of vertical whitespace. It spans the full width of the main content column (about 70–80% of the viewport), and is presented as a low-height horizontal band with a subtle visual definition—either a very light grey background or a 1px border on the top and bottom—consistent with the conservative, flat UI style.

Inside this band, content is arranged in a single horizontal line on desktop, respecting the F-pattern. The vertical padding is 8–12px (top and bottom) and the horizontal padding is 16px from the left and right edges of the column.

From left to right, the layout is: a small, muted label such as 'Filter by:' followed by the Grade Level dropdown, then the Subject/Role dropdown, each control separated by 16px of horizontal space. The dropdowns are medium-width (each taking approximately 15–20% of the column), with compact heights and 8–12px internal padding. To the right of these, there is a keyword search input that is slightly wider, occupying around 25–30% of the column width. Finally, aligned to the far right, sits a Sort dropdown (e.g., 'Sort by: Last name A–Z'), taking about 15% of the width. The rightmost controls align with the right edge of the content column, preserving a clean vertical edge.

If the viewport narrows, controls naturally wrap to a second line while maintaining left alignment and consistent vertical spacing (8–12px between rows of controls), but on standard desktop sizes they remain on a single row, emphasizing a continuous, scannable filter bar. The whole bar has about 24px of space below it before the results list begins, clearly separating it as an interactive control zone.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static; it scrolls with the page and is not sticky. It always appears directly between the page-init content and the results list area.

component_layouts.layout_narrative:
The results list occupies the main body of the page below the filter bar. It begins approximately 24–32px under the bottom edge of the filter band, and continues downward as a single vertical column of result items within the same centered content column.

The list area stretches the full width of the content column, leaving only the column’s inherent side margins. There is no additional background panel; instead, the list sits directly on the page’s base background (white or very light neutral), with each result item visually separated by individual borders or spacing. The top of the list aligns visually with the left edge of the filter controls, maintaining a clean vertical rhythm.

By default, the list is configured to display one staff-result-item per row, each spanning 100% of the column width. Vertical spacing between result items follows the 8–16px rhythm: for example, a 1px divider line or subtle shadow plus 16–24px of vertical gap between the content blocks. The list is tall and scrollable; as users move down the page, rows extend downward, preserving consistent left alignment of photos and names to support rapid scanning.

If there are no results or an error, this region is occupied instead by the empty-state or error-state component, which appears in place of the stack of items but respects the same left alignment and column width, with its content centered horizontally and positioned in the upper portion of the results area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static; the list scrolls naturally as content grows. It acts as a container region for either multiple staff-result-item components or a single state-indicator component (empty or error).

component_layouts.layout_narrative:
Each staff result item appears as a horizontal row that spans the full width of the content column, resembling a clean, flat card without heavy decoration. The item has a white background matching the page (or an extremely subtle off-white) with a 1px light-grey bottom border or a 16px vertical gap to separate it from the next item. Internal padding follows the spacing system: approximately 16–24px on the left and right, and 12–16px on the top and bottom.

The internal layout is a two-column horizontal structure. On the left, occupying roughly 65–70% of the row’s width, is the main staff information block. On the right, occupying about 30–35%, is the primary action and any secondary actions, aligned to the right edge.

Within the left block, at the far left, a staff photo or avatar is placed. The image is typically square (e.g., 64×64px or 72×72px), aligned vertically to the top of the text block. There is a 16px horizontal gap between the photo and the text. To the right of the photo, the staff member’s name appears as a bold, slightly larger line at the top. Immediately below the name, separated by 4–8px, is a line for their role/position (e.g., 'Grade 3 Teacher', 'Music Specialist'), and below that, in smaller text, optional details such as grade level, room number, or department. Contact information (email, phone) appears either on the same line as the role (if short) or as a following line with 4–8px spacing, maintaining a compact block of 3–4 lines total, aligned left.

On the right side of the row, vertically center-aligned relative to the text block, is the primary action 'View Profile' rendered as a styled text link or a minimal button consistent with the site’s straightforward aesthetic. This action is right-aligned, with 0–8px spacing from the right padding of the card. If secondary actions appear (e.g., an email icon), they are placed to the left of the primary action with 8–12px of horizontal separation. This right block maintains a clear vertical alignment across items, creating a neat column of actions when scanning down the page.

Overall, each item is dense enough that 3–5 rows are visible above the fold (depending on screen height), but the use of consistent padding and hierarchy preserves legibility and the institutional, no-frills feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static; each item is part of the scrolling results list. It does not float or stick, and maintains consistent width with the content column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative:
The empty-state component appears within the same spatial region as the results list when no staff match the current filters. It respects the central content column width and alignment, beginning at the same vertical position where the first staff-result-item would normally appear—approximately 24–32px below the filter bar.

Rather than centering vertically in the viewport, the empty-state is anchored in the upper portion of the results area to stay immediately visible after users adjust filters. The content is horizontally centered within the column but has a slight left bias to maintain the F-pattern: for example, an icon aligned slightly to the left, followed by text that spans the central 60–70% of the column.

The structure typically includes: a simple, small illustrative icon or symbol (optional) at the top, sized around 40–64px high, followed by 16px of vertical spacing, then a short, bolded headline such as 'No staff found for these filters.' This headline is center-aligned within the column. Beneath the headline, separated by 8–12px, a concise explanatory sentence or two suggests next steps (e.g., 'Try clearing some filters or searching by last name.'). Below this, if present, a 'Clear filters' text link or minimal button is centered, with 16–24px of vertical spacing from the explanatory text.

The entire empty-state block uses modest padding (e.g., 24–32px top and bottom relative to its content) to feel like a cohesive message, but it does not extend to a full-height panel. This keeps the page feeling consistent with the standard list layout and avoids disrupting the institutional, utilitarian character.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static; it occupies the results-list area when triggered by an empty result set and scrolls with the page like any other content block.

component_layouts.layout_narrative:
The error-state component also resides within the results list region, replacing result items when a loading or server error occurs. Its horizontal placement, width, and initial vertical position mirror that of the empty-state: it begins around 24–32px below the filter bar, inside the centered content column, ensuring it appears where users expect to see results.

Visually, it is slightly more prominent than the empty state through the use of a stronger accent aligned with the site's institutional style—such as a left-aligned red or dark-alert-colored vertical bar (4px wide) or a small warning icon at the start of the message. The primary error headline (e.g., 'We’re having trouble loading staff information.') is left-aligned within the column and sits to the right of the icon/indicator, with about 8–12px horizontal spacing between them. Below the headline, separated by 4–8px, a short body line explains the issue in plain language and offers a next step (e.g., 'Please refresh the page or try again in a few minutes.').

Below the explanatory text, aligned to the left with 16–24px of vertical spacing, is an action such as a 'Retry' button or link. This layout preserves the F-pattern by giving a clear left-hand starting anchor and then letting the eyes move to the right as needed, rather than centering everything.

The error-state block uses modest vertical padding (around 16–24px above and below its content) and may be enclosed in a lightly tinted background that spans the width of the content column, differentiating it from normal list content while remaining visually conservative and in line with the institutional design language.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static; it appears inline in the results-list area and scrolls with the rest of the page content, disappearing when the error condition is resolved and normal results can be shown.

name: Teacher Profile
title: Teacher Profile
description: View an individual teacher’s information and send a secure message from a parent or guardian to that teacher.

core_features: Read the staffMemberId URL parameter and load the corresponding teacher profile using getTeacherProfile.
