Display teacher information including name, role title, grade levels taught, subjects, years of experience, room number, and biography.
Show basic contact details for the teacher (email and/or phone) as read-only reference information.
Provide a secure parent/guardian contact form (parent/guardian name, student name, email, message) bound to the current teacher profile.
Validate the contact form fields and submit messages via sendTeacherMessage, associating the message with the staffMemberId of the loaded teacher.
Display inline submission status (loading, success, or error) based on the response from sendTeacherMessage.
Offer navigation back to the Staff Directory and optional links to view other staff with similar grade level or subject filters on the staff directory page.
user_workflows: View a teacher profile: 1) User clicks a teacher entry on staff_directory.html, which opens teacher_profile.html?staffMemberId=... 2) On page load, the staffMemberId is read from the URL. 3) The page calls getTeacherProfile(staffMemberId). 4) While waiting, a loading state is shown. 5) When the profile data is returned, the page displays the teacher’s name, role, grades taught, primary subject, years of experience, room, biography, and contact info. 6) The contact form is initialized and bound to this teacher.

Send a message to the teacher: 1) After the teacher profile is visible, the user scrolls to the contact form. 2) The user enters parent/guardian name, student name, email, and message text. 3) On submit, the form validates required fields and email format. 4) If validation passes, the page calls sendTeacherMessage with the current staffMemberId and the entered form values. 5) While waiting, the submit button is disabled and a sending state is shown. 6) On success, the form shows a success confirmation using the returned status/message and may clear or reset fields. 7) On error, an error message is displayed, and the user can correct or retry.

Return to staff directory or see related staff: 1) User clicks the "Back to Staff Directory" link, which navigates to staff_directory.html (no additional parameters required). 2) Optionally, the page can show links like "See other teachers for this grade" or "See other teachers for this subject" that send the user to staff_directory.html with grade and/or subject as URL parameters derived from the loaded teacher profile.
interactions: On page load, the application reads the staffMemberId query parameter and triggers getTeacherProfile(staffMemberId).
If staffMemberId is missing or invalid, the page shows an error state and a link back to staff_directory.html instead of calling getTeacherProfile.
Users can click on the teacher’s email address (if provided) to open their email client via a mailto link as an alternative contact method (this does not replace the secure form).
Users type into the contact form fields (parent/guardian name, student name, email, message). Each input updates the in-memory form state.
On contact form submit, client-side validation checks that all required fields are filled and that the email appears valid.
If validation passes, sendTeacherMessage is called; during this call a loading indicator is shown and duplicate submissions are prevented.
If sendTeacherMessage returns success, the form displays a success message and can optionally clear inputs or keep them with a visible confirmation.
If sendTeacherMessage returns an error (success=false or network error), an error message is displayed and the form returns to editable state.
Clicking the "Back to Staff Directory" navigation link takes the user to staff_directory.html without persisting any local state.
Clicking optional related-staff navigation links (if present) takes the user to staff_directory.html with query parameters based on the current teacher’s grade levels or primary subject.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary state for this page is determined by the staffMemberId URL parameter. When teacher_profile.html loads, it parses staffMemberId from the query string. If staffMemberId is present, the page enters a 'loading profile' state and calls getTeacherProfile(staffMemberId). If the API call succeeds and returns a StaffMember, the page enters a 'profile loaded' state, where all profile components receive the StaffMember data. If the API call fails or returns no matching teacher, the page enters an 'error: profile not found' state and displays an appropriate message with a link back to staff_directory.html. If staffMemberId is absent from the URL, the page immediately enters an 'error: missing staffMemberId' state and does not call getTeacherProfile. If the user reaches this page again with a different staffMemberId in the URL (e.g., via navigation or browser back/forward), the controller re-runs the initialization: it re-reads staffMemberId, clears any previous teacher and form state, and calls getTeacherProfile for the new ID. The contact form’s submit logic always uses the current staffMemberId from the URL/controller state when invoking sendTeacherMessage; it does not rely on hidden or stored values to avoid stale associations. The form maintains its own transient state (field values, validation errors, submission status) in memory only for the current page view; no localStorage or cross-page persistence is used. Message submission results (success or error) are not encoded into the URL; refreshing the page clears the form and any transient status, but the teacher profile will reload based solely on the staffMemberId parameter.

components.functionality: Coordinates initial page load, reads the staffMemberId query parameter, loads the teacher profile via getTeacherProfile, manages loading and error states, and provides the resulting StaffMember data and staffMemberId to child components.

Displays the loaded teacher’s key profile information, such as full name, role title, primary subject label, grade level labels, and optional profile photo. Provides at-a-glance identification of the teacher whose profile is being viewed.

Shows detailed information about the teacher, including biography text, years of experience, grade levels taught, subjects taught, room number, and department if available. This component reads from the StaffMember data but does not modify it.

Displays the teacher’s contact preferences as basic reference details by showing the email address and/or phone number from the StaffMember data, if available. The email address is optionally rendered as a mailto link and the phone number as a tel link to support direct contact, without interfering with the secure message form.

Provides a secure message form for parents/guardians to contact the currently loaded teacher. Collects parent/guardian name, student name, email, and message body. Validates input, then calls sendTeacherMessage with staffMemberId from the controller and the entered values. Manages its own local state for field values, validation errors, and submission status (idle, submitting, success, error).

Displays real-time status messages related to the contact form submission. Shows loading indicators while sendTeacherMessage is in progress, a confirmation message when a TeacherMessage is successfully sent (based on the response status), or an error message if the submission fails.

Provides navigation controls to leave the teacher profile. Always includes a "Back to Staff Directory" link to staff_directory.html. Optionally includes links that navigate to staff_directory.html with query parameters derived from the current teacher’s grade_levels or primary_subject (e.g., filters for similar staff), without loading any additional data on this page.

Handles and displays error states related to loading the teacher profile. If staffMemberId is missing from the URL, or if getTeacherProfile fails or returns no result, this component shows an appropriate message and a link back to staff_directory.html. When an error is active, it hides or disables profile-dependent components such as the details section and contact form.

layout.grid_system: Centered fixed-width content column likely based on a 12-column grid (~1080–1200px wide) within full-bleed horizontal bands.
layout.layout_pattern: The page keeps the mockup’s strict top‑to‑bottom structure. Above the fold, global elements (utility bar, main nav, banner, tabs) sit exactly as in the mockup. The Teacher Profile page content then begins in a single centered column below the tab bar. Inside this column, the sequence is: breadcrumb (global, not one of the specified components), then the teacher-profile-controller as the logical wrapper, which visually presents the teacher-summary-header first (teacher name and key facts, with a right-aligned photo area echoing the mockup’s right-aligned school image). Directly below that, the teacher-details-section presents narrative content (bio, teaching philosophy, schedule overview). Below the narrative, the contact stack appears: teacher-contact-info (static contact info), teacher-contact-status (conditional inline alert), then the teacher-contact-form (primary interaction). staff-navigation-links sits at the very bottom of the column for moving to other staff pages. profile-error-state is reserved for error scenarios and appears near the top of this column, directly under the breadcrumb and above all other content, replacing or pushing down normal sections as needed.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page keeps the mockup’s strict top‑to‑bottom structure. Above the fold, global elements (utility bar, main nav, banner, tabs) sit exactly as in the mockup. The Teacher Profile page content then begins in a single centered column below the tab bar. Inside this column, the sequence is: breadcrumb (global, not one of the specified components), then the teacher-profile-controller as the logical wrapper, which visually presents the teacher-summary-header first (teacher name and key facts, with a right-aligned photo area echoing the mockup’s right-aligned school image). Directly below that, the teacher-details-section presents narrative content (bio, teaching philosophy, schedule overview). Below the narrative, the contact stack appears: teacher-contact-info (static contact info), teacher-contact-status (conditional inline alert), then the teacher-contact-form (primary interaction). staff-navigation-links sits at the very bottom of the column for moving to other staff pages. profile-error-state is reserved for error scenarios and appears near the top of this column, directly under the breadcrumb and above all other content, replacing or pushing down normal sections as needed.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The global header, banner, and tab strip still occupy roughly the top 40% of the viewport on first load, consistent with the mockup. The main Teacher Profile column is centered within a fixed-width content area about 1080–1200px wide. Within this column, the teacher-summary-header plus its bullets occupy the upper part of the remaining 60% of the viewport, with the teacher name and key facts spanning roughly 70–80% of the column’s width and a modest right-aligned portrait/image taking up the remaining 20–30%. The teacher-details-section then takes up most of the visible content area below, followed by the contact information and form, which together will typically extend beyond the initial viewport height. Vertical spacing between major sections (summary header, details, contact, staff navigation) follows the 24–32px rhythm from the mockup, with tighter 8–16px spacing between related elements (e.g., between heading and bullets, between form fields).
layout_strategies.content_density: Content density remains moderate, in line with the institutional feel. Text is compact enough to keep the teacher name, key facts, and at least the beginning of the details section visible without excessive scrolling on a typical desktop. Line lengths are controlled by the fixed-width column to maintain readability. The summary header and bullets are tight but readable, similar to the mockup’s event details. The contact form fields are stacked with 8–12px gaps and 16–24px breathing room around form sections, avoiding a cramped feel but still signaling a practical, information-focused interface rather than an airy marketing page.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow mirrors the F-pattern of the mockup. The eye falls from the global nav and tabs to the top-left of the content column (breadcrumb and Teacher Profile context), then immediately down to the large teacher name in the teacher-summary-header. It scans horizontally across that row, noticing secondary information to the right (teacher portrait or icon, similar to the right-aligned school image in the mockup). The eye then drops down to the short bullet list of key teacher facts aligned under the name, before continuing vertically through the narrative teacher-details-section. After reading the narrative, attention naturally moves to the contact area: first to the static teacher-contact-info summary, then to any teacher-contact-status alert (when present, its color and bar-like appearance will briefly dominate), and finally to the teacher-contact-form button and fields. staff-navigation-links at the bottom gives a subtle left-aligned exit path back to the staff list or next/previous profiles without competing with the primary content.

layout.overall_description: Below the familiar stack of horizontal global bars (utility strip, main blue navigation, a subdued banner, and a tab row), the Teacher Profile page content sits in a centered, fixed-width column that visually matches the rest of the site. At the very top of this column, a breadcrumb runs left to right, followed by a comfortable gap before the main content. The teacher’s name appears as a large, left-aligned heading in the teacher-summary-header, occupying roughly the left 70–75% of the content row, while a modest rectangular area on the right (about 25–30%) can show a small teacher photo or neutral placeholder, echoing the mockup’s right-aligned school mark. Directly below the name, a compact list of 3–5 bullet points summarizes key details such as grade, homeroom, and room number, aligned flush left and contained to about 70–80% of the column width so they feel like a quick fact summary rather than full-width body text. After a clear vertical separation, the teacher-details-section presents a few paragraphs of narrative information in a single column, with headings and subheadings stepping down in size from the H1 and generous line spacing for readability. Beneath this, the page transitions into the contact zone: a small teacher-contact-info block first outlines formal contact channels, then, when applicable, a teacher-contact-status bar appears just above the teacher-contact-form, spanning the full text width like a flat notification strip. The contact form itself is a simple vertical stack of labeled fields and a prominent submit button aligned to the left, occupying nearly the full column width while maintaining side margins intact. At the bottom of the page, the staff-navigation-links sit as a light-weight, text-based navigation row allowing parents to return to the staff list or switch to another teacher profile. If an error occurs (such as a missing profile), a prominent profile-error-state band appears directly under the breadcrumb, pushing the rest of the content down or replacing it entirely, ensuring clarity while still respecting the page’s overall structural rhythm. The end result is a conservative, orderly layout with a predictable top-to-bottom reading flow and a subtle horizontal accent at the teacher name and portrait row.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The teacher-profile-controller acts as the main content wrapper positioned directly below the global tab bar. It defines a centered content column roughly 1080–1200px wide within the full browser width, matching the site’s existing grid. The controller begins about 24–32px below the tab bar, with 24–32px of top padding before any internal content. Within this controller, content flows in a strict vertical stack: breadcrumb (global element, top-left), then a small 16–24px gap before the teacher-summary-header. Below the summary header and its bullet list, the teacher-details-section is separated by about 24px of vertical space, followed by the contact area (teacher-contact-info, teacher-contact-status, teacher-contact-form) each separated by 16–24px. Finally, the staff-navigation-links sit at the bottom with about 32px of top margin to visually signal the end of the main content. The controller ensures consistent left and right padding of 24–32px inside the column so that text never touches the edges, maintaining the institutional, grid-aligned feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level container that scrolls with the page and constrains all child components to a centered fixed-width column; no sticky or floating behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The teacher-summary-header occupies the topmost content position inside the teacher-profile-controller, immediately below the breadcrumb with about 16–24px of vertical separation. It is arranged as a two-column horizontal row within the main content width. On the left, approximately 70–75% of the width is devoted to text: a large H1 teacher name at the top, aligned flush with the left margin of the content column, followed by a short bullet or key fact list underneath. The H1 uses a size and weight analogous to the mockup’s main event title, with 8–12px of space below it before the bullet list begins. The bullet list is constrained to the same left text column width and presents 3–5 concise items with 8px vertical spacing between bullets, mirroring the compact event facts in the mockup. On the right, the remaining 25–30% of the row is reserved for an optional teacher image or avatar block, right-aligned and vertically centered relative to the H1 and bullet stack. This right block should be slightly narrower than the text column and inset with 16–24px of padding around any image, visually echoing the right-aligned ‘Fish Creek School’ mark in the mockup while remaining subordinate to the teacher name. The entire summary header section has 24–32px of bottom margin before the teacher-details-section begins, ensuring it reads as a distinct header band for the profile content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static horizontal row within the main column; the right-hand image area shrinks or hides responsively when space is constrained but does not float independently.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The teacher-details-section sits directly beneath the teacher-summary-header, separated by approximately 24px of vertical whitespace. It spans roughly 70–80% of the content column width, aligned to the left, leaving a comfortable right margin that visually aligns with the width of the bullet list above. Within this section, a medium-weight subheading such as “About” or “Class Information” can appear at the top, aligned with the left edge of the H1 above and spaced 8–12px above the first paragraph. Paragraphs of narrative text are stacked vertically with 8–16px between them, maintaining moderate density. There are no sidebars; all details are in a single, linear text column. Any short lists (e.g., subjects taught, weekly schedule highlights) appear as indented bullets with the same left alignment as the paragraphs, ensuring a continuous vertical reading line. The details section extends until its content ends, after which there is a 24–32px gap before the teacher-contact-info, visually indicating a transition from informational content to actionable contact content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block-level section that expands vertically based on content; scrolls naturally with the page and does not overlap other components.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The teacher-contact-info component is placed beneath the teacher-details-section with about 24px of vertical padding between them. It aligns flush left with the main text column and typically occupies around 70–80% of the content column width, mirroring the width of the details above so the left reading edge remains consistent. At the top of this block, a small, bold label such as “Contact Information” appears, with 8px of space below it. Under this label, key contact details (school phone, extension, general office email, and any policy-related notes such as response time or use of secure messaging) are displayed as short lines or a compact list with 4–8px vertical spacing between items. The visual treatment remains flat and informational—no heavy card borders—possibly with a subtle top rule or slightly tinted background if needed to separate it from the narrative, but keeping within the conservative aesthetic. There is a 16–24px gap below this contact-info block before the teacher-contact-status (if present) or directly before the teacher-contact-form if no status is shown, so the eye clearly perceives a step from static information to interactive messaging.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline block within the main vertical flow; expands vertically as needed and does not float or stick.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The teacher-contact-form is positioned directly below the teacher-contact-info (or below teacher-details-section if contact info is minimal or omitted), with approximately 16–24px of vertical spacing above it. When a teacher-contact-status message is visible, that status bar appears directly above the form’s heading, effectively separating the contact-info block from the form itself. The form spans nearly the full width of the content column (about 80–90% of the column width) while respecting consistent left and right margins, making it visually more dominant than the contact-info text while still feeling orderly. A descriptive form heading like “Send a Message” appears at the top-left of the form, followed by 8–12px of space before the first field. Each form field is stacked vertically with labels above inputs, both left-aligned; vertical spacing between individual fields is about 12–16px. Optional helper text or error text for each field appears directly below the corresponding input, using 4–8px gaps. The primary submit button is placed at the bottom-left of the form, with 16–24px of top margin above it to separate it from the last field, and 8–16px of bottom padding below the button before the form ends. The form maintains the site’s flat style—no heavy shadows or rounded card containers—relying on alignment, consistent spacing, and the button’s stronger color to convey hierarchy.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard scrolling form section; fields and button flow in document order with no sticky positioning. Height grows with content and validation messages.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The teacher-contact-status component appears as an inline notification band tied closely to the teacher-contact-form. When present, it is rendered directly above the form’s heading within the same content column, separated from the teacher-contact-info by about 8–16px of vertical space. It spans the same width as the form (approximately 80–90% of the content column), aligning exactly with the form’s left and right edges for a cohesive block. The status takes the form of a flat horizontal bar with a subtle background tint (e.g., pale green for success, pale red for error) and 8–12px of vertical padding and 16–24px horizontal padding inside. Text is left-aligned, with any status icon placed to the far left followed by the message text on a single line or wrapped across two short lines. The bar’s height is compact so it does not dominate the page but is still clearly noticeable as a feedback element. When absent, the space collapses completely and the form shifts up, preserving an efficient, moderate density layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Conditional inline block that appears and disappears without overlay; when rendered, it pushes the form downward in the vertical flow. No fixed or sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The staff-navigation-links component sits at the bottom of the teacher-profile-controller, following the teacher-contact-form by approximately 32px of vertical spacing to signal the end of primary actions. It spans the full width of the content column but keeps its text content aligned to the left, matching the main text column. Within this area, the primary link (e.g., “Back to Staff Directory”) appears at the far left, styled similarly to a standard text link or small button, while optional secondary links like “Previous Teacher” and “Next Teacher” may be aligned horizontally in the same row with 16–24px space between them. Alternatively, on narrower layouts, these secondary links can wrap to the next line while maintaining left alignment. The visual style is deliberately understated—no large buttons or heavy bars—so this navigation reads as a light, tertiary path rather than competing with the summary, details, or form. A small 16–24px bottom padding beneath this component ensures it does not feel cramped against the page’s bottom edge.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static navigation row at the end of the main content flow; scrolls naturally with the page and does not stick to the viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The profile-error-state component is positioned high in the content column to immediately inform the user of critical issues such as a missing or inaccessible teacher profile. It appears directly under the breadcrumb and within the teacher-profile-controller, taking the place of the usual teacher-summary-header when active. The error state spans the full usable width of the content column (approximately 90–100% within the column padding) and appears as a flat, horizontally oriented alert band with 16–24px of vertical padding and 24px horizontal padding inside. An alert icon is aligned to the left, followed by a clear error title and, if needed, one or two lines of explanatory text underneath or beside it, with 4–8px spacing between the title and description. Below the text, a primary or secondary action such as “Back to Staff Directory” may appear as a text link or subtle button aligned flush left. The component is separated from the breadcrumb by about 16–24px, and from any subsequent content (if displayed at all) by 24–32px. In the most common error scenario, the profile-error-state is shown alone and the other components (summary, details, contact info, form) are hidden, creating a concise, focused message while still respecting the page’s fixed-width, banded layout pattern.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Conditional inline alert at the top of the main content area; when active, it either pushes other components down or replaces them entirely. It scrolls with the page and does not overlay content.

name: Homework & Grading Policies
title: Homework & Grading Policies
description: Page for viewing, filtering, and bookmarking homework and grading policy sections by grade level, with in-page navigation to specific sections.

core_features: Load available grade levels for homework and grading policies using getPolicyGradeLevels and allow the user to select a grade.
Fetch and display all policy sections for the selected grade level using getPolicySectionsForGrade.
Show a section-based table of contents for the currently selected grade that lets users jump to any section on the page.
Display detailed policy content for each section, including any nightly homework time limits when provided by nightly_homework_minutes_limit.
Allow users to bookmark individual policy sections via bookmarkPolicySection and show a confirmation message with a link to view all bookmarks.
Support deep links into the page using URL parameters (gradeLevel, sectionKey, anchorId) to pre-select a grade and scroll to a specific section on load.
Keep the selected grade and (optionally) the focused section reflected in the URL so users can share or revisit the same view.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1: First-time visit without URL parameters
1. User navigates to homework_policies.html via site navigation.
2. On page load, the page calls getPolicyGradeLevels.
3. When grade levels are returned, the page selects a default grade (for example, the first available value from getPolicyGradeLevels) and displays it in the grade selector.
4. The page calls getPolicySectionsForGrade with the selected gradeLevel.
5. When sections are returned, the page:
   - Populates the table of contents sidebar with the list of sections (using section_title or section_key_label).
   - Renders each policy section in the main content area, including content and any nightly_homework_minutes_limit if present.
6. The user can click items in the table of contents to scroll to specific sections.
7. The user can click a bookmark action on any section to save it; a confirmation with a "View all bookmarks" link appears if bookmarkPolicySection succeeds.

Workflow 2: Arriving with a pre-selected grade (from index.html)
1. User clicks a link on index.html that points to homework_policies.html with a gradeLevel URL parameter.
2. On page load, the page reads gradeLevel from the URL.
3. The page calls getPolicyGradeLevels and waits for the response.
4. After grade levels are loaded, the page checks if the URL gradeLevel value exists in the list from getPolicyGradeLevels.
   - If it exists, that grade is selected in the grade selector.
   - If it does not exist, the page falls back to a default grade (e.g., the first returned value) and updates the URL accordingly.
5. The page calls getPolicySectionsForGrade with the resolved gradeLevel.
6. Sections for that grade are rendered, and the table of contents is built from these sections.
7. User can change the grade via the selector, which triggers a new call to getPolicySectionsForGrade and updates the gradeLevel parameter in the URL.

Workflow 3: Deep link from My Bookmarks (anchor or sectionKey)
1. User clicks a saved bookmark on my_bookmarks.html that links to homework_policies.html with some combination of gradeLevel, sectionKey, and/or anchorId parameters.
2. On page load, the page reads all three URL parameters (if present).
3. The page calls getPolicyGradeLevels, resolves the selected grade as in Workflow 2, and sets the grade selector to that grade.
4. The page calls getPolicySectionsForGrade with the resolved gradeLevel.
5. Once sections are loaded and rendered:
   - If anchorId is present in the URL, the page locates the section whose anchor_id matches anchorId and scrolls to that section.
   - Else, if sectionKey is present, the page finds the first section whose section_key matches sectionKey and scrolls to that section.
6. The focused section is visually distinguishable (e.g., highlighted functionally) while the user reads it.
7. The user can still change grade levels or navigate via the table of contents; when they do, the page updates the URL parameters accordingly (for example, updating gradeLevel and clearing sectionKey/anchorId when a new grade is selected).

Workflow 4: Bookmarking a policy section
1. User scrolls to a specific policy section for the selected grade.
2. User clicks the "Bookmark" control associated with that section.
3. The page calls bookmarkPolicySection with the section's id.
4. While waiting for the response, the UI can show a loading/disabled state for that section's bookmark control.
5. When the response returns:
   - If success is true, the page displays a confirmation banner or inline success state using the returned message and bookmark details.
   - The confirmation contains a link to my_bookmarks.html labeled "View all bookmarks"; clicking it navigates directly to my_bookmarks.html with no additional parameters.
   - The bookmark control for that section can show that it has been bookmarked during this session (e.g., a different label or state), based solely on the successful response (no additional interface calls).
   - If success is false, the page displays an error message derived from the response (if provided) and keeps the section unmarked.
6. The user may bookmark multiple sections in the same visit, triggering the same flow each time.

Workflow 5: Navigating within the page via the table of contents
1. After a grade is selected and sections are loaded, the table of contents shows a list of sections for that grade.
2. User clicks a section entry in the table of contents.
3. The page scrolls to the corresponding section in the main content area, using the section's anchor_id or another internal identifier.
4. Optionally, the URL is updated with anchorId and/or sectionKey to reflect the newly focused section (so that reloading the page or sharing the URL brings the user back to that section).
5. User can repeat this process to move between different sections.

Workflow 6: Emphasizing nightly homework limits
1. After sections are loaded for a grade, each section is rendered.
2. For any section where nightly_homework_minutes_limit has a value, the component displays that value in a clearly labeled area within that section (for example, "Nightly homework limit: [value] minutes"), using only the dynamic value from the API.
3. If nightly_homework_minutes_limit is not provided for a section, no limit is displayed for that section.
4. Users can compare expectations between grades by changing the grade selector, which triggers re-loading of sections and their associated nightly limits.
interactions:
Interaction: Grade selection
- Component: Grade selector (dropdown or similar control).
- Trigger: User changes the selected grade.
- Behavior:
  - Call getPolicySectionsForGrade with the newly selected gradeLevel value.
  - Clear or reset any previously focused section state (e.g., remove anchorId / sectionKey from internal state and from the URL).
  - Re-render the table of contents and section list for the new grade.
  - Update the URL's gradeLevel parameter to match the selected grade (without a full page reload, if possible).

Interaction: Initial page load
- Component: Page-level initialization logic.
- Trigger: Page is loaded.
- Behavior:
  - Read current URL parameters: gradeLevel, sectionKey, anchorId.
  - Call getPolicyGradeLevels.
  - Resolve the selected grade based on the URL and the available grade levels.
  - Call getPolicySectionsForGrade with the resolved gradeLevel.
  - Render grade selector, table of contents, and all sections.
  - After sections are rendered, if anchorId or sectionKey is present, scroll to the matching section.

Interaction: Table of contents click
- Component: Table of contents sidebar.
- Trigger: User clicks a section entry.
- Behavior:
  - Identify the corresponding PolicySection based on its id or anchor_id.
  - Scroll the main content area to that section.
  - Update internal state to mark this section as the current focus.
  - Update the URL parameters to include anchorId and/or sectionKey for the focused section, keeping gradeLevel intact.

Interaction: Bookmark button click
- Component: Bookmark control inside each policy section.
- Trigger: User clicks the bookmark button on a specific section.
- Behavior:
  - Call bookmarkPolicySection with the policySectionId of the clicked section.
  - While waiting, disable the bookmark button or show a loading state.
  - On success:
    - Display a confirmation message using the response's message and bookmark data.
    - Provide a "View all bookmarks" link that navigates to my_bookmarks.html.
    - Optionally mark that specific section as bookmarked in the current page state (e.g., changing the button label) based solely on the successful response.
  - On failure:
    - Show an error message and keep the bookmark button enabled for retry.

Interaction: "View all bookmarks" link
- Component: Bookmark confirmation message.
- Trigger: User clicks the "View all bookmarks" link within a success confirmation.
- Behavior:
  - Navigate the browser to my_bookmarks.html with no additional parameters, as specified in outgoing_connections.

Interaction: URL change (navigation and sharing)
- Component: Deep-link state manager.
- Trigger: Grade selection changes or table-of-contents section changes.
- Behavior:
  - Update the browser URL with the current gradeLevel and, when applicable, anchorId and/or sectionKey.
  - Do not trigger a full reload; instead, maintain current state and allow users to share or bookmark the URL.
  - On a hard reload or new visit using the same URL, the page uses these parameters again to reconstruct the same view.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The page state is controlled via in-memory UI state and URL query parameters only; localStorage is not used.

URL Parameters:
- gradeLevel (string):
  - Read on initial load. If present and found in the values returned by getPolicyGradeLevels, it becomes the selected grade in the grade selector and is used as the parameter to getPolicySectionsForGrade.
  - If absent or invalid, the page selects a default grade from the list returned by getPolicyGradeLevels and sets gradeLevel in the URL (for example, using history.pushState or a full reload when necessary).
  - Whenever the user changes the grade via the selector, the page updates the gradeLevel parameter in the URL and clears sectionKey and anchorId parameters to avoid inconsistent deep links.

- sectionKey (string):
  - Read on initial load. Used only after sections for the selected grade are loaded.
  - If provided and anchorId is not present, the page searches the PolicySection list returned by getPolicySectionsForGrade for the first section whose section_key matches sectionKey.
  - If a match is found, that section is scrolled into view and marked as the focused section.
  - When the user selects a different section from the table of contents, sectionKey may be updated to correspond to that newly focused section (if that section has a section_key) or removed when focusing a section that should not be addressed by sectionKey.

- anchorId (string):
  - Read on initial load. After sections for the selected grade are loaded, if anchorId is present, it has priority over sectionKey for determining the initial scroll position.
  - The page finds the section whose anchor_id matches anchorId and scrolls to it.
  - When the user clicks different sections in the table of contents or otherwise changes focus, anchorId is updated to correspond to the focused section's anchor_id, if available.

State Resolution Order on Load:
1. Read gradeLevel, sectionKey, anchorId from URL.
2. Call getPolicyGradeLevels, then determine selected grade:
   - If gradeLevel is valid, use it.
   - Otherwise, use a default grade from the returned list and update gradeLevel in the URL.
3. Call getPolicySectionsForGrade with the resolved gradeLevel.
4. After sections are loaded:
   - If anchorId is present and matches a section, scroll to that section.
   - Else if sectionKey is present and matches a section, scroll to that section.
   - Otherwise, leave the page at the top of the content.

Runtime State:
- Selected grade level: kept in component state and synchronized with the gradeLevel URL parameter.
- Loaded sections: an in-memory list of PolicySection objects returned by getPolicySectionsForGrade.
- Focused section: derived from either URL parameters (anchorId/sectionKey) on load or the last user interaction (e.g., last clicked table-of-contents item). This may be reflected back into URL parameters.
- Bookmark feedback state: per-section transient state based on the result of bookmarkPolicySection, not persisted outside this page except through the Bookmark data stored by the backend.

components.functionality: Initializes and manages the grade selection used to filter policies. On page load, it calls getPolicyGradeLevels to retrieve available grade levels, sets the current selection based on the gradeLevel URL parameter or a default from the returned list, and exposes the selected grade to other components. When the user changes the grade, it triggers a refresh of policy sections and updates the gradeLevel parameter in the URL while clearing sectionKey and anchorId.

Listens for changes to the selected grade level from the grade-filter-form component and calls getPolicySectionsForGrade with the selected gradeLevel. It maintains loading and error states, and when data is received, it stores the list of PolicySection objects for use by the table of contents and section list components.

Renders an in-page table of contents for the currently loaded PolicySection list. Each entry corresponds to a section (e.g., based on section_title or section_key_label) and allows users to click to navigate directly to that section. When a section is clicked, the sidebar notifies the deep-link-state-manager to scroll to the section, mark it as focused, and update URL parameters (anchorId and optionally sectionKey). The component updates its active entry based on the current focused section.

Displays all policy sections for the selected grade level in the main content area. For each PolicySection item provided by the policy-section-data-loader, it renders the section title, content, and a dedicated area for nightly_homework_minutes_limit when present. Each section is associated with its anchor_id so that other components (such as the table of contents or deep links) can scroll to it.

Represents a single policy section within the list. It binds to an individual PolicySection object and is responsible for:
- Rendering the section title (section_title).
- Rendering the main policy text (content).
- Displaying the nightly_homework_minutes_limit value when present, clearly labeled using only the dynamic value provided.
- Exposing data attributes or DOM ids consistent with anchor_id for deep linking.
- Embedding the bookmark control for this section and reporting click events to that control.

Provides the bookmark button/action for a single policy section. When invoked, it calls bookmarkPolicySection with the associated policySectionId. It manages the loading state of the bookmark request, interprets the response, and signals the bookmark-feedback-banner to show a confirmation or error. On success, it can set an in-memory flag so that the current section appears bookmarked during this session.

Displays confirmation or error messages after a bookmarkPolicySection call. On success, it uses the response message and bookmark.bookmark data to inform the user that the section has been bookmarked. It includes a "View all bookmarks" link that navigates to my_bookmarks.html when clicked. The banner can be global (top of page) or contextual, but it only reacts to data provided by bookmark-section-control.

Coordinates URL parameters with in-page state. On initial load, it reads gradeLevel, sectionKey, and anchorId from the URL and passes them to the grade-filter-form and policy-section-data-loader. After sections are loaded, it uses anchorId and/or sectionKey to instruct policy-section-list to scroll to the appropriate section. It also listens for events such as grade changes and table-of-contents clicks, updating the URL parameters accordingly (e.g., setting gradeLevel, anchorId, sectionKey) without using localStorage.

Coordinates the initial sequence of operations when the page loads. It triggers getPolicyGradeLevels, resolves the initial grade based on URL and available grades, invokes getPolicySectionsForGrade, and then notifies the deep-link-state-manager and other components when sections are ready. It ensures that scrolling to a section using anchorId or sectionKey happens only after both grade levels and sections have been successfully loaded.

layout.grid_system: Centered fixed-width content column likely based on a 12-column grid (~1080–1200px wide) within full-bleed horizontal bands.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the same top‑heavy, stacked-bar pattern as the mockup: utility bar, main nav, banner, and tab row occupy the upper part. Within the main content band, everything sits in a single centered fixed-width column (~1080–1200px). The intra-page order is: breadcrumb at the top-left of the content column, then a large H1 (“Homework & Grading Policies”), followed by a brief intro/description. Immediately beneath this, the grade-filter-form sits as a full-width horizontal control band. Below it, any transient bookmark-feedback-banner appears. The main content area is then divided into a simple two-region layout within the same content column: on the left, the policy-section-list (a vertical stack of policy-section-item blocks); on the right, an inline, right-aligned policy-toc-sidebar that feels like a secondary column but is still visually part of the same central content band. Non-visual components (deep-link-state-manager, initial-load-orchestrator) work behind the scenes, while the policy-section-data-loader temporarily replaces the policy-section-list region with a loading state when required.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the same top‑heavy, stacked-bar pattern as the mockup: utility bar, main nav, banner, and tab row occupy the upper part. Within the main content band, everything sits in a single centered fixed-width column (~1080–1200px). The intra-page order is: breadcrumb at the top-left of the content column, then a large H1 (“Homework & Grading Policies”), followed by a brief intro/description. Immediately beneath this, the grade-filter-form sits as a full-width horizontal control band. Below it, any transient bookmark-feedback-banner appears. The main content area is then divided into a simple two-region layout within the same content column: on the left, the policy-section-list (a vertical stack of policy-section-item blocks); on the right, an inline, right-aligned policy-toc-sidebar that feels like a secondary column but is still visually part of the same central content band. Non-visual components (deep-link-state-manager, initial-load-orchestrator) work behind the scenes, while the policy-section-data-loader temporarily replaces the policy-section-list region with a loading state when required.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Roughly the top 40% of the viewport is taken by the global site chrome (utility, nav, banner, tabs), consistent with the mockup. The remaining ~60% is the main content band. Within that main band, the breadcrumb, H1, and intro copy use the full width of the central column. The grade-filter-form also spans nearly the full inner width, with 24–32px of vertical spacing above and below to separate it from text. Below, the content column is split horizontally: the policy-section-list occupies about 70–74% of the column width on the left, while the policy-toc-sidebar occupies about 26–30% on the right, mimicking the ‘main content + small right element’ pattern seen in the mockup’s right-aligned school logo. Vertical spacing between major chunks (H1, filter, feedback banner, main content split, and between each policy-section-item) follows the 8px base scale, typically 24–32px for sections and 12–16px for inner padding.
layout_strategies.content_density: The density stays moderate, mirroring the institutional, information-focused mockup. Text lines are relatively compact but not cramped, with paragraphs and bullet lists occupying most of the width in the left main content region. The grade-filter-form is dense but orderly, with form controls placed in a single row where possible. The policy-section-list presents sections in well-defined blocks with clear headings, bullets, and explanatory paragraphs separated by 8–16px spacing. The policy-toc-sidebar remains visually lighter and less dense, using shorter labels and more whitespace to avoid competing with the main content. Overall, the layout prioritizes scannable information over airy visual drama.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow keeps the F-pattern from the mockup: users’ eyes enter from the top-left (site logo and utility), move across the global nav, then are anchored by the page banner and tabs. Inside the content, attention lands first on the breadcrumb and large H1 at the top-left of the content column. From there, the eye moves down to the grade-filter-form (a strong, functional band), then quickly scans right to notice the policy-toc-sidebar at the top of the main content region. Once a specific section is chosen via filters or TOC, focus returns to the left column where policy-section-items are read vertically. Bookmark-section-controls are aligned to the right of each section heading, picked up by a secondary horizontal scan across each section’s first line. Feedback from bookmarking appears just below the filter form, near the top of the content band, so users see it immediately after interacting.

layout.overall_description: Below the school’s standard stacked header bars (utility strip, main blue navigation bar, grey banner with the current section title, and blue tab row), the ‘Homework & Grading Policies’ page content sits in a centered fixed-width column on a neutral background. At the top of this column, a small breadcrumb runs along the left, followed by a large, bold H1 that anchors the page. A short explanatory sentence or two sits directly beneath the title. Just below this textual framing, a full-width grade filter band stretches across the column, containing dropdowns and controls for narrowing policies by grade. When a bookmark action occurs, a colored bookmark feedback banner briefly appears directly under this filter band, spanning the same width. Below this, the main content region is visually split into two adjacent areas within the same column: on the left, a tall vertical stack of policy sections, each with a clear heading, descriptive bullets, and paragraphs; on the right, a slimmer, right-aligned vertical table of contents listing links to each section. As users scroll, the left column presents section after section in a consistent rhythm of headings, body text, and a small bookmark control on the right edge of each heading row, while the right TOC quietly tracks position (and may stick subtly within its column). When content is loading or filters change, the left content area is temporarily replaced with a clear institutional-style loading state, keeping the structural layout intact. Overall, the page feels orderly, conservative, and easy to scan, mirroring the mockup’s linear, bar-based structure but adapted for interactive homework and grading policy navigation.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The grade-filter-form is positioned within the main content column directly below the H1 title and its short introductory text. It spans approximately 90–100% of the content column width, with 24–32px of vertical spacing above it (from the intro text) and 16–24px below it (before any bookmark-feedback-banner or the main content split). Visually, it reads as a horizontal control band: form controls (e.g., a ‘Grade’ dropdown, optional ‘Subject’ dropdown, and a ‘Apply’ or ‘Reset filters’ button) are aligned left-to-right in a single row where viewport width allows. Each field has 8–16px internal horizontal padding and 8–12px vertical padding, adhering to the 8px base spacing. On narrower widths within the fixed column, controls may wrap to a second line but maintain left alignment. The overall height of the filter band is compact yet substantial enough to signal importance, roughly 64–96px tall including padding. It uses the same conservative, flat styling as the site: simple borders, institutional blue for primary actions, and minimal decoration.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main content column; it does not float or stick on scroll. It defines the top of the interactive content area and remains directly below the H1 as the user scrolls past it.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The policy-section-data-loader occupies the same left-hand content area as the policy-section-list. When active (e.g., on initial load or after changing filters), it replaces the policy-section-list within the main content column while leaving the grade-filter-form and policy-toc-sidebar intact. Vertically, it begins about 16–24px below the grade-filter-form (or bookmark-feedback-banner if present) and extends downward as needed. Horizontally, it aligns with the left region that normally holds the section list, taking roughly 70–74% of the content column width, starting flush with the left edge of the content column and ending at the margin where the right TOC column begins. The loader may appear as a centered spinner with a short status line, or as 2–4 stacked skeleton blocks that mimic the size and spacing of policy-section-items, each block separated by 16–24px vertical gaps. The remaining right-hand area where the policy-toc-sidebar lives remains visible but may show a subtle ‘Updating…’ hint or disabled appearance to signal that content is refreshing, while the loader visually dominates the left reading area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears and disappears dynamically in the main content column. It is static (non-floating) while visible and always occupies the same region reserved for the policy-section-list, ensuring the layout does not jump horizontally during loading.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The policy-toc-sidebar is placed within the main content column but aligned to the right, forming a narrow vertical navigation strip beside the main content. It begins roughly 24–32px below the grade-filter-form (or bookmark-feedback-banner if present), at the same vertical level where the first policy-section-item begins on the left. Horizontally, it occupies about 26–30% of the content column width and is separated from the left content by a 24–32px gutter. Its top aligns with the top of the first section in the left column, creating a visual pairing. The sidebar itself is a simple, flat panel: a small heading like ‘Jump to section’ at the top, followed by a vertical list of links representing each policy section (e.g., ‘K–Grade 1 Homework’, ‘Grade 2–3 Grading’, etc.). Each link is full-width within the sidebar, with 8–12px vertical padding and 8–16px horizontal padding, separated by 4–8px. The sidebar background remains mostly neutral to avoid overpowering the left content, with subtle typographic hierarchy (bold for the current section, normal for others).
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a right-aligned, inline sidebar within the main content column. On taller pages, it can become sticky within the viewport: as the user scrolls, the sidebar’s top edge maintains an offset below the global header and tab bars (e.g., sticking ~24px below the tab row) while staying locked to the right of the content column. It does not extend beyond the height of the main content column and stops sticking near the footer area to preserve conservative, non-intrusive behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The policy-section-list occupies the primary left-hand portion of the main content region below the filter and feedback areas. It starts approximately 24–32px below the grade-filter-form or bookmark-feedback-banner and runs down the page in a single vertical column. Horizontally, it begins flush with the left edge of the central content column and extends to about 70–74% of the column width, leaving space on the right for the policy-toc-sidebar and a 24–32px gutter between them. Within this region, multiple policy-section-items are stacked top-to-bottom with 24–32px of vertical spacing between them, creating a clear rhythm. The list itself has no heavy bounding box; instead, each item is demarcated by its own heading, spacing, and possibly a light top border. The entire list aligns with the same grid as the rest of the page, maintaining consistent left margins and line lengths for comfortable reading.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the left portion of the main content column. It scrolls naturally as the user moves down the page, while the right-hand policy-toc-sidebar can remain partially in view if sticky behavior is enabled.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each policy-section-item is a content block within the policy-section-list’s left column. It spans the full width of that left column (roughly 70–74% of the content column width), starting at the shared left margin of the content column and ending near the gutter to the right sidebar. Vertically, each item uses a card-like structure: a section heading row at the top, followed by bullet points and paragraphs. The heading row has 8–16px of padding above and below, and may show a subtle bottom border or extra spacing to separate it from the body text. The bookmark-section-control is positioned on the far right of this heading row, aligning horizontally with the section title but right-justified within the item’s width. Beneath the heading row, the body content (key bullet facts and narrative text) is arranged in a single column, with bullets separated by 8–12px and paragraphs spaced 12–16px apart. Each entire section item is separated from the next by 24–32px of vertical spacing, giving each section a distinct visual block while maintaining a moderately dense academic feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static content blocks that scroll within the left content column. Each section item provides an anchor point for deep-linking; when navigated to from the policy-toc-sidebar or URL, the top of the item is aligned just below the stacked header bars so the section heading is clearly visible without being obscured.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The bookmark-section-control is a small, compact action button associated with each policy-section-item, visually tied to the section heading. It appears in the top-right corner of each section item’s header row, aligned vertically with the baseline of the section title text. Horizontally, it is right-justified within the section’s width, typically 8–16px from the right inner edge of the policy-section-item. The control might be represented as a star icon with an optional label like ‘Bookmark’ or a simple outlined button, sized to roughly match the height of the heading line (around 24–32px tall) with 8–12px horizontal padding. It maintains at least 8–12px of space from the heading text to its left to avoid crowding. This placement allows users scanning across the top line of a section to quickly spot the control without interrupting the left-aligned reading flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within each policy-section-item’s heading area. It scrolls with its section, and its state (bookmarked vs not bookmarked) is reflected in its icon/color but does not change the layout around it.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The bookmark-feedback-banner appears as a transient horizontal strip within the main content column, located just below the grade-filter-form. It spans the full width of the content column (matching the filter form’s width) and is centered within the page grid. Vertically, it has 12–16px of padding top and bottom and 16–24px left and right, making it slightly taller than a single line of text but still compact. There is 8–16px of vertical space between the bottom of the filter form and the top of this banner, and 16–24px between the bottom of the banner and the start of the main content split (policy-section-list and policy-toc-sidebar). The banner background uses a subtle tinted color (e.g., pale blue or green) consistent with the institutional style to indicate success or information, with clear left-aligned text. If an action button such as ‘View bookmarks’ exists, it is right-aligned within the same row, maintaining an 8–16px margin from the text. When hidden, the content below shifts up but the overall structure of the page remains intact.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears and disappears dynamically directly under the grade-filter-form. It is static while visible (no stickiness) and scrolls away naturally as the user moves down the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The deep-link-state-manager has no visible UI. Its ‘layout’ effect is expressed by how the page scrolls and positions elements when a deep link is used. When a URL includes a reference to a specific policy section, the manager ensures that the corresponding policy-section-item is scrolled to the top of the main content area such that its heading appears just below the stacked global header bars (utility, nav, banner, tabs). This often means adding an internal offset equal to the combined height of those bars plus a small buffer (e.g., 16–24px of breathing room) so headings are not hidden behind fixed elements. It also coordinates with the policy-toc-sidebar to highlight the matching entry, and with the bookmark-section-control states if applicable.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual stateful controller with no direct spatial footprint. Its behavior affects scroll position and active states of in-page navigation elements, but it does not render any visible component.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The initial-load-orchestrator is also non-visual; it influences which visual components appear first and in what sequence. On first page load, it ensures that the grade-filter-form renders immediately beneath the H1, then coordinates the policy-section-data-loader and policy-section-list so that either a loading state or initial content appears in the left content area below the form. It may also delay rendering of the policy-toc-sidebar until enough section data is available to populate it, preventing any empty or misaligned side panel. From a user’s perspective, the layout simply appears fully formed: breadcrumb and H1, then filter, then (if needed) a brief loading state where the section list will go, followed shortly by the filled-in list and the right-hand TOC.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual controller; no dedicated spatial region. It orchestrates the timing of when other visible components occupy their designated layout positions, ensuring the page does not flash incomplete structures.

name: My Bookmarks
title: My Bookmarks
description: Displays the user’s saved policy bookmarks and lets them quickly open or remove bookmarked sections.

core_features: Fetch and display the user’s current bookmarks using getMyBookmarks, including title snapshot and creation time.
Provide one-click navigation from each bookmark to the original policy page, scrolling to the bookmarked section when possible.
Allow users to remove individual bookmarks via removeBookmark with inline feedback.
Offer basic client-side tools (e.g., text search, sort order) for organizing and finding bookmarks, with settings reflected in URL parameters.
Highlight and auto-scroll to a specific bookmark when its id is provided in the URL parameters.
user_workflows: View all bookmarks: 1) User opens my_bookmarks.html (via header navigation). 2) On page load, the controller reads URL parameters (e.g., sort, q, selectedBookmarkId). 3) The page calls getMyBookmarks with no parameters. 4) The returned bookmarks are stored in page state. 5) The bookmarks list component applies search and sort rules based on URL parameters and renders the resulting list. 6) If no bookmarks are returned, an empty-state message is shown instead of the list.

Open a bookmarked policy section: 1) User clicks anywhere on a bookmark row (except the remove control). 2) The click handler reads that bookmark’s page_url and anchor_id. 3) The handler parses any existing query parameters from page_url (e.g., gradeLevel, sectionKey) if they are present. 4) The user is navigated to homework_policies.html with gradeLevel and sectionKey populated from the parsed URL when available, and anchorId set to the bookmark’s anchor_id (if present). 5) homework_policies.html uses these parameters to scroll or jump to the specific policy section.

Remove a bookmark: 1) User clicks the remove control for a bookmark row. 2) A confirmation interaction is shown (e.g., inline prompt or modal) using only client-side state. 3) If the user confirms, the page calls removeBookmark with the bookmark’s bookmark_id. 4) On success, the bookmark is removed from the local list state and the list re-renders. 5) If removeBookmark returns an error (success=false), an error message is shown and the bookmark remains in the list. 6) The current URL parameters (e.g., sort, q) remain unchanged.

Search within bookmarks: 1) User types into the search input in the toolbar. 2) On change or submit, the toolbar updates the q parameter in the page URL (e.g., my_bookmarks.html?q=homework&sort=newest) using a client-side navigation/update mechanism. 3) The page controller reads the updated URL, re-applies the search filter to the existing getMyBookmarks results (no new API call is required), and the list re-renders showing only bookmarks whose title snapshot or page_url matches the search term.

Change sort order: 1) User selects a sort option (e.g., newest or oldest) in the toolbar. 2) The toolbar updates the sort parameter in the URL (e.g., my_bookmarks.html?sort=oldest&q=currentTerm). 3) The page controller reads the new sort parameter and applies the corresponding sort to the in-memory bookmark list using created_at. 4) The bookmarks list re-renders in the new order.

Link to and focus a specific bookmark: 1) Another page (such as homework_policies.html) links to my_bookmarks.html with a selectedBookmarkId parameter in the URL. 2) On load, the page controller stores selectedBookmarkId from the URL. 3) After getMyBookmarks resolves, the list component locates the bookmark matching selectedBookmarkId, scrolls it into view, and visually highlights it using only client-side state. 4) If selectedBookmarkId does not match any bookmark, the list renders normally with no special highlight.
interactions: Header navigation click on "My Bookmarks": navigates to my_bookmarks.html without additional parameters, triggering an initial getMyBookmarks fetch and default sort state.
Search input change/submit in the toolbar: updates the q parameter in the URL and triggers re-filtering of the in-memory bookmark list.
Sort selector change in the toolbar: updates the sort parameter in the URL and triggers re-sorting of the in-memory bookmark list by created_at.

Bookmark row click (non-remove area): navigates the browser to homework_policies.html, building the destination URL by combining any existing query parameters from bookmark.page_url with anchorId set from bookmark.anchor_id when available.
Remove button click on a bookmark: opens a confirmation interaction in page state; if confirmed, calls removeBookmark with the bookmark’s bookmark_id.

Remove confirmation success: on a successful removeBookmark response, the corresponding bookmark is removed from the displayed list without a full page reload; if the removed bookmark was highlighted via selectedBookmarkId, the highlight state is cleared.
Remove confirmation failure: if removeBookmark returns success=false, an error message specific to that bookmark row is displayed; the bookmark remains visible.

Empty-state acknowledgement: when getMyBookmarks returns an empty array, the page displays an explanatory message and may provide a simple link back to the homework policies page (as a standard navigation link using URL parameters, not relying on any specific bookmark).
page_functionality.state_logic: The page does not use localStorage; all shareable state is represented via URL parameters and transient in-memory state. URL parameters supported include: sort (string; supported values determined by the implementation, such as 'newest' or 'oldest'; default applied when missing), q (string; current text search query used to filter bookmark titles and/or page_url), and selectedBookmarkId (string; used to identify a bookmark that should be scrolled into view and highlighted after getMyBookmarks completes). On initial load and on any history/navigation changes that affect these parameters, the page controller re-reads the URL, applies the corresponding filters/sorts/highlighting to the in-memory results of the most recent getMyBookmarks call, and re-renders child components. The getMyBookmarks interface is always called without parameters when the page is first loaded or when a full reload occurs; sort and filter are applied client-side so that changing URL parameters does not require new API calls. When navigating away to homework_policies.html, the page constructs the destination URL using bookmark.page_url as the base: query parameters present in bookmark.page_url (such as gradeLevel or sectionKey, if they exist) are parsed and preserved; anchorId is explicitly passed using bookmark.anchor_id when available. The page does not write any state back into the data models except through removeBookmark, which is called only when the user confirms bookmark removal.

components.functionality: Initializes the My Bookmarks page by reading URL parameters, fetching bookmark data, and distributing processed state (filtered, sorted, highlighted) to child components.
event_handlers: onPageLoad: calls getMyBookmarks(), reads sort, q, and selectedBookmarkId from the URL, stores raw results and URL-derived state in memory.
onUrlChange: listens for changes to URL parameters (e.g., via browser navigation) and re-applies sorting/filtering/highlighting to the existing bookmarks collection without recalling getMyBookmarks.
onBookmarksUpdated: receives new bookmark collections after removal and re-applies URL-based state before notifying child components.

components.functionality: Provides controls for searching within bookmarks and selecting sort order, and ensures these preferences are reflected in URL parameters.
event_handlers: onSearchInputChange: updates the q parameter in the URL to match the user’s current search text; triggers onUrlChange in the page controller.
onSearchSubmit: prevents default form submission and ensures q is set in the URL; designed for keyboard users who submit via Enter.
onSortChange: updates the sort parameter in the URL to the selected value; triggers onUrlChange in the page controller.
onClearSearch: removes the q parameter from the URL (or sets it to an empty string), causing the full bookmark list to be shown.

components.functionality: Displays the current collection of bookmarks returned from getMyBookmarks after client-side search and sort have been applied, and provides item-level actions for navigation and removal.
event_handlers: onItemRender: for each bookmark, applies visual highlighting if bookmark_id matches selectedBookmarkId; attaches click and remove handlers.

onBookmarkClick: when a bookmark row (excluding its remove control) is clicked, constructs the navigation URL to homework_policies.html using bookmark.page_url and bookmark.anchor_id, then triggers navigation.
onRequestRemove: when the bookmark’s remove control is clicked, notifies the bookmark-remove-controller to open a confirmation flow for the selected bookmark.
onNoResults: if the filtered list is empty while the raw getMyBookmarks results are non-empty, shows a 'no matches' message that depends only on current q and not on static data.

components.functionality: Handles the remove flow for bookmarks, including confirmation, calling removeBookmark, and updating shared bookmark state.
event_handlers: onRequestRemove: receives a bookmark_id from the bookmarks-list and opens a confirmation state (e.g., sets currentBookmarkToRemove in memory).
onConfirmRemove: calls removeBookmark with the currentBookmarkToRemove’s bookmark_id; waits for the API response.
onRemoveSuccess: when removeBookmark returns success=true, removes the corresponding bookmark from the in-memory collection and notifies page-init-controller so the bookmarks-list re-renders.
onRemoveFailure: when removeBookmark returns success=false, records the error message for that bookmark and notifies bookmarks-list so it can display an inline error.
onCancelRemove: clears currentBookmarkToRemove and closes any confirmation UI without calling removeBookmark.

components.functionality: Shows alternative content when there are no bookmarks to display, either due to the user having no bookmarks at all or due to filters removing all visible results.
event_handlers: onCheckEmptyAll: if getMyBookmarks returns an empty array, displays a message indicating there are no saved bookmarks and can provide a simple navigation link (using standard URL) back to the policies page.

onCheckEmptyFiltered: if raw bookmark data exists but the filtered list based on q is empty, displays a 'no matching bookmarks' message and may offer a control to clear the search, which triggers bookmarks-toolbar.onClearSearch.

components.functionality: Ensures that when selectedBookmarkId is present in the URL, the corresponding bookmark is scrolled into view and highlighted after data load.
event_handlers: onBookmarksLoaded: after getMyBookmarks completes, checks whether selectedBookmarkId corresponds to an existing bookmark; if so, instructs bookmarks-list to scroll that item into view and apply highlight state.
onSelectedBookmarkIdChange: if the URL’s selectedBookmarkId parameter changes while the page is open, updates the highlight and scroll behavior accordingly.
onHighlightClear: when the highlighted bookmark is removed or when selectedBookmarkId is removed from the URL, clears highlight state in bookmarks-list.

layout.grid_system: Centered fixed-width content column likely based on a 12-column grid (~1080–1200px wide) within full-bleed horizontal bands.
layout.layout_pattern: Inside the existing site shell (utility bar, global nav, banner, tabs), the My Bookmarks page uses a single centered content column on a 12‑column grid (~1080–1200px wide). Within that column the flow is strictly top‑to‑bottom: breadcrumb (from the template, above these components), then a large left‑aligned H1 'My Bookmarks', followed by a short introductory sentence. Directly under this, a horizontal bookmarks toolbar spans the content column, and beneath it the bookmarks list occupies the bulk of the page. When there are no bookmarks, an empty‑state message replaces the list in the same area. Any removal actions are integrated into the toolbar and list items rather than existing as a separate visual block.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Inside the existing site shell (utility bar, global nav, banner, tabs), the My Bookmarks page uses a single centered content column on a 12‑column grid (~1080–1200px wide). Within that column the flow is strictly top‑to‑bottom: breadcrumb (from the template, above these components), then a large left‑aligned H1 'My Bookmarks', followed by a short introductory sentence. Directly under this, a horizontal bookmarks toolbar spans the content column, and beneath it the bookmarks list occupies the bulk of the page. When there are no bookmarks, an empty‑state message replaces the list in the same area. Any removal actions are integrated into the toolbar and list items rather than existing as a separate visual block.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Roughly the top 40% of the viewport is occupied by the global header stack (utility bar, navigation, banner, tabs) as in the mockup. The remaining 60% holds the main content column. Within the content column, about the top 20–25% of that space goes to breadcrumb, H1, and intro text, around 10–15% to the toolbar, and the rest to the bookmarks list or empty state. All components obey the 8px spacing system: 24–32px vertical gaps between major blocks (H1 → toolbar → list), and 8–16px internal padding within the toolbar, list rows, and empty‑state panel.
layout_strategies.content_density: The page keeps a moderate content density consistent with an institutional, information‑driven site. The toolbar is compact but readable, with tightly grouped controls and 8–12px vertical padding. List items are stacked with clear separation but limited vertical padding so that several bookmarks are visible at once without scrolling excessively. Line lengths in the content column are kept to ~70–80% of the column width to maintain readability.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The user’s eye follows an F‑pattern: it lands on the global elements, then moves to the breadcrumb and large 'My Bookmarks' heading at the top left of the content column. From there, it scans right across the toolbar’s main controls (e.g., sort, filter, remove‑all) and then moves down into the vertically stacked bookmarks. Within each row, the eye starts at the left with the bookmark title, glances rightward to any metadata and remove controls, then drops down to the next row. When there are no bookmarks, the eye is anchored by a centered empty‑state message panel occupying the list area beneath the heading.

layout.overall_description: Below the site’s stacked header bars, the main content sits in a centered fixed‑width column on a light background, framed by generous side margins. At the top of this column, a breadcrumb in small text stretches across the width, followed by a strong, left‑aligned H1 'My Bookmarks' with ample white space above and about 16–24px below. A brief explanatory sentence sits just under the H1, aligned with the same left edge. Roughly 24px beneath the intro, a horizontal bookmarks toolbar runs the full width of the content column, appearing as a flat, light‑tinted bar with controls aligned in a single row. Directly under this toolbar, separated by about 16px, the bookmarks list fills most of the remaining vertical space as a single column of full‑width rows, each with the bookmark title on the left and remove actions tucked to the right. When there are no bookmarks, the list area is instead occupied by a centered, card‑like empty‑state message aligned to the same left margin as the heading but visually centered in the available vertical space, keeping the page feeling orderly and calm. All components scroll together as a single column, maintaining the conservative, government‑style visual rhythm of horizontal bands and clearly separated sections.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page-init-controller is non-visual and occupies no explicit space in the layout. Conceptually, it is anchored to the top edge of the main content column, just above the breadcrumb and H1 region, so that any initial focus or page-state logic is applied as soon as the content area loads. It does not alter spacing; the 24–32px top padding from the global tabs down to the breadcrumb remains governed by the page template.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: No visual footprint; behaves as an invisible controller associated with the top of the main content column and scrolls with the page as part of the normal document flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The bookmarks toolbar sits within the main content column, directly beneath the H1 'My Bookmarks' and its brief intro text. It begins approximately 24px below the last line of the intro, forming a clear horizontal band across 100% of the content column (about 70–80% of the full page width, centered). The toolbar’s height is determined by a single row of controls and about 8–12px of vertical padding above and below them, resulting in a strip roughly 48–56px tall. Controls are left-aligned following the page’s F-pattern: primary actions (e.g., a sort dropdown or filter) are grouped starting at the left margin of the text column, with secondary actions such as 'Remove all bookmarks' right-aligned within the same bar. Internally, controls are spaced using 8–16px gaps, but the outer edges align to the same left and right margins as the heading and list.
