There is about 16px of vertical space separating the bottom edge of the toolbar from the top of the bookmarks list or empty-state area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block-level section inside the content column; it scrolls normally with the page and is not sticky. It maintains a flat background (either white or a very light tinted band) to remain understated yet clearly distinct from surrounding text.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The bookmarks list occupies the main content area directly below the toolbar, starting about 16px under the toolbar’s bottom edge. It spans the full width of the content column, with list items aligned to the same left and right margins as the toolbar and H1. Each bookmark row is a horizontally oriented card-like line item approximately 56–72px tall, with 12–16px of horizontal padding inside the row. Within each row, the bookmark title is left-aligned in a bold or strong style taking up roughly the left two-thirds of the row; any secondary information (e.g., policy code or section path) appears below or to the right of the title in a smaller, lighter line. On the far right of each row, aligned vertically with the title, a 'Remove' link or icon button for the bookmark-remove-controller is positioned, occupying roughly the rightmost 15–20% of the row but visually subtle. Rows are separated by 8–12px of vertical gap to keep the list compact yet scannable. The list extends downward as needed, with the first 3–6 bookmarks typically visible above the fold on standard laptop viewports, consistent with the moderate density of the original mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a vertically scrolling, block-level list inside the content column. Items stack in a single column and expand the page height; there is no fixed or virtualized behavior. On narrow viewports, the row layout collapses responsively but maintains the left-first, then right action pattern.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The bookmark-remove-controller manifests visually as removal action affordances embedded in two locations: within the bookmarks toolbar and within each bookmarks-list row. In the toolbar, it appears as a right-aligned text button or subtle outlined button labeled 'Remove all bookmarks', sitting flush with the toolbar’s right padding and aligned vertically to the center of the controls row. Within each bookmark row, it appears as a small text link or icon button on the far right, aligned with the baseline of the bookmark title or its secondary line, separated from the text by 16–24px of horizontal space. These affordances do not create standalone sections; they live inside the toolbar band and list rows, contributing minimally to height but clearly visible due to contrast and placement at the right edge.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Has no independent block-level footprint; all interactive elements controlled by this component are placed inside the bookmarks-toolbar and bookmarks-list rows, following their alignment and scrolling behavior. Actions move with their parent elements as the user scrolls.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The bookmark-empty-state occupies the exact spatial region normally used by the bookmarks-list. It begins in the same position—about 16px below the bottom edge of the toolbar—and spans the full width of the content column. Visually, it is a centered message block: a flat or lightly bordered panel with 16–24px of internal padding, aligning its left edge with the text column. The message headline (e.g., 'You have no bookmarks yet') is left-aligned at the top of this panel, with one or two short explanatory lines beneath it spaced by 8–12px. The block’s vertical size is enough to feel substantive—roughly 25–40% of the visible content area—so that the page does not feel empty; there is additional whitespace above and below the panel to maintain a calm, institutional feel. If an illustrative icon is used, it appears to the left of the text or above it, still contained within the same panel, but scaled small so that text remains the primary focus.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Conditionally replaces the bookmarks-list in the document flow; when active, the list is not rendered. It scrolls naturally with the rest of the page content and maintains the same margins and alignment as the list it replaces.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The bookmark-focus-helper is an invisible behavioral layer that does not create any visible element on the page. Conceptually, it is associated with the bookmarks-list (or empty-state panel) region, ensuring that keyboard focus or screen-reader focus moves to the top of this region after key actions (such as removing all bookmarks). It occupies no extra height or width and does not alter the established spacing between the toolbar and list/empty-state content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: No visual or spatial footprint; it operates on focus and scrolling behavior, anchoring focus to the beginning of the bookmarks content area while the visual layout remains unchanged.

name: School Supply Lists
title: School Supply Lists
description: View grade-specific school supply lists, filter and sort items, and add selected supplies to a personal checklist for that grade.

core_features: Load available grade levels and allow the user to select which grade’s supply list to view, optionally pre-selecting based on the URL parameter gradeLevel.
Retrieve and display the supply list for the selected grade using getSupplyListForGrade, applying optional-only and sort options from URL parameters.
Toggle between viewing all items and only optional items, updating the list via getSupplyListForGrade and keeping the state in the URL parameter optionalOnly.
Sort items by estimated cost using the sortBy URL parameter and getSupplyListForGrade options, allowing users to find more affordable items.
Allow users to add individual supply items (with a chosen quantity) to their personal supply checklist using addSupplyItemToChecklist.
Provide a navigation control to open the My Supply Checklist page (supply_checklist.html) for the currently selected grade, passing the gradeLevel in the URL.
user_workflows:
Workflow: View a supply list for a specific grade
1. User navigates to supply_lists.html, optionally with a gradeLevel URL parameter.
2. The page-state-manager component reads gradeLevel from the URL.
3. The grade-level-selector component calls getSupplyGradeLevels and sets its current selection based on gradeLevel (if present) or a default selection rule.
4. The supply-list-loader component calls getSupplyListForGrade with the selected gradeLevel and options derived from optionalOnly and sortBy URL parameters.
5. The supply-list component renders the returned items, grouped or labeled as appropriate using the provided item fields.

Workflow: Filter list to only optional items
1. User clicks or taps the optional-only toggle control in the supply-list-toolbar.
2. The toolbar updates the optionalOnly value in the URL query string and triggers a page refresh or state update.
3. The page-state-manager derives the new options from the updated URL and calls getSupplyListForGrade again with the same gradeLevel but updated optionalOnly.
4. The supply-list component re-renders, showing only items that match the optional-only filter as applied by the backend.

Workflow: Sort items by estimated cost
1. User changes the sort option control (e.g., a dropdown) in the supply-list-toolbar to a value that sorts by estimated cost.
2. The toolbar updates the sortBy URL parameter and triggers a refresh or state update.
3. The page-state-manager calls getSupplyListForGrade with the same gradeLevel and optionalOnly, but with the new sortBy value in the options argument.
4. The supply-list component renders the items in the order provided by getSupplyListForGrade.

Workflow: Add a supply item to the personal checklist
1. In the supply-list, the user selects an item’s quantity (if applicable) via a quantity input associated with that item.
2. The user activates the add-to-checklist control for that item.
3. The supply-item-actions component calls addSupplyItemToChecklist with the current gradeLevel, the item’s id, and the chosen quantity.
4. On success, the component reads the response (success flag, message, supply_checklist_id, checklist_title, added_item) and updates local UI state (e.g., shows confirmation, disables or updates the add button for that item).
5. The user may repeat this process for additional items.

Workflow: Go to My Supply Checklist for the current grade
1. User clicks the “View My Supply Checklist” navigation control on the page.
2. The checklist-navigation-button component reads the current gradeLevel selection.
3. The component navigates to supply_checklist.html with gradeLevel set to the current gradeLevel in the URL query string.
4. The checklist page can then use that gradeLevel to load the appropriate checklist.
interactions:
Interaction: Grade level selection change
- Trigger: User changes the selected grade in the grade-level-selector.
- Behavior: Update the gradeLevel query parameter in the page URL. Reset or preserve optionalOnly and sortBy according to the design rules (e.g., preserve existing values). Re-run getSupplyListForGrade with the new gradeLevel and current options. Re-render the supply-list component.

Interaction: Optional-only toggle
- Trigger: User toggles a control labeled to indicate showing only optional items vs all items.
- Behavior: Update the optionalOnly query parameter to represent the new boolean state. Call getSupplyListForGrade with current gradeLevel and updated optionalOnly (and existing sortBy). Update the list to reflect the server-filtered items.

Interaction: Sort selection change
- Trigger: User selects a different sort option in the supply-list-toolbar.
- Behavior: Update the sortBy query parameter to the selected sort key. Call getSupplyListForGrade with current gradeLevel, current optionalOnly, and new sortBy. Render items in the order returned.

Interaction: Add item to checklist
- Trigger: User clicks an "add" control or submits a small form associated with a supply item row.
- Behavior: Validate that gradeLevel and the item id are available. Read the quantity input (or a default quantity if none is provided). Call addSupplyItemToChecklist with gradeLevel, supplyItemId, and quantity. On success, show the success message, optionally reflect that the item has been added (e.g., change button label or state) using the returned added_item data. On failure, show an error message and allow retry.

Interaction: View My Supply Checklist navigation
- Trigger: User clicks the “View My Supply Checklist” button/link.
- Behavior: Construct a URL to supply_checklist.html that includes the current gradeLevel query parameter. Navigate to that URL using standard link behavior, so that the checklist page can interpret the gradeLevel parameter.

Interaction: Direct load with URL parameters
- Trigger: User opens supply_lists.html with some combination of gradeLevel, optionalOnly, and sortBy in the query string.
- Behavior: The page-state-manager parses the parameters and initializes the grade-level-selector, supply-list-toolbar, and supply-list components to match those values. The first call to getSupplyListForGrade uses these derived options so that the initial view matches the deep link.
page_functionality.state_logic: On initial load, the page reads gradeLevel, optionalOnly, and sortBy from the URL query string. The gradeLevel value determines which grade is selected and is required when calling getSupplyListForGrade and addSupplyItemToChecklist. If gradeLevel is missing, the page may choose a default grade level based on the list returned by getSupplyGradeLevels (for example, the first available item), but this choice is not hardcoded and is derived at runtime. The optionalOnly parameter is interpreted as a boolean; if it is present and indicates a true value, the page initializes the optional-only toggle to the 'on' state and passes that flag in the options argument to getSupplyListForGrade. The sortBy parameter is treated as an opaque sort key string and is passed unchanged into the options argument for getSupplyListForGrade, and the UI’s sort control is initialized to match that value if it exists. Any user change to grade selection, optional-only, or sort selection updates the corresponding URL parameters so the current view can be bookmarked or shared. No checklist state is stored on this page; checklist data is created and updated exclusively through addSupplyItemToChecklist, and navigation to the checklist page always passes the current gradeLevel via the URL.

components.functionality: Coordinates initial data loading and subsequent updates by reading URL parameters (gradeLevel, optionalOnly, sortBy), invoking getSupplyGradeLevels and getSupplyListForGrade, and propagating current state to child components. It also handles updates to the URL query string when user interactions change core state.

Displays a control that allows the user to select a grade level. On initialization, it receives the list of grade levels from page-state-manager (fetched via getSupplyGradeLevels) and sets the current selection based on the gradeLevel URL parameter or a runtime default. When the user changes the grade, it notifies the page-state-manager to update the URL and reload the supply list.

Provides controls to toggle between viewing all items and only optional items, and to choose a sort order (including by estimated cost). It initializes its control states from the optionalOnly and sortBy URL parameters. On user changes, it updates these parameters via the page-state-manager, which in turn re-calls getSupplyListForGrade.

Encapsulates the logic for calling getSupplyListForGrade whenever the gradeLevel, optionalOnly, or sortBy state changes. It holds the current loading and error states and passes the loaded list data (grade_label and items) down to the supply-list component.

Renders the list of supply items for the selected grade using the data returned by getSupplyListForGrade. For each item, it displays its name, description, category_label, quantity_recommended, estimated_cost, is_required_label, and notes. It also hosts per-item interactions such as setting quantity and adding to the checklist via child components.

Represents a single supply item within the supply-list. It displays all relevant item fields and contains controls for quantity input and adding that specific item to the checklist. It delegates checklist operations to the supply-item-actions component.

Handles interactions for adding a supply item to the user’s checklist. When triggered, it reads the current gradeLevel from the page state, the item’s id, and the chosen quantity, then calls addSupplyItemToChecklist. It processes the response (success flag, message, checklist title, and added_item data) to update UI state (e.g., confirmation messaging or disabling the add button for that item).

Provides a control labeled for viewing the user’s supply checklist. On activation, it reads the current gradeLevel from the page-state-manager and constructs a link to supply_checklist.html with gradeLevel set as a URL parameter, then performs standard navigation to that URL.

Maintains synchronization between internal page state (current gradeLevel, optionalOnly, sortBy) and the URL query string. On relevant state changes, it updates the query parameters without introducing additional data sources. On URL changes (e.g., back/forward navigation), it updates the page state and triggers the appropriate data reloads via page-state-manager.

Displays status and feedback messages related to loading the supply list and adding items to the checklist. It listens for events such as loading start/complete, and addSupplyItemToChecklist success or error, and renders the provided message strings without hardcoding content.

layout.grid_system: Centered fixed-width content column likely based on a 12-column grid (~1080–1200px wide) within full-bleed horizontal bands.
layout.layout_pattern: The page content sits inside the existing centered main content column beneath the global header, banner, and tab bar. Within that column, layout follows a linear, top-to-bottom flow: breadcrumb and H1 (already established by the site), then a compact row combining the grade-level selector on the left and a checklist navigation button on the right (mirroring the mockup’s H1 + right-aligned badge image). Below that sits a full-width utility band for filtering/sorting, followed by any feedback messages and loader state, and finally the main vertical supply list. Each supply item row is a single horizontal strip with its actions aligned to the right, reinforcing the F-pattern scan from left (item name) to right (actions). No sidebars are introduced; all secondary elements remain inline within this single column.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page content sits inside the existing centered main content column beneath the global header, banner, and tab bar. Within that column, layout follows a linear, top-to-bottom flow: breadcrumb and H1 (already established by the site), then a compact row combining the grade-level selector on the left and a checklist navigation button on the right (mirroring the mockup’s H1 + right-aligned badge image). Below that sits a full-width utility band for filtering/sorting, followed by any feedback messages and loader state, and finally the main vertical supply list. Each supply item row is a single horizontal strip with its actions aligned to the right, reinforcing the F-pattern scan from left (item name) to right (actions). No sidebars are introduced; all secondary elements remain inline within this single column.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The global header stack (utility bar, main nav, banner, tabs) still occupy roughly the top 40% of the initial viewport; the School Supply Lists content begins immediately below, filling the remaining 60%. Inside the content column, interactive controls (grade selector, toolbar, feedback) occupy the upper 25–30% of the content area above the fold, while the visible portion of the supply list occupies the rest. Each horizontal band (grade selector row, toolbar row, feedback bar, list block) uses about 24–32px vertical separation, while internal padding within components uses 8–16px, matching the 8px system. Text and list items use around 70–80% of the fixed-width content column, leaving comfortable side margins.
layout_strategies.content_density: Density is moderate and practical. Controls and list rows are compact enough to keep multiple items visible without excessive scrolling, but still have sufficient padding (8–12px vertically) for clarity and tap/click comfort. The toolbar and grade selector row are relatively dense horizontal strips, similar to the navigation bars in the mockup, while the list area is slightly more open with consistent line spacing and clean separators. There is no overly generous whitespace: spacing is functional, consistent with a conservative, institutional site.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Visual flow follows an F-pattern. After users pass through the existing site header, their eye lands on the page H1 on the left, then moves right to the checklist navigation button within the same horizontal band as the grade selector. From there, attention moves down to the filter/sort toolbar, scanning left to right across available controls. Feedback messages appear directly beneath this band, ensuring they are noticed before the list. The user then scans down the list, reading each item’s name and details from left to mid, then glancing right to the actions area (add to checklist), repeating this left-to-right pass for each row. This creates a stable, predictable scan path anchored on left-aligned labels and right-aligned secondary controls.

layout.overall_description: Within the familiar, top-heavy school website shell, the School Supply Lists page content appears as a single, centered column beneath the tab bar. At the top of this column, the breadcrumb and a large left-aligned page title establish context, with a compact horizontal band directly below that combines the grade-level selector on the left and a prominent checklist navigation button aligned to the right, echoing the mockup’s pattern of primary text plus a small right-aligned element. A conservative, flat filter-and-sort toolbar spans most of the content width below, acting as a subtle utility strip. Any feedback messages, such as success or error notices, appear as slim, full-width bars just under this toolbar so they are impossible to miss yet do not dominate. The main body is a single vertical supply list, each item occupying a full-width row with its name and description aligned left and its add-to-checklist controls grouped on the right. Vertical spacing between these horizontal bands is consistent and based on 8px increments, producing a clear, layered hierarchy while keeping the page feeling efficient and information-focused. Non-visual controllers like state management and URL syncing operate behind the scenes, leaving a straightforward, text-first layout that feels stable, official, and easy to scan.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component functions as an invisible wrapper around the entire School Supply Lists content area within the main content column. Conceptually, it spans the full width of the column (approximately 1080–1200px centered on the page), from just below the page title and breadcrumb down to the bottom of the supply list. It does not render any visible UI; instead, it governs the state and orchestrates visibility and interactions for the grade selector, toolbar, loader, list, actions, feedback messages, and checklist navigation button. Visually, its presence is only implied by coordinated updates and consistent behavior across the components contained in this vertical stack.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual logical container; occupies the full main content column but does not affect spacing beyond grouping its child components.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The grade-level selector sits near the top of the page’s content column, directly under the page H1 (and any brief introductory text if present). It forms the left side of a shared horizontal band that is about 70–80% of the content column’s width and centered. Within this band, the selector is left-aligned, starting at the same left edge as the H1 and other body text. The control itself (e.g., label plus dropdown, or a segmented grade picker) is compact, with approximately 12px vertical padding and 16px horizontal padding around tap targets, aligning with the mockup’s bar-like elements. The band has about 16–24px of vertical margin above it (separating it from the H1) and about 16–24px below (separating it from the toolbar), creating a clear but tight relationship. On larger screens, the grade selector occupies roughly two-thirds of this horizontal band, leaving room on the right for the checklist navigation button. On smaller widths, the selector can remain at the top of this band and the button may wrap beneath while staying right-aligned, preserving the hierarchy.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element within the flow; remains anchored under the H1 and scrolls with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The supply list toolbar sits immediately below the grade-level selector band, separated by approximately 16–24px of vertical space. It appears as a flat, full-width utility strip contained within the main content column, spanning about 80% of the column width and centered. The toolbar height is modest, around 40–56px, with 8–12px of vertical padding and 16–24px of horizontal padding inside, reflecting the compact bar treatments of the mockup. Filter controls are left-aligned, following the F-pattern, while sort options and any secondary controls (like view toggles) are aligned toward the right edge of the toolbar. The background may use a very light neutral tone to differentiate it from the white content area while keeping a conservative, institutional appearance. The toolbar creates a strong horizontal stop before the list, helping visually separate controls from content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static horizontal band; scrolls with content and anchors the controls at the top of the list without being sticky.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The loader occupies the same spatial footprint as the supply list, appearing directly under the toolbar (or under any feedback message if present). When active, it centers its content both horizontally and vertically within the available list area visible in the viewport. This means that, in an initial load, the loader appears roughly in the middle of the screen below the toolbar, spanning the width of the text area (about 70–80% of the column) with a simple, conservative indicator (e.g., spinner and short text) aligned center. There is 16–24px of top margin between the toolbar (or feedback bar) and the loader’s first visible element, and similar spacing below, so it feels like a contained block. When loading is complete, the loader collapses entirely, freeing the full vertical stack for the list content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Temporarily occupies the list area; shows and hides dynamically without changing the overall structural layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The supply list is a vertical stack of item rows that begins directly below the toolbar or feedback messages, with about 16–24px of vertical gap separating them. It spans the full width of the main content text area (roughly 70–80% of the centered content column), aligning its left edge exactly with the grade selector and H1 text alignment. The list extends downward as needed, forming the primary scrollable body of the page. Each row is separated from the next by a subtle divider or 8–12px of vertical spacing, yielding a moderately dense, easy-to-scan column. There is no sidebar or secondary column; all item details and actions stay within this single column, reinforcing the linear top-to-bottom reading pattern.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard scrollable content; its height grows with the number of items, pushing the page length downward.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each supply item row is a full-width horizontal strip within the list, stretching across the entire text area from left to right. The row has 8–12px of vertical padding and 12–16px of horizontal padding on both sides, maintaining a compact yet comfortable density. The left side (taking about 60–70% of the row’s width) contains the item name in a slightly emphasized style, with any short descriptive text or notes directly beneath it in a regular weight, aligned flush left. The right side (about 30–40% of the width) is reserved for the supply-item-actions block, aligned to the right edge of the row. A light bottom border or 8px of vertical spacing separates each row, giving a structured, table-like feel without heavy borders. Rows are aligned in a strict vertical column, so the eye can move down the left-hand labels and over to the right-hand actions in a repeated F-pattern rhythm.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static list items; they maintain fixed alignment within the list and do not float or overlap, resizing vertically if content wraps.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The supply item actions occupy the right-hand portion of each row. This area is right-aligned within the row’s horizontal space and grouped in a compact inline arrangement. The primary control (e.g., an "Add" button or checkbox) appears closest to the right edge, with any supporting inputs such as a quantity field or stepper directly to its left, separated by 8–12px. Vertical alignment centers this action cluster relative to the item name block on the left, so the row feels balanced. The action area occupies approximately one-third of the row’s width on larger screens, shrinking proportionally on smaller widths while preserving its right alignment. Padding around these controls follows the 8–16px horizontal and 8–12px vertical standard, providing appropriate click/tap targets without visual heaviness. This layout ensures users scan left to understand the item, then right to act, without visual clutter.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline within each row; remains anchored to the right side of the row and scrolls along with the list.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The checklist navigation button is positioned in the same horizontal band as the grade-level selector, but aligned to the far right of the main content column. Visually, it mirrors the mockup’s small right-aligned school image next to the H1: it is a compact but noticeable element that balances the left-heavy text. In this shared band, the grade selector occupies the left two-thirds, while the checklist button occupies the right third, with at least 16–24px of horizontal separation between them. The button itself is a medium-sized, flat-styled button with 12–16px vertical padding and 16–24px horizontal padding, ensuring it feels like a clear call-to-action without overshadowing the page title. It aligns vertically with the grade selector’s controls to form a neat, single row. On narrower screens, the button can wrap below the grade selector while still being right-aligned within the column, maintaining its visual prominence at the top of the content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static call-to-action within the flow; remains near the top of the page content and scrolls out of view as users move down the list.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component operates invisibly in the background, conceptually attached to the main content column rather than a specific visual region. It tracks interactions such as the selected grade, applied filters, and sort options, and syncs them with the URL parameters. There is no visual footprint and it does not change spacing or alignment; its presence is inferred only by the fact that page state persists and can be shared via URL.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual behavioral layer; does not occupy physical space or alter layout, only manages URL-state synchronization.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Feedback messages appear as a slim, full-width horizontal strip directly below the supply list toolbar and above the supply list (or loader, if active). They span the same width as the content column (about 70–80% of the page width, centered) and align with the left edge of the main text. Each message uses a flat, lightly tinted background color and 8–12px of vertical padding, with 12–16px of horizontal padding, creating a compact but clearly visible bar—similar in feel to a narrow banner. Multiple messages, if allowed, stack vertically with 8px spacing between them. There is approximately 8–16px of vertical space separating the toolbar and the first feedback message, and similar spacing between the last message and the top of the list area. This placement ensures feedback is encountered immediately after using controls, without disrupting the overall linear flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline and dismissible/ephemeral; appears and disappears between the toolbar and list while preserving the surrounding vertical spacing pattern.

name: My Supply Checklist
title: My Supply Checklist
description: View and manage your saved school supply checklist items by grade, mark items as purchased, remove items, and jump back to the School Supply Lists to add more.

core_features: Load available grade levels using getSupplyGradeLevels and show a grade filter tied to the URL gradeLevel parameter.
Load the user’s supply checklists and items using getMySupplyChecklist, optionally filtered by the URL gradeLevel parameter.
Display checklist items grouped under their checklist (typically by grade), including item name, estimated cost, required/optional status, quantity (if provided), and added date (if needed).
Allow users to mark checklist items as purchased or not purchased by calling setSupplyChecklistItemPurchased and updating the displayed state.
Allow users to remove items from their checklist by calling removeSupplyChecklistItem and updating the displayed list.
Show summary information (e.g., number of items, purchased vs. not purchased, total estimated cost) based on the data returned by getMySupplyChecklist.
Provide navigation links back to supply_lists.html to add more items, passing along an appropriate gradeLevel in the URL when the user is focused on a specific grade.
user_workflows:
Workflow: View my supply checklist
1. User navigates to supply_checklist.html (with or without a gradeLevel URL parameter).
2. On page load, the page reads the gradeLevel parameter from the URL (if present).
3. The page calls getSupplyGradeLevels to populate the grade filter options.
4. The page calls getMySupplyChecklist, passing the gradeLevel parameter if it exists; otherwise, it calls getMySupplyChecklist with no gradeLevel parameter to get all checklists.
5. The checklist data is displayed grouped by checklist (for each grade represented in the response). If there are no items, an empty-state message is shown with a link to supply_lists.html.
6. The user can review item details, including name, estimated cost, required/optional status, and quantity (if available).

Workflow: Filter my checklist by grade
1. User sees a grade selector populated with options from getSupplyGradeLevels and the current selection derived from the URL gradeLevel parameter (if present).
2. User changes the selected grade in the grade filter.
3. The page updates the URL to include or change the gradeLevel parameter to the newly selected value, without adding any other static parameters.
4. After the URL is updated, the page calls getMySupplyChecklist with the new gradeLevel value.
5. The displayed checklists and items refresh to show only items for that grade. Summary and empty-state logic update accordingly.
6. User can copy or share the URL; another user opening that URL will see the same grade-focused checklist because the gradeLevel parameter is preserved.

Workflow: Mark an item as purchased or not purchased
1. Within a checklist section, each item row shows a control (such as a checkbox or toggle) representing its is_purchased status.
2. User interacts with this control for a specific item (checklist_item_id).
3. The page calls setSupplyChecklistItemPurchased with the selected checklistItemId and the new isPurchased value.
4. While waiting for the response, the item can either be optimistically updated (toggling its purchased state in the in-memory checklist data) or show a loading indicator.
5. On success (success = true in the response), the item’s is_purchased state is confirmed in the UI; summary counts (e.g., purchased vs. not purchased) are recalculated based on the updated data.
6. On failure (success = false), the item’s purchased state reverts to its previous value (if changed optimistically) and an error message is shown via the notification component, using the message from the API response.

Workflow: Remove an item from my checklist
1. For every checklist item, the row displays a remove action (such as a button) linked to that item’s checklist_item_id.
2. User clicks the remove action for a specific item.
3. The page may show a confirmation prompt (purely client-side) to avoid accidental removals.
4. After confirmation, the page calls removeSupplyChecklistItem with the corresponding checklistItemId.
5. On success (success = true), the item is removed from the in-memory checklist data for that checklist, and the UI re-renders without the item. If this was the last item in a checklist, the entire checklist section can be hidden.
6. Summary metrics and any total cost calculations are updated. If there are no remaining items across all checklists, the empty-state component is displayed.
7. On failure (success = false), the item remains visible and an error message is displayed using the message from the API response.

Workflow: Add more items from a specific grade
1. User is viewing the checklist for a particular grade (either because the URL has a gradeLevel parameter or because multiple grade sections are visible).
2. In each checklist section, there is an "Add more items" control associated with that checklist’s grade_level.
3. User clicks the "Add more items" control in the desired grade section.
4. The page navigates to supply_lists.html, appending a gradeLevel parameter derived from the associated checklist’s grade_level (or current focused gradeLevel) to the URL.
5. On supply_lists.html, the gradeLevel parameter is used to show the corresponding supply list for that grade, so the user can add more items.
6. After adding items on supply_lists.html, the user can navigate back to My Supply Checklist (e.g., via the header link), and the newly added items appear when getMySupplyChecklist is called again.

Workflow: Handle an empty or partially empty checklist
1. The page calls getMySupplyChecklist based on the current gradeLevel parameter (if any).
2. If the response contains no checklists or all checklists have zero items, the empty-state component renders a message explaining that there are no saved items yet.
3. The empty-state component displays a navigation control that links to supply_lists.html. If a gradeLevel parameter is present in the current URL, that same value is passed along in the outgoing link; otherwise, supply_lists.html is opened without a gradeLevel parameter.
4. If some grades have items and others do not, only non-empty checklist sections are shown; the empty-state component may be hidden, since there is at least one visible item.
interactions:
Interaction: Page load and API initialization
- Trigger: Browser loads supply_checklist.html (or URL changes via navigation).
- Behavior: Read the gradeLevel parameter from the URL. Call getSupplyGradeLevels with no parameters. Then call getMySupplyChecklist, passing the gradeLevel parameter only if it exists. Store the returned data in page state for use by all components.

Interaction: Grade filter change
- Trigger: User selects a different grade from the grade-level filter component.
- Behavior: Update the URL’s gradeLevel parameter to the selected grade value. After the URL is updated, call getMySupplyChecklist with the new gradeLevel value. Replace the existing checklist state with the new response and re-render checklist sections and summary.

Interaction: Toggle item purchased state
- Trigger: User clicks an item’s purchased checkbox/toggle.
- Behavior: Determine the current is_purchased value in the UI and invert it to compute the new isPurchased value. Call setSupplyChecklistItemPurchased(checklistItemId, isPurchased). On success, update that item’s is_purchased field in the checklist state. On failure, revert the UI control and show an error notification using the message from the API.

Interaction: Remove checklist item
- Trigger: User clicks the remove button/icon for a checklist item and confirms the action (if a confirmation step is implemented).
- Behavior: Call removeSupplyChecklistItem(checklistItemId). On success, remove the item from the local checklist data structure and re-render the section. If no items remain in a checklist, hide that checklist section. If no items remain overall, show the empty-state component. On failure, keep the item and show an error notification.

Interaction: Navigate to School Supply Lists to add more items
- Trigger: User clicks an "Add more items" control in a checklist section.
- Behavior: Read that section’s associated grade_level (or the current URL gradeLevel, if the view is filtered). Navigate to supply_lists.html with a gradeLevel parameter equal to that grade value (e.g., supply_lists.html?gradeLevel=[selectedGrade]). No other data is persisted; supply_lists.html will reload its own data based on this parameter.

Interaction: Navigate to My Supply Checklist from other pages
- Trigger: User clicks the "My Supply Checklist" link in the site header from any other page (e.g., supply_lists.html or index.html).
- Behavior: Browser navigates to supply_checklist.html. If the navigation link includes a gradeLevel parameter, the page reads it and performs a focused getMySupplyChecklist call; if not, it fetches all checklists. The previous page’s state is not stored; only the URL parameter determines the focused grade.

Interaction: Dismiss notification messages
- Trigger: User clicks a close/dismiss control on an error or success banner.
- Behavior: Clear the corresponding notification state so that the banner is no longer rendered. This does not affect checklist data; it only hides the message.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The primary external state for this page is the URL query parameter gradeLevel. When present, this parameter determines which grade’s checklist items are requested and displayed:
- On every page load (and on relevant URL changes), the page reads the gradeLevel parameter.
- If gradeLevel is present, getMySupplyChecklist is called with gradeLevel set to that value; only the corresponding checklists/items are returned and rendered.
- If gradeLevel is not present, getMySupplyChecklist is called without parameters; all checklists associated with the user are returned and rendered.

Internal page state holds the current lists from getMySupplyChecklist, the available grade options from getSupplyGradeLevels, and transient UI status such as loading flags and notification messages. This internal state is not persisted in localStorage or any other browser storage; it is only in memory for the current page session.

When a user changes the grade filter:
- The grade-level filter component updates the URL gradeLevel parameter to the chosen grade value (or removes it if the user selects an option meaning "all grades").
- After the URL change, the controller triggers a fresh call to getMySupplyChecklist using the new gradeLevel state, and the view updates accordingly.

When setSupplyChecklistItemPurchased or removeSupplyChecklistItem are called, the page updates its in-memory checklist state based on the API responses. If desired, after these operations the page may also choose to re-call getMySupplyChecklist using the current gradeLevel to ensure full synchronization. All navigation to supply_lists.html uses URL parameters only; no checklist state is stored or read via localStorage or similar mechanisms.

components.functionality: Coordinates initial data loading and reacts to URL parameter changes. On page load, it reads the gradeLevel parameter from the URL, calls getSupplyGradeLevels to obtain grade options, and calls getMySupplyChecklist with or without the gradeLevel parameter. It stores the resulting checklists and grade options in in-memory state and passes them to child components. It also listens for URL changes triggered by the grade-level filter and re-invokes getMySupplyChecklist as needed.

Displays a control (such as a dropdown or tabs) listing all available grade levels from getSupplyGradeLevels, plus an option to view all grades if required by the design. It reads the current selection from URL.gradeLevel and keeps the UI in sync with that parameter. When the user selects a grade, it updates the URL’s gradeLevel parameter and notifies the controller to reload checklist data for that grade.

Shows high-level summary metrics based on the current checklist data: total number of checklist items returned by getMySupplyChecklist, number of purchased vs. not purchased items, and total estimated cost derived from estimated_cost_snapshot fields. It recalculates these values whenever the checklist data changes (e.g., after toggling purchased status, removing items, or refetching for a different gradeLevel).

Iterates over the list of checklists returned by getMySupplyChecklist and renders one checklist-grade-section component for each checklist object. It passes down checklist metadata (such as supply_checklist_id, grade_level, grade_label, title, created_at) and its items array. It also coordinates updates: when a child section reports an item has been toggled or removed, this container updates its copy of the checklist data or triggers a refresh from getMySupplyChecklist via the controller.

Represents a single checklist (typically for one grade) and lists all of its items. It displays the checklist’s title and grade_label, and renders a checklist-item-row for each item in the items array. It provides an "Add more items" control that constructs a navigation URL to supply_lists.html with gradeLevel set to the checklist’s grade_level. It listens for item-level events (purchased toggles and removals) and passes them up to the container and controller.

Represents a single SupplyChecklistItem line in the UI. It displays the name_snapshot, estimated_cost_snapshot, is_required_snapshot (e.g., as required/optional text), quantity (if present), and current is_purchased state. It provides controls to toggle the purchased state and to remove the item. When the user toggles purchased, it calls setSupplyChecklistItemPurchased with checklist_item_id and the new isPurchased value (usually via the controller). When the user removes the item, it calls removeSupplyChecklistItem with checklist_item_id.

Provides a functional link or button that sends the user to supply_lists.html to add more supply items. It builds the outgoing URL by reading the associated checklist’s grade_level or, if used independently, the current URL.gradeLevel value. The resulting URL uses the pattern supply_lists.html?gradeLevel=[focusedGradeLevel], where [focusedGradeLevel] is the relevant grade level value from the checklist or URL.

Displays when getMySupplyChecklist returns no checklists, or when all returned checklists have zero items. It informs the user that there are no saved supply items yet and offers a navigation control to visit supply_lists.html. If URL.gradeLevel is present, it appends that gradeLevel value to the outgoing link; otherwise, it links to supply_lists.html without additional parameters.

Displays success or error messages resulting from setSupplyChecklistItemPurchased and removeSupplyChecklistItem calls. For example, if an API response includes success = false, it shows the message text returned by the API. It can also show a brief confirmation when actions succeed, depending on design choices. The banner can be dismissed by the user, which clears the notification from the page state without affecting the underlying checklist data.

layout.grid_system: Centered fixed-width content column likely based on a 12-column grid (~1080–1200px wide) within full-bleed horizontal bands.
layout.layout_pattern: Below the global site chrome (utility bar, main nav, banner, tabs), the page uses a single centered content column on a 12-column grid (~1100–1200px wide) with a classic top-to-bottom flow. The sequence inside the content column is: breadcrumb and page framing (inherited), then a large left-aligned H1 'My Supply Checklist' with the grade-level filter aligned to the right on the same horizontal row, followed by an optional notification banner, then a full-width checklist summary bar, and finally the main checklist list container with grade sections and item rows stacked vertically. The add-more-items link appears at the bottom of the checklist area, left-aligned. When there are no items, the empty-state panel replaces the checklist sections within the same content column position.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Below the global site chrome (utility bar, main nav, banner, tabs), the page uses a single centered content column on a 12-column grid (~1100–1200px wide) with a classic top-to-bottom flow. The sequence inside the content column is: breadcrumb and page framing (inherited), then a large left-aligned H1 'My Supply Checklist' with the grade-level filter aligned to the right on the same horizontal row, followed by an optional notification banner, then a full-width checklist summary bar, and finally the main checklist list container with grade sections and item rows stacked vertically. The add-more-items link appears at the bottom of the checklist area, left-aligned. When there are no items, the empty-state panel replaces the checklist sections within the same content column position.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Consistent with the mockup, the global header region above this page consumes roughly the top 40% of the initial viewport. Within the remaining 60% devoted to this page’s content, the H1 row (title + filter) occupies a relatively shallow band at the top of the content column, the notification banner (when present) takes a single bar-height line directly below, the summary bar occupies a slightly taller card-like band (about 15–20% of the visible content area), and the checklist container consumes the majority (around 60–70%) of the page’s content height with scrollable sections. All elements sit inside the same centered content column, generally spanning 70–90% of that width, with 24–32px vertical spacing between major sections and 8–16px between related elements such as rows and labels.
layout_strategies.content_density: The page maintains moderate density: text lines target a readable width and comfortable line spacing, but blocks are kept relatively compact to minimize scrolling. Vertical padding within components follows an 8px base (8–12px inside rows, 16–24px inside panels), keeping the layout practical and information-focused. The summary bar and notification banner are visually compact bars rather than large, spacious hero sections. Checklist item rows are tightly but cleanly stacked with 8–12px vertical separation, reflecting a functional, list-focused experience rather than a minimalist editorial layout.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Visual flow follows an F-pattern aligned with the rest of the site. The eye first lands on the breadcrumb and then the left-aligned H1, then scans right to notice the grade-level filter at the top-right of the content column. Attention then drops vertically to the notification banner (if present) and then to the checklist summary bar, which serves as a mid-page anchor for understanding progress. From there, the eye moves down into the checklist list container, reading each grade section heading from left to right and then scanning the associated item rows in a vertical list. The add-more-items link at the bottom-left of the list provides a natural end-of-flow action, allowing users to jump back to add more supplies.

layout.overall_description: Within the broader institutional site frame, the 'My Supply Checklist' page appears as a single, centered content column beneath the header, tabs, and banner. At the top of this column, a large, left-aligned H1 clearly states the page title, while a compact grade-level filter control is aligned to the right on the same horizontal line, echoing the mockup’s pattern of a prominent left title with a smaller, right-aligned element. Directly below, an optional notification banner stretches across the full width of the content column as a flat, colored bar, delivering success or error messages without overwhelming the title. A few pixels down, a subtle but solid checklist summary bar spans most of the column width, presenting key stats like total items and purchased counts in a slightly tinted panel. Beneath this, the main checklist container fills the remainder of the visible area and extends downward as the user scrolls, organizing items by grade in stacked sections. Each grade section begins with a bold, left-aligned subheading and a compact summary line, followed by a vertically ordered list of item rows that align neatly to the grid and use flat, bordered rows for checkboxes, labels, and actions. The space between sections is consistent and moderate, keeping the list readable yet dense enough for efficient review. At the bottom of the checklist area, a left-aligned textual 'Add more items' link provides an exit back to the supply lists, mirroring the conservative, utility-first nature of the design. When the checklist is empty, a centered empty-state panel sits in the same position as the list, visually framed within the content column, offering clear explanatory text and a prominent link to add items, maintaining the same institutional, blue-accented aesthetic and measured spacing as the rest of the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This controller component does not present any visible UI. Conceptually, it sits at the very top of the page’s content column, immediately below the site’s global header stack (utility bar, main navigation, banner, and tabs), initializing state for the grade-level filter, checklist summary metrics, and checklist items before any visible components are rendered. It does not occupy visual space and does not affect the perceived layout; all spacing and alignment are driven by the visible components that follow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual logic layer only; no rendered DOM footprint beyond any necessary script hooks, and does not participate in layout flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The grade-level filter is positioned in the top-right of the main content column, horizontally aligned with the 'My Supply Checklist' H1 on the left. It occupies roughly 20–25% of the content column width, anchored flush to the right margin of the column. Vertically, the control sits with about 8–12px of padding above and below its input area, matching the button/tab padding from the mockup. The filter appears as a single-line dropdown or segmented selector with a label (e.g., 'Grade') above or to the left, maintaining a flat, bordered style consistent with the conservative visual language. There is roughly 24–32px of vertical space below this entire H1 row (title plus filter) before the next section (notification banner or summary bar), ensuring a clear separation between the page header region and the checklist content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, right-aligned block within the H1 row; participates in normal document flow and reflows under the title on narrow screens while maintaining top-of-content presence.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The checklist summary bar appears directly beneath the notification banner when present, or immediately under the H1 row when there is no notification. It spans nearly the full width of the content column (about 90–100% of the column’s inner width), centered within the fixed-width layout and aligned with the left edge of the H1. Vertically, it functions as a compact panel about 64–96px tall, with 16–24px of vertical padding inside. Its background is a light neutral or subtle blue tint, creating a low-contrast but distinct bar that visually groups key summary information such as total items, purchased items, and remaining items. Internally, content is arranged left-to-right in 2–3 segments: for example, a bold numeric count on the left, and secondary details or explanatory text aligned to the center or right within the same row. The bar has approximately 24px of space below it before the main checklist list container begins, reinforcing its role as a framing summary before the detailed list.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block-level section sitting in the normal scroll flow; not sticky, but consistently positioned near the top of the content column to act as an anchor summary.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The checklist list container sits immediately below the summary bar, separated by about 24px of vertical whitespace. It spans the full content column width minus standard side padding (approximately 80–90% of the page width, centered), aligning visually with the left edge of the H1 and summary bar. This container stretches downward and forms the main scrollable portion of the page, occupying the bulk (60–70%) of the vertical space under the fold. Within this container, multiple grade sections are stacked vertically, each consuming the full available width. The top of the container starts with a subtle, flat top edge (no heavy card framing), giving the feeling of a continuous list that is sectioned by headings and light dividers. Vertical spacing between grade sections is 24–32px, while spacing between item rows within a section is 8–12px, maintaining a clean but efficient list density. At the very bottom of this container, aligned to the left and separated by 24px from the last checklist section or empty-state panel, sits the add-more-items link.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Acts as the main vertical content region, flowing normally with the page scroll. It does not introduce separate scrollbars; it expands naturally based on the number of grade sections and items.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each checklist-grade-section appears as a stacked block within the checklist list container, occupying 100% of that container’s width. At the top of the section, a grade heading (e.g., 'Grade 3') is left-aligned, set in a typographic style slightly smaller than the main H1 but larger and bolder than body text, following the hierarchy seen in the mockup’s content headings. This heading sits with 8–12px of vertical padding above and below and approximately 24px of spacing from the previous section. Directly beneath the heading, an optional single-line summary (e.g., '12 items saved • 5 purchased') is left-aligned and sits in a subdued text style, separated by about 4–8px from the heading. Under this header area, the section lists its checklist-item-rows in a vertical stack, each row stretching to nearly the full width of the section. The right edge of the section aligns with the right edge of the content column, maintaining a clean columnar feel. A light horizontal rule or subtle background alternation may separate sections, but overall the styling remains flat and understated to match the institutional design.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block element nested inside the checklist-list-container; expands vertically as more item rows are included and pushes subsequent sections downward in the normal document flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each checklist-item-row is a horizontal strip within its grade section, occupying approximately 100% of the section’s width with 8–16px of internal horizontal padding on each side. Vertically, rows are about 40–56px tall depending on content, with 8–12px vertical padding to maintain comfortable tap/click targets. On the left side of the row, aligned to the section’s left edge, sits the main interaction control: a checkbox or toggle to mark the item as purchased, followed immediately by the item name and any short descriptor in a single line of text. This left cluster takes about 60–70% of the row’s width. On the right side, aligned flush to the section’s right edge, are secondary details such as quantity, optional notes, and a discrete remove link or icon, stacked or aligned horizontally based on available width, collectively taking up roughly 30–40% of the row. Checked/purchased items may display with a lighter text color or subtle strikethrough, but the overall style remains flat with minimal decoration, possibly separated from adjacent rows by a 1px bottom border or 8px of vertical gap. Rows are spaced closely (approximately 4–8px margin between them) to maintain moderate density within the section.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline-block or flex row within the grade section that stretches full width and participates in normal vertical stacking; no fixed positioning. On narrower screens, right-aligned metadata can wrap to a second line beneath the item name while maintaining left alignment and overall structural clarity.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The add-more-items-link is positioned at the bottom-left of the checklist-list-container, following the final checklist-grade-section or the empty-state panel when the list is empty. It is a single-line text link (optionally with a small left icon) styled in the brand blue and aligned with the left edge of the content column, ensuring continuity with the F-pattern flow. There is approximately 24–32px of vertical space separating it from the last visible list content, giving it breathing room as a distinct call to action without appearing as a separate section. Horizontally, it occupies only the intrinsic width of the text, leaving ample whitespace to its right, which keeps the layout feeling calm and uncluttered. This placement makes it an intuitive next step when the user reaches the end of their checklist.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard inline/block link in the normal flow; it scrolls with the content and does not remain fixed. On narrow viewports, it remains left-aligned under the list content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The empty-state-panel appears only when the user has no saved checklist items. In this state, it sits within the checklist-list-container, occupying the same vertical position where grade sections and item rows would normally appear. It is centered horizontally within the content column, typically spanning about 60–70% of the column’s width, creating a contained card-like area that stands out against the page background while still feeling integrated. Vertically, the panel has generous internal padding (24–32px on all sides) and is separated from the summary bar above by about 32px of whitespace. The content inside is arranged top-to-bottom: a clear, moderately sized heading (e.g., 'No items in your checklist yet') at the top left, followed by one or two short explanatory paragraphs, and finally a prominent text link or button-style link ('Go to School Supply Lists') aligned left within the panel. The panel background uses a light neutral or very pale blue tone with a subtle border to echo the conservative institution style, without heavy illustration or playful graphics. Surrounding vertical whitespace ensures it feels like a focused message, but overall height remains moderate so that it fits comfortably within a typical viewport without excessive scrolling.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Replaces the stack of grade sections inside the checklist-list-container when no items exist. It scrolls naturally with the page and does not remain fixed; its width and centering adjust with the main content column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The notification-banner sits near the top of the page’s content column, directly below the H1 plus grade-level filter row and above the checklist summary bar. It spans the full width of the content column, matching the left and right alignment of the H1, and appears as a flat horizontal strip approximately 40–56px tall depending on message length. The banner uses a solid or lightly tinted background color that corresponds to the message type (e.g., greenish for success, red for error, yellow for warning) while still fitting within the broader blue and neutral palette. Inside, text is left-aligned, with an optional close/dismiss icon aligned to the top-right of the banner. Vertical padding is modest (8–12px), keeping the banner compact and in line with the moderate density of the mockup. There is about 16–24px of spacing above and below the banner, ensuring it does not collide visually with either the title row or the summary bar.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline alert bar that appears and disappears in place within the normal document flow. It is not sticky; as the user scrolls, it moves with the content. When dismissed, it collapses smoothly, allowing the summary bar to shift upward and occupy the space.

name: Summer Camps
title: Summer Camps
description: Search and review summer camp sessions offered by the school, with filters for month, daily schedule, and weekly fee, and navigation to detailed camp pages.

core_features: Load available filter options for months, daily schedules, maximum weekly fee presets, and sort options using getSummerCampFilterOptions.
Initialize the page’s filter and sort state from URL parameters (month, dailySchedule, maxFee, sortBy).
Allow users to select month, daily schedule, and maximum weekly fee filters, and combine them before running a search.
Allow users to choose a sort order (for example, by start date or fee) using sort options from getSummerCampFilterOptions.
Search summer camp sessions via searchSummerCampSessions using the current filters and sort order.
Display search results as a list of camp cards, each showing key information such as session dates, hours, weekly fee, grade range, and a brief summary.
Handle the case where no camp sessions match the filters by showing an empty-state message and offering clear actions to adjust filters.
Allow users to click a camp session card to navigate to camp_detail.html with the selected session’s ID in the URL.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1 – Landing on the page (with or without pre-selected filters):
1. User navigates to summer_camps.html, optionally with URL parameters (month, dailySchedule, maxFee, sortBy).
2. The page calls getSummerCampFilterOptions to retrieve months, daily schedule options, max fee presets, and sort options.
3. The URL parameters are read and matched against the filter and sort options:
   - If a URL value matches an available option, that option is pre-selected.
   - If maxFee is present but does not match any preset, a custom max fee value is set.
   - If sortBy matches one of the sort options, it is selected; otherwise a default sort is used.
4. The page constructs a filters object and sort_by value from this initialized state.
5. The page calls searchSummerCampSessions with {filters, sort_by}.
6. The returned list of camp sessions is rendered as cards in the results list.
7. The user sees the current filters and sort selection reflected in the UI and can start adjusting them as needed.

Workflow 2 – Adjusting filters and viewing updated camp sessions:
1. User changes one or more filter controls in the filter bar (month, daily schedule, maximum weekly fee).
2. The filter bar updates its internal filter state based on the user’s selections.
3. User clicks an "Apply filters" action (e.g., a button) in the filter bar.
4. The filter bar constructs a new query string representing the active filters and keeps or updates the current sortBy value.
5. The URL is updated (using push/replace state) so that month, dailySchedule, maxFee, and sortBy reflect the new selections. Filters that are cleared are removed from the URL.
6. The page calls searchSummerCampSessions with the updated filters and sort_by.
7. The results list updates with the new set of camp session cards, and a results summary component is updated to show how many camps match the filters.
8. The user can repeat these steps to refine the results or clear filters.

Workflow 3 – Changing sort order:
1. User opens the sort control (e.g., a dropdown) near the results list.
2. User selects a new sort option, which corresponds to one of the sort_options values from getSummerCampFilterOptions.
3. The sort control updates its local sort state and triggers a sort change event.
4. The URL’s sortBy parameter is updated to the newly selected sort key while preserving any active filter parameters.
5. The page calls searchSummerCampSessions with the current filters and the new sort_by value.
6. The results list re-renders in the new order without changing the current filters.

Workflow 4 – Clearing filters:
1. User clicks a "Clear filters" action in the filter bar.
2. The filter bar resets its internal filter state to an unfiltered state (e.g., no month, no daily schedule, no max fee) while retaining the current sort order or resetting to a default sort, depending on design.
3. The URL parameters month, dailySchedule, and maxFee are removed from the query string; sortBy is updated if needed.
4. The page calls searchSummerCampSessions with an empty or minimal filters object and the chosen sort_by value.
5. The results list displays all available active camps according to the current sort order.
6. The user can then reapply filters as needed.

Workflow 5 – Opening a detailed camp session page:
1. User reviews the camp session cards in the results list.
2. User clicks anywhere on a camp session card’s primary interactive area (for example, card header or a “View details” action).
3. The click handler retrieves the session’s id from the bound data model.
4. The browser navigates to camp_detail.html?campSessionId=<id>.
5. The detailed camp page (camp_detail.html) can then use the campSessionId to load full information and an interest form (handled on that page, not here).

Workflow 6 – Handling no results:
1. User applies filters that result in zero matching camp sessions.
2. searchSummerCampSessions returns an empty array.
3. The results list component detects there are no items and hides the list of cards.
4. An empty-state message component is shown instead, explaining that no camps match the current filters.
5. The empty-state component provides actions such as a link or button to clear filters or to adjust specific filters (e.g., remove max fee).
6. When the user uses these actions, the filter bar updates its state, the URL parameters are updated, and searchSummerCampSessions is called again to repopulate results.
interactions: On page initialization, the page reads URL parameters (month, dailySchedule, maxFee, sortBy) and uses them to set initial filter and sort control values before executing the first search.
On initialization, the page calls getSummerCampFilterOptions once to populate dropdowns/selectors for month, daily schedule, maximum fee presets, and sort options.

When a user changes the month filter control (e.g., selects a month option), the filter bar updates internal filter state but does not immediately search until the user applies filters (unless configured to auto-apply).
When a user changes the daily schedule filter control, the corresponding option value is stored in the filter state, pending the next search execution.
When a user selects a maximum weekly fee preset, the selected value is stored; if the user enters a custom fee value, that numeric value replaces any preset in the internal filter state.

When a user clicks an "Apply filters" button, the filter bar validates the filter values (e.g., ensures max fee is a valid number if provided), updates the URL parameters to match the selected filters and existing sortBy, and triggers a new call to searchSummerCampSessions.

When a user clicks a "Clear filters" button, the filter bar resets its filter state, removes the corresponding URL parameters, and triggers a new call to searchSummerCampSessions with no filter constraints (or minimal defaults).

When a user changes the sort option in the sort control, the sortBy URL parameter is updated, keeping current filter parameters, and searchSummerCampSessions is called again with the same filters and new sort_by value.

When searchSummerCampSessions resolves, the results list component binds the returned array of camp sessions to a collection of camp session cards; only sessions with is_active set to true are rendered (if the API can return inactive records).
When a user clicks a camp session card, a navigation event is triggered, sending the browser to camp_detail.html with campSessionId set to the selected camp’s id in the URL query string.

When no results are returned, the results list hides cards and the empty-state component shows explanatory text and interactive elements like "Clear filters", which when clicked, call back into the filter bar’s clear logic, update the URL, and trigger a new search.

When the browser back/forward buttons are used and the URL query string changes, the URL-state manager component detects the change, re-initializes the filter and sort state based on the updated parameters, and triggers searchSummerCampSessions so that the UI always reflects the URL.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page uses the URL query parameters month, dailySchedule, maxFee, and sortBy as the single source of truth for filter and sort state. On page load, the URL is parsed: month is matched against the months options from getSummerCampFilterOptions; dailySchedule is matched to daily_schedule_options values; maxFee is parsed as a number and either matched to a max_fee_presets value or treated as a custom value; sortBy is matched to sort_options values, with a safe default if no match is found. These resolved values populate the filter bar and sort control. Whenever the user changes filters and explicitly applies them, or changes sort, the URL query parameters are updated to reflect the new state. Clearing a filter removes its parameter from the URL. The searchSummerCampSessions call always uses the state derived from the URL, ensuring that refreshing the page or using browser navigation recreates the same view. No persistent client storage (such as localStorage) is used; only URL parameters control the state.

components.functionality: Manages synchronization between URL query parameters (month, dailySchedule, maxFee, sortBy) and the in-memory filter/sort state used by other components. On initial load and on browser navigation events, it reads the URL parameters, normalizes them using the options from getSummerCampFilterOptions, and provides the resolved state to the filter bar and sort control. When filters or sort order change via UI interactions, it updates the URL query string accordingly.

Calls getSummerCampFilterOptions when the page initializes to retrieve the available months, daily schedule options, maximum fee presets, and sort options. Exposes these options to the filter bar and sort control components, and notifies the URL-state manager once options are loaded so it can correctly resolve any existing URL parameters to valid options.

Provides interactive controls for filtering camp sessions by month, daily schedule, and maximum weekly fee. It consumes option lists from getSummerCampFilterOptions and initial values from the URL-state manager. Users can select a month option, a daily schedule option, and either a fee preset or a custom numeric max fee. The component aggregates these selections into a filters object and exposes actions to apply or clear filters. Applying filters triggers a URL update and a new search; clearing filters resets state and removes relevant URL parameters.

Displays a sort selector populated with sort_options from getSummerCampFilterOptions. It initializes its selected value using the sortBy URL parameter (resolved by the URL-state manager). When the user selects a different sort option, it updates the URL’s sortBy parameter and triggers a new search via the search executor component, keeping the current filters unchanged.

Coordinates calls to searchSummerCampSessions based on the current URL-derived filters and sort_by value. It listens for events from the URL-state manager, filter bar, and sort control indicating that filters or sort order have been applied or changed. On each trigger, it constructs the search parameters (filters and sort_by) and calls searchSummerCampSessions. Once results are returned, it passes the array of sessions to the results list and results summary components.

Shows a summary of the current search results, such as the total number of matching camp sessions and a short description of active filters (e.g., selected month or max fee), without hardcoding any specific values. It updates whenever camp-search-executor receives new results or filters change, helping families understand what they are viewing.

Renders the list of summer camp session cards returned by searchSummerCampSessions. It receives an array of sessions and iterates over them to create individual camp-session-card instances. It ensures that only sessions that should be shown (e.g., is_active) are rendered. If the array is empty, it hides the card list and triggers the empty-state-message component to show instead.

Represents a single summer camp session in card format within the results list. Each card displays key details pulled from the SummerCampSession data, such as name, session_label, formatted start_date and end_date, daily_start_time and daily_end_time, weekly_fee with currency, location if provided, eligible_grade_labels or eligible_grades, and activities_summary or a short description. The entire card (or a dedicated action area) is clickable and navigates to camp_detail.html with the campSessionId URL parameter set to this session’s id.

Displays an informative message and actions when searchSummerCampSessions returns no results. It explains that no camps match the current filters (without hardcoding any specific filter values) and provides controls like a button to clear all filters or links to relax certain criteria. When these controls are used, it calls back into the filter bar to reset filters, which updates the URL and triggers a new search.

layout.grid_system: Centered fixed-width content column likely based on a 12-column grid (~1080–1200px wide) within full-bleed horizontal bands.
layout.layout_pattern: The page keeps the classic, linear, single-column flow seen in the mockup. Above the fold, the global frame (utility bar, main navigation, banner, tabs, breadcrumb, and H1) remains unchanged and consumes the top ~40% of the viewport. The Summer Camps–specific components all live within the main content column, directly beneath the page title and any brief introductory text. Within that column, the order is: filter bar (with its search executor), then a compact summary/sort row, then the vertical list of camp session cards or an empty state message. All elements are left-aligned within a centered, fixed-width content column and follow a top-to-bottom, clearly segmented structure.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page keeps the classic, linear, single-column flow seen in the mockup. Above the fold, the global frame (utility bar, main navigation, banner, tabs, breadcrumb, and H1) remains unchanged and consumes the top ~40% of the viewport. The Summer Camps–specific components all live within the main content column, directly beneath the page title and any brief introductory text. Within that column, the order is: filter bar (with its search executor), then a compact summary/sort row, then the vertical list of camp session cards or an empty state message. All elements are left-aligned within a centered, fixed-width content column and follow a top-to-bottom, clearly segmented structure.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The fixed-width content column (about 1080–1200px) remains centered, with generous left/right margins on large screens. The header/navigation stack still occupies roughly the top 40% of the viewport. Within the main content area (bottom ~60%), the top slice is reserved for the page-specific header (breadcrumb, H1, intro). Immediately below, the camp filter bar occupies a distinct band about 1/5 to 1/4 of the content area height above the fold. Beneath the filters, a low-profile summary/sort row takes minimal vertical space, followed by the results list, which uses the majority of the remaining height. Each camp card spans most of the content column width (about 90–100% of the column), with vertical spacing between cards in the 16–24px range.
layout_strategies.content_density: Density stays moderate, mirroring the mockup. Bars and tools (filter bar, summary/sort row) are relatively compact, with 8–12px internal padding and 16–24px between bands. The results list is readable but not airy: cards are close enough that 3–5 are visible per screen on a laptop, depending on content length.
